```mermaid
    end

    A3 --> B4
    A1 --> D1
    A2 --> E4
    A4 --> E2
    A5 --> E5
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    C1 --> B2
    C2 --> B2
    C3 --> B2
    C4 --> B3
    C5 --> B3
    B4 --> D1
    B4 --> D2
    B4 --> D3
    B4 --> D4
    D1 --> E1
    B4 --> E2
    D2 --> E3
    B3 --> E4
    D3 --> E5
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flowchart: PMMA Free-Radical Addition Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Initiation: BPO decomposes to 2 BPO radicals] --> A2[Radical attacks C=C of MMA]
    A2 --> A3[Propagation: Chain grows by successive MMA addition]
    A3 --> A4[Chain Transfer: Radical transfers to dead polymer or monomer]
    A4 --> A5[Termination: Coupling or disproportionation of two radicals]
    A5 --> A6[Cross-linking: EGDMA bridges two PMMA chains]
    A6 --> A7[3D Network of PMMA + cross-links - final denture base]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Classification of Denture Base Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DB[DENTURE BASE MATERIALS] --> H[HEAT-CURED POLYMERS]
    DB --> C[COLD-CURED AUTO-POLYMER]
    DB --> L[LIGHT-CURED]
    DB --> TH[THERMOPLASTIC INJECTABLE]
    DB --> HV[HIGH-IMPACT MODIFIED]
    DB --> M[METAL REINFORCED]

    H --> H1[PMMA - ADA 12 Type 1]
    H --> H2[Polycarbonate - seldom used]
    H --> H3[UDMA-based]

    C --> C1[PMMA auto - ADA 12 Type 2]
    C --> C2[Used for repairs and relining]

    L --> L1[Triethylene Glycol Dimethacrylate TEGDMA matrix]
    L --> L2[Used for record bases and trays]

    TH --> TH1[Nylon-based Valplast]
    TH --> TH2[Polycarbonate Lexan]
    TH --> TH3[Acetal Resin - Delrin]
    TH --> TH4[PEEK Poly-Ether-Ether-Ketone - emerging]

    HV --> HV1[Rubber-toughened PMMA]
    HV --> HV2[Glass fiber reinforced PMMA]
    HV --> HV3[Carbon fiber reinforced PMMA]
    HV --> HV4[Aramid Kevlar fiber reinforced]

    M --> M1[Cast metal base Cr-Co alloy]
    M --> M2[Swaged metal base]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Crazing and Craze Formation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Applied Tensile Stress] --> L2[Microvoid Formation at Inclusions]
    L2 --> L3[Polymer Bridging Fibrils Across Voids]
    L3 --> L4[Stress Concentration at Fibril Ends]
    L4 --> L5[Fibril Rupture]
    L5 --> L6[Visible Craze - Whitened Region]
    L6 --> L7[If Unchecked: True Crack Propagation]
    L7 --> L8[Catastrophic Brittle Fracture]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Crazing** is **NOT** a crack — it is a network of microvoids (10⁻⁷ m) bridged by polymer fibrils. Crazes are reversible if stress is removed early; cracks are irreversible. PMMA is unique in showing extensive crazing before fracture, which gives it the **"ductile-like" warning** before failure — unlike ceramics which fail catastrophically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** List **any six ideal requirements** of a denture base material.

**Model Answer (6 points, 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Biocompatibility** – non-toxic and non-irritant to oral mucosa.
2. **High transverse (flexural) strength** – minimum 65 MPa per ADA Spec. 12.
3. **Adequate impact strength** – to resist accidental fracture on dropping.
4. **Dimensional stability** – minimal polymerization shrinkage and water sorption.
5. **Esthetics** – translucent pink shade matching gingival tissues.
6. **Chemical inertness** – resistant to saliva, food acids, and oral cleansers.
*(Optional 7th: low thermal conductivity, color stability, easy to repair)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the **American Dental Association (ADA) Specification No. 12** for denture base polymers, and list the **three types** classified.

**Model Answer:**

ADA Specification No. 12 (1975, revised) classifies denture base polymers into:
- **Type I:** Heat-polymerizable (heat-cured) PMMA — definitive dentures.
- **Type II:** Auto-polymerizable (cold-cured) PMMA — repairs, relines, temporaries.
- **Type III:** Thermoplastic blank or powder — for injection molding (e.g., polycarbonate, nylon).
- **Type IV:** Light-activated (light-cured) UDMA-based resin.
- **Type V:** Microwave-cured PMMA.

Specification defines minimum mechanical properties: **flexural strength ≥ 65 MPa**, **flexural modulus ≥ 2000 MPa**, **water sorption ≤ 0.8 mg/cm²**, and **residual monomer ≤ 0.5 %**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Instructions:** Answer **either (a) OR (b)** from each choice.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **chemistry of polymerization of PMMA denture base resin**, including the role of **initiator, inhibitor, activator, and cross-linking agent** with relevant chemical equations.

**Model Answer:**

PMMA polymerizes via **free-radical addition polymerization** of methyl methacrylate (MMA) monomer.

**Step 1: Initiation (by Benzoyl Peroxide, BPO)**

Upon heating (heat-cured) or in presence of a tertiary amine activator (cold-cured), BPO undergoes homolytic cleavage:

$$
\left( \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO} \right)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta \text{ or amine}} 2 \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^{\bullet}
$$

The benzoate radical then attacks the C=C double bond of MMA, generating a chain-initiating radical:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2 = \text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO–CH}_2\text{–C}^{\bullet}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3
$$

**Step 2: Propagation**

The chain radical adds successive MMA monomers in a head-to-tail manner:

$$
\text{R–CH}_2\text{–C}^{\bullet}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3) + n \, \text{MMA} \rightarrow \text{R–}[\text{CH}_2\text{–C}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)]_n\text{–C}^{\bullet}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)
$$

**Step 3: Termination (coupling or disproportionation)**

Two growing chains terminate either by:
- **Coupling:** $R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow R–R$
- **Disproportionation:** $R\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet} + R'\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow R\text{=CH}_2 + R'\text{–CH}_3$

**Step 4: Role of Additives**

| Additive | Function |
|---|---|
| **BPO (initiator)** | Generates free radicals on decomposition |
| **DMPT (tertiary amine activator)** | Cold-cure systems; reduces BPO decomposition T to room T |
| **Hydroquinone (inhibitor)** | Quenches stray radicals; prevents premature gelation during storage |
| **EGDMA (cross-linker)** | Difunctional monomer; forms 3D network, improves strength, reduces creep and water sorption |

The cross-linking reaction between PMMA chains and EGDMA:

$$
2 \; \text{PMMA–C}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2 = \text{C}(\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC})\text{C} = \text{CH}_2 \rightarrow \text{PMMA–C–C(=)–O–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–O–C(=)–C–PMMA}
$$

**Conclusion:** PMMA formation is a **chain-growth, free-radical, addition polymerization** with a 3D network topology when EGDMA is present.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **compression molding technique** for fabricating a heat-cured PMMA denture base, highlighting the **critical control points** that determine the final properties.

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1: Wax-up & Flasking (3 marks)**
- Wax denture is invested in a dental flask using Type II gypsum (plaster) in the lower half and Type V (dental stone) in the upper half.
- Counter (separating medium — sodium alginate / tinfoil substitute) applied to prevent gypsum–gypsum adhesion.

**Stage 2: Wax Elimination (2 marks)**
- Flask immersed in boiling water for 5 min → wax flows out → mold cavity created.
- Sodium alginate mold-release applied to gypsum.
- Critical: do not overheat the stone → causes surface roughness in the denture.

**Stage 3: P/L Mixing & Packing (1 mark)**
- Powder:liquid ratio = **3:1 by volume** (2.5:1 by mass).
- Mix to **dough stage** (~5–7 min) — stringy, dough-like consistency.
- Pack into mold cavity in a hydraulic press at **2000–3000 psi**.

**Stage 4: Curing Cycle (1 mark)**
- **74 °C for 90 min** → initial polymerization.
- **100 °C (boiling water) for 30 min** → completion.
- **Slow bench cooling** (never quench in cold water) → prevents internal stress and warping.

**Critical Control Points Summary:**
- P/L ratio deviation → porosity or granular surface.
- Inadequate dough stage → granular, weak denture.
- Rapid heating → boiling monomer → porosity (gaseous inclusions).
- Fast cooling → stress cracks and dimensional inaccuracy.
- Inadequate pressure → flash formation; internal voids.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **heat-cured, cold-cured, and light-cured PMMA** with respect to initiator system, residual monomer, biocompatibility, dimensional accuracy, and applications.

**Model Answer (in tabular form):**

| Property | Heat-Cured (HCPMMA) | Cold-Cured (APPMMA) | Light-Cured (LCPMMA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Benzoyl peroxide (thermal) | BPO + tertiary amine (DMPT) | Camphorquinone + amine (photo) |
| **Activation energy** | Heat (74–100 °C) | Room temperature | Visible light (470 nm, ~10 min) |
| **Residual monomer** | 0.2–0.4 % | **0.5–3.0 %** | 0.4–0.8 % |
| **Biocompatibility** | Best | Poorest | Good |
| **Molecular weight** | Highest (5–10 × 10⁵) | Lower (2–5 × 10⁵) | Moderate (3–6 × 10⁵) |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Best (controlled shrinkage) | Worst (high shrinkage) | Good |
| **Porosity** | 0.1–0.3 vol % | 0.5–2 vol % | Very low |
| **Color stability** | Excellent | Yellows on aging | Excellent |
| **Application** | Definitive dentures | Repairs, relines, temporaries | Record bases, occlusal splints |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Lowest | Highest |

**Conclusion:** Heat-cured PMMA remains the gold standard for definitive prostheses due to its low residual monomer and high molecular weight, while cold-cured is reserved for clinical repairs.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **modifications of PMMA** to overcome its **low impact strength**, including fiber reinforcement and high-impact grades.

**Model Answer:**

**Problem:** Conventional PMMA has **impact strength ~0.5 ft-lb/inch** — insufficient to resist accidental drops, leading to **midline fracture** in ~33 % of dentures within 5 years of use.

**Modification 1: Rubber-Toughened (High-Impact) PMMA**

- Butadiene-styrene rubber (BSR) or butadiene-acrylate is incorporated (5–15 wt %) as a dispersed phase.
- Mechanism: rubber particles act as **stress concentrators** that trigger **multiple crazing and shear yielding** → absorbs impact energy.
- Impact strength increases from **0.5 to ~0.8–1.2 ft-lb/inch** (~60–140 % improvement).
- Drawback: slight decrease in transverse strength and modulus; rubber phase slightly discolors over time.

**Modification 2: Fiber Reinforcement**

- **Glass fibers** (E-glass, silanized): 5–10 wt % chopped fibers (3–6 mm length) → flexural strength up to 100 MPa.
- **Carbon/graphite fibers**: highest stiffness improvement; modulus doubles; black color limits use.
- **Aramid (Kevlar) fibers**: high tensile, low density; difficult to polish.
- **Ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) fibers**: best impact resistance but poor bonding to PMMA matrix → requires silane coupling agent.

**Mechanism of Toughening:** Fibers deflect crack propagation through mechanisms of **crack bridging, fiber pull-out, and fiber fracture** — each consumes fracture energy.

**Modification 3: Chemical Cross-linking (EGDMA > 2 %)**

- Increases glass transition temperature from 105 °C to ~115 °C.
- Improves fatigue resistance and reduces creep, but makes the material more brittle and harder to repair.

**Modification 4: Nano-Reinforcements (Emerging)**

- **Nano-SiO₂, nano-ZrO₂, nano-diamond, carbon nanotubes (CNT)** at 0.5–5 wt % → simultaneous improvement in flexural strength, impact strength, and antibacterial activity.
- KTU-relevant emerging topic.

**Conclusion:** A combination of **rubber toughening + fiber reinforcement + nano-additives** can boost impact strength by **200–400 %** without severely compromising esthetics.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Denture Base Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "heat-cured" with "cold-cured" initiator chemistry** — Cold-cured uses BPO + DMPT (amine activator), NOT heat. Mixing up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "polymerization shrinkage = 20 %" without distinguishing theoretical vs practical** — The theoretical volumetric shrinkage is ~20 %, but the **practical linear shrinkage is only 0.3–0.5 %** after hygroscopic compensation. Examiners dock marks for the blanket "20 %" answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of EGDMA** — Cross-linking is the reason PMMA doesn't dissolve in saliva. Many students skip it.
> 4. **Confusing "crazing" with "cracking"** — Crazes are reversible microvoids; cracks are irreversible. This is a favorite 2-mark KTU question.
> 5. **Skipping ADA Spec. No. 12** — The 65 MPa flexural strength minimum is a **mandatory recall** in any denture base property question.
> 6. **Mixing up the P/L ratio direction** — Powder-to-liquid is **3:1 by volume**, not 1:3.
> 7. **Forgetting to specify "slow cooling"** — Quenching the cured flask is a guaranteed **dimensional distortion** failure in compression molding. Examiners value this insight.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **denture base** is the load-bearing pink acrylic plate of a removable prosthesis that sits on the oral mucosa and retains artificial teeth.
- ✅ **PMMA** (since 1937) is the **gold standard** denture base polymer, used in ~95 % of cases.
- ✅ **ADA Spec. No. 12** sets the bench: **flexural strength ≥ 65 MPa**, water sorption ≤ 0.8 mg/cm², residual monomer ≤ 0.5 %.
- ✅ **Composition = Powder (PMMA beads + BPO + pigments) + Liquid (MMA + EGDMA + HQ inhibitor).**
- ✅ **P/L ratio = 3:1 by volume (2.5:1 by mass); dough stage at 5–7 min.**
- ✅ Polymerization is **free-radical addition** via BPO initiator; cold-cure uses DMPT tertiary amine activator.
- ✅ **Heat-cured PMMA** is best in biocompatibility, color stability, and accuracy.
- ✅ **Cold-cured PMMA** has highest residual monomer (0.5–3 %) → not for definitive dentures.
- ✅ **Theoretical volumetric shrinkage = ~20 %**; **practical linear shrinkage = ~0.3–0.5 %** after hygroscopic compensation.
- ✅ **Curing cycle:** 74 °C / 90 min → 100 °C / 30 min → **slow bench cool** (NEVER quench).
- ✅ **Main failure = midline fracture (33 %)** due to low impact strength (~0.5 ft-lb/in).
- ✅ **Toughening routes:** rubber (BSR) blending, fiber reinforcement, nano-additives, cross-linking.
- ✅ **Crazing** (reversible, fibril-bridged microvoids) ≠ **cracking** (irreversible, true fissure).
- ✅ **Thermal expansion mismatch** with porcelain teeth: $\alpha_{PMMA} \approx 81 \times 10^{-6}$/°C vs $\alpha_{tooth} \approx 11 \times 10^{-6}$/°C — major cause of tooth debonding.
- ✅ **Emerging materials:** UDMA, PEEK, nylon, polycarbonate, acetal — for thermoplastic, flexible, or implant-supported prostheses.
- ✅ **Conversion efficiency** is the master biocompatibility lever: higher conversion = less residual MMA = less mucosal irritation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crown and Bridge Resins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crown and Bridge Resins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crown and Bridge Resins** are a specialized class of **dental polymeric materials** used in **prosthodontics** to fabricate **artificial crowns** (prosthetic caps restoring damaged teeth) and **dental bridges** (fixed partial dentures replacing one or more missing teeth by anchoring to adjacent abutment teeth).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PBPOT304 — Biomedical & Biopolymers, Module 3), these resins are classified under **biocompatible, restorative biomaterials** that combine the **aesthetic advantages of polymers** with the **mechanical robustness required for load-bearing intraoral applications**.

The materials principally employed are:

- **Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA)** — the traditional denture-base polymer, also used in temporary crowns and bridges.
- **Bis-GMA-based composites** — reinforced resin systems used in indirect restorations.
- **UDMA (Urethane Dimethacrylate)** and **TEGDMA (Triethylene Glycol Dimethacrylate)** matrices — modern hybrid resin systems.
- **CAD/CAM polymethyl methacrylate blanks (PMMA discs/blocks)** — for digitally milled provisional restorations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Custom Tailored Tooth Suit":**
> Think of a damaged or missing tooth as a torn or missing button on a shirt. A crown is like a **perfectly molded cap** that slips over the damaged tooth (the "button post"), restoring both appearance and function. A bridge is like a **two-button bridge** — it uses the strong neighboring teeth (abutments) as anchors to support a false tooth (pontic) in the gap. The "fabric" used to stitch this dental suit is the **crown and bridge resin** — strong enough to withstand chewing forces (typically 70–150 N in the molar region), yet polished to look indistinguishable from natural enamel.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following empirical constants are **standardized benchmarks** in dental materials science for these resins:

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compressive strength of PMMA | $\sigma_c$ | **70–100** | MPa |
| Transverse strength of PMMA | $\sigma_t$ | **60–70** | MPa |
| Flexural modulus of composite resin | $E_f$ | **8–15** | GPa |
| Vickers hardness (composite) | $HV$ | **50–100** | kgf/mm² |
| Polymerization shrinkage (MMA) | $\Delta V/V$ | **6–8** | % |
| Polymerization shrinkage (composite) | $\Delta V/V$ | **1.5–3** | % |
| Glass transition temperature (PMMA) | $T_g$ | **105–110** | °C |
| Wear rate (composite) | — | **0.5–3.0** | µm/year |
| Bite force (molar region) | $F$ | **70–150** | N |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Crown and Bridge Resins

The resins are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By chemical matrix**: PMMA, Bis-GMA, UDMA, PEMA.
2. **By polymerization mode**: Heat-cured, Cold-cured (auto-polymerizing), Light-cured, Dual-cured, Microwave-cured.
3. **By clinical duration**: Temporary (provisional) vs. Permanent (definitive) vs. Semi-permanent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For Module 3, the examiner frequently tests the **distinction between PMMA (homopolymer) and Bis-GMA composites (heterogeneous, reinforced systems)**. Know the trade-off: PMMA → high polishability, low strength; Composite → high strength, technique-sensitive polishing.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Tooth Preparation
The abutment tooth is **mechanically reduced** (typically 1.5–2.0 mm occlusally and 1.0–1.5 mm axially) to create **clearance** for the crown material. A **finish line** (shoulder, chamfer, or knife-edge) is prepared.

### Step 2 — Impression Capture
An **elastomeric impression** (polyvinyl siloxane or polyether) is taken. For digital workflows, an **intraoral scanner** generates a **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** file.

### Step 3 — Pattern Fabrication
- **Conventional path**: A wax pattern is built on a die.
- **Digital path**: A 3D-printed resin pattern (PMMA) is milled or printed.

### Step 4 — Resin Selection and Packing
- For heat-cured PMMA, monomer and polymer are mixed at a **powder:liquid ratio of 3:1 by volume**.
- The dough is packed into a **flask** and pressed in a **hydraulic press** (≈ 15–20 MPa).

### Step 5 — Polymerization (Curing)
Free-radical addition polymerization is initiated either by:
- **Heat** (water bath at 74 °C for 90 min, then 100 °C for 30 min — the long-curing cycle).
- **Chemical activator** (tertiary amine + BPO, room temperature).
- **Photo-activation** (camphorquinone + amine co-initiator, blue light at 470 nm).
- **Microwave** (2450 MHz, 3–5 min at 500 W).

### Step 6 — Finishing and Polishing
Sequential abrasion from coarse to fine: tungsten carbide burs → abrasive stones → pumice slurry → polishing compounds (e.g., **tin oxide, zinc oxide**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict LaTeX rule:** Vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-radical polymerization rate | $R_p = k_p \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}} \cdot [M]$ | Cure kinetics |
| 2 | Degree of polymerization | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i + R_t}$ | Chain length control |
| 3 | Number-average MW | $\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0$ | Molecular weight |
| 4 | Volumetric shrinkage | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \dfrac{\rho_{poly} - \rho_{mono}}{\rho_{poly}}$ | Marginal gap |
| 5 | Filler volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$ | Composite reinforcement |
| 6 | Flexural stress (3-point bend) | $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}$ | ISO 4049 testing |
| 7 | Flexural modulus | $E = \dfrac{L^{3}}{4 b d^{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta \delta}$ | Stiffness of bridge span |
| 8 | Rule of mixtures (composite) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ | Modulus estimate |
| 9 | Tg — Fox equation (copolymers) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | Copolymer behavior |
| 10 | Vickers hardness | $HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^{2}}$ | Surface resistance |
| 11 | Wear (Archard equation) | $V = k \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot s}{H}$ | Occlusal wear |
| 12 | Polymerization stress | $\sigma_{poly} = \dfrac{\Delta V / V_0}{C}$ (where $C$ = compliance) | Marginal integrity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond dentistry, the same resin chemistries are deployed in:

- **Maxillofacial prosthetics** (silicone + PMMA hybrids for orbital/nasal defects).
- **Bone cements** (PMMA + BaSO₄ radiopacifier — taught in orthopaedic Module overlap).
- **3D-bioprinted scaffolds** (UDMA-based photopolymers).
- **Hearing-aid shells** and **orthotic devices** (CAD/CAM PMMA).

The cross-disciplinary principle is identical: **design a polymeric material that balances biocompatibility, mechanical load-bearing, aesthetic translucency, and processability**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Polymerization Shrinkage of MMA → PMMA

**Given Data:**
- Density of monomer (MMA), $\rho_{mono} = 0.94$ g/cm³
- Density of polymer (PMMA), $\rho_{poly} = 1.18$ g/cm³
- Molar mass of MMA, $M_0 = 100.12$ g/mol

**Step 1 — Molar volume of monomer**

The molar volume is the volume occupied by 1 mole of substance:

$$
V_{m,\text{mono}} = \frac{M_0}{\rho_{mono}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
V_{m,\text{mono}} = \frac{100.12 \text{ g/mol}}{0.94 \text{ g/cm}^{3}}
$$

$$
V_{m,\text{mono}} = 106.51 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{mol}
$$

*Explanation:* One mole of liquid MMA occupies 106.51 cm³ before reaction.

**Step 3 — Molar volume of repeating unit in polymer**

For PMMA, the repeating unit has the same molar mass (100.12 g/mol) but a higher density because the chains are packed closer in the solid state:

$$
V_{m,\text{poly}} = \frac{M_0}{\rho_{poly}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
V_{m,\text{poly}} = \frac{100.12 \text{ g/mol}}{1.18 \text{ g/cm}^{3}}
$$

$$
V_{m,\text{poly}} = 84.85 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{mol}
$$

*Explanation:* One mole of polymerized MMA repeating unit occupies only 84.85 cm³.

**Step 5 — Volumetric shrinkage formula**

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{V_{m,\text{mono}} - V_{m,\text{poly}}}{V_{m,\text{mono}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values**

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{106.51 - 84.85}{106.51}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{21.66}{106.51}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = 0.2034
$$

**Step 7 — Express as a percentage**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} \approx 20.3 \% \text{ (theoretical)}}
$$

*Explanation:* The theoretical maximum volumetric shrinkage is ~20%, but in practice, since not all monomer converts, and additives are present, the **observed shrinkage is 6–8%** — a clinically important number that causes marginal gaps and microleakage in crowns.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Flexural Strength (3-Point Bending, ISO 4049)

**Given Data:**
- Load at fracture, $P = 45$ N
- Span length, $L = 20$ mm
- Specimen width, $b = 4$ mm
- Specimen thickness, $d = 2$ mm

**Step 1 — Recall the 3-point flexure formula**

$$
\sigma = \frac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}
$$

*Explanation:* The maximum tensile stress on the lower surface of a beam under central load.

**Step 2 — Substitute values into the numerator**

$$
3 \cdot P \cdot L = 3 \cdot 45 \text{ N} \cdot 20 \text{ mm} = 2700 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator**

$$
2 b d^{2} = 2 \cdot 4 \text{ mm} \cdot (2 \text{ mm})^{2} = 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 4 = 32 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation**

$$
\sigma = \frac{2700 \text{ N·mm}}{32 \text{ mm}^{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma = 84.4 \text{ MPa}}
$$

*Explanation:* This flexural strength satisfies the **ISO 4049 minimum of 50 MPa** for Type II restorative composite, confirming the material's suitability for crown/bridge use.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Modulus

**Given Data:**
- Filler: silica glass, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $V_f = 0.60$
- Matrix: Bis-GMA/TEGDMA, $E_m = 3$ GPa, $V_m = 0.40$

**Step 1 — Rule of mixtures (isostrain — Voigt model)**

$$
E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
E_c = (70)(0.60) + (3)(0.40)
$$

$$
E_c = 42.0 + 1.2
$$

$$
\boxed{E_c = 43.2 \text{ GPa}}
$$

*Explanation:* The composite modulus is **~14× higher than the neat resin**, illustrating the dramatic reinforcing effect of fillers — the engineering basis for using composites in load-bearing crowns.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PMMA vs. Composite vs. PFM

| Property | PMMA (Temporary) | Composite Resin | PFM (Crown Standard) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aesthetic translucency | Moderate | High | Highest (porcelain) |
| Compressive strength | 70–100 MPa | 250–350 MPa | 800–1500 MPa |
| Flexural strength | 60–70 MPa | 100–150 MPa | 400–600 MPa |
| Polymerization shrinkage | 6–8 % | 1.5–3 % | 0 (fired) |
| Wear resistance | Low | Moderate-High | Very High |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Biocompatibility | Excellent | Excellent (with care) | Excellent |
| Service life | 1–6 months | 5–10 years | 15–30 years |
| Repairability | Easy | Moderate | Difficult |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Crown and Bridge Resins

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crown and Bridge Resins] --> B[By Chemistry]
    A --> C[By Cure Mode]
    A --> D[By Clinical Use]

    B --> B1[PMMA]
    B --> B2[Bis-GMA Composite]
    B --> B3[UDMA Hybrid]
    B --> B4[PEEK Polyetheretherketone]

    C --> C1[Heat Cured]
    C --> C2[Auto Polymerized Cold Cured]
    C --> C3[Light Cured 470 nm]
    C --> C4[Dual Cured]
    C --> C5[Microwave Cured]

    D --> D1[Temporary Provisional]
    D --> D2[Permanent Definitive]
    D --> D3[Semi Permanent]
```

## 4.2 Crown Fabrication Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Tooth Preparation] --> P2[Impression or Digital Scan]
    P2 --> P3[Die or STL Model]
    P3 --> P4[Wax Up or CAD Design]
    P4 --> P5[Resin Selection]
    P5 --> P6[Polymerization Heat Light Chemical]
    P6 --> P7[Divesting and Trimming]
    P7 --> P8[Polishing Sequence]
    P8 --> P9[Cementation on Abutment]
    P9 --> P10[Finished Crown or Bridge]
```

## 4.3 Polymerization Reaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[MMA Monomer CH2 C CH3 COOCH3] --> R1[Initiation BPO 70 C or Amine RT]
    R1 --> R2[Free Radical CH2 dot C CH3 COOCH3]
    R2 --> G1[Propagation Chain Growth]
    G1 --> P1[PMMA Long Chain Polymer]
    P1 --> T1[Termination Radical Coupling or Disproportionation]
    P1 --> S1[Shrinkage 6 to 8 percent]
    P1 --> H1[Heat Generation Exothermic]
```

## 4.4 Comparison of Three Restorative Material Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PMMAA[PMMA Temporary Crown]
        M[Matrix Only PMMA] --> N[No Filler]
    end

    subgraph COMPA[Composite Resin Crown]
        F1[Inorganic Filler 60 to 80 percent] --> S1[Silane Coupling Agent]
        S1 --> M1[Bis GMA Resin Matrix]
        M1 --> I1[Photoinitiator CQ Amine]
    end

    subgraph PFMA[Porcelain Fused to Metal PFM]
        M2[Metal Substructure Ni Cr or Au] --> O[Opaque Porcelain Layer]
        O --> D[Dentin Porcelain]
        D --> E[Enamel Porcelain]
        E --> G[Glaze]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The progression from PMMA → Composite → PFM mirrors the **material-property trade-off** in design — increasing performance and durability comes at the cost of processability, repairability, and cost. The student's role in KTU exams is to **map clinical demand to material architecture**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]
**Q: Define crown and bridge resins. List any four ideal requirements of these materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Crown and bridge resins are **polymeric biomaterials** used to fabricate **artificial crowns** and **fixed partial dentures (bridges)** in prosthodontics (1 mark).

**Four ideal requirements** (1/2 mark each, total 2 marks):

1. **Adequate mechanical strength** — must withstand masticatory forces of 70–150 N in the molar region without fracture.
2. **Excellent biocompatibility** — non-toxic, non-allergenic, non-irritant to pulp and soft tissues.
3. **Dimensional stability** — minimal polymerization shrinkage and water sorption to preserve marginal fit.
4. **Aesthetic translucency** — should mimic the natural tooth shade and light transmission properties of enamel and dentin.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]
**Q: Differentiate between heat-cured and cold-cured PMMA resins used in crown and bridge fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Heat-Cured PMMA | Cold-Cured PMMA |
|---|---|---|
| Initiator | Benzoyl peroxide (BPO) | Tertiary amine + BPO |
| Curing temperature | 74–100 °C (water bath) | Room temperature (≈ 25 °C) |
| Molecular weight | Higher (10⁶) | Lower (10⁵) |
| Strength | Higher | Lower |
| Residual monomer | < 0.5 % | 3–5 % |
| Application | Definitive crowns | Temporary / provisional crowns |

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the differential table — minimum 4 valid points.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. **Confusing "compressive strength" with "flexural strength"** — these are distinct properties tested by different ISO standards (ISO 9917 vs. ISO 4049).
> 2. **Omitting units (MPa, °C, %)** — every numerical answer must carry a unit, or 0.5 mark is deducted.
> 3. **Writing equations without explaining the symbols** — for a derivation question, the examiner expects **explicit variable definitions** before substitution.
> 4. **Stating "PMMA is strong"** without comparative numerical evidence — always quote MPa values.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the classification of crown and bridge resins. Discuss the composition of Bis-GMA-based composite resin in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):**
Crown and bridge resins are classified as follows (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** — homopolymer, used in temporary crowns.
2. **Bis-GMA-based composites** — reinforced resin systems with 60–80% inorganic fillers.
3. **UDMA/TEGDMA hybrids** — modern low-shrinkage systems.
4. **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone)** — high-performance thermoplastic for fixed prostheses.

**Composition of Bis-GMA composite (4 marks):**

| Component | Function | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| **Bis-GMA (matrix)** | Monomer backbone, high viscosity | 10–25 % |
| **TEGDMA (diluent)** | Reduces viscosity, improves handling | 5–10 % |
| **Silica/glass filler** | Mechanical reinforcement | 60–80 % |
| **Silane coupling agent (γ-MPS)** | Bonds filler to matrix | 1–2 % |
| **Camphorquinone (CQ)** | Photoinitiator | 0.2–0.5 % |
| **Tertiary amine (DMAEMA)** | Co-initiator | 0.2–0.5 % |
| **Barium / strontium glass** | Radiopacity for X-ray visibility | 5–15 % |
| **UV stabilizers / inhibitors (BHT)** | Prevents premature cure, extends shelf life | < 0.1 % |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Define polymerization shrinkage. A monomer system has $\rho_{mono} = 0.93$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{poly} = 1.17$ g/cm³ with molar mass 86 g/mol. Calculate the theoretical volumetric shrinkage.**

**Model Solution:**

Polymerization shrinkage is the **reduction in volume** that occurs when monomer molecules (with weak van der Waals spacing) are converted into densely packed polymer chains (with covalent C–C spacing).

(1 mark for definition)

**Step 1 — Molar volume of monomer**

$$
V_{m,\text{mono}} = \frac{M_0}{\rho_{mono}} = \frac{86}{0.93} = 92.47 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{mol}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Molar volume of polymer repeating unit**

$$
V_{m,\text{poly}} = \frac{M_0}{\rho_{poly}} = \frac{86}{1.17} = 73.50 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{mol}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Volumetric shrinkage**

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{92.47 - 73.50}{92.47} = \frac{18.97}{92.47}
$$

(2 marks)

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = 0.2052 = 20.52\%}
$$

(1 mark for the boxed final answer with unit)

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation (1 mark)**

In practice, observed shrinkage is 6–8 % because **not all monomer converts** (degree of conversion = 60–75 % for clinical light-cure), and filler particles occupy volume that does not shrink.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Compare PMMA and Bis-GMA-based composite resins in terms of composition, mechanical properties, polymerization shrinkage, and clinical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | PMMA | Bis-GMA Composite |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Single monomer (MMA) | Bis-GMA + TEGDMA + filler |
| **Compressive strength** | 70–100 MPa | 250–350 MPa |
| **Flexural strength** | 60–70 MPa | 100–150 MPa |
| **Tensile strength** | 30–40 MPa | 50–80 MPa |
| **Polymerization shrinkage** | 6–8 % | 1.5–3 % |
| **Wear resistance** | Low | Moderate–High |
| **Aesthetics** | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Biocompatibility** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Clinical use** | Temporary crowns, denture bases | Permanent crowns, inlays, bridges |
| **Repairability** | Easy | Difficult |

(2 marks for comparison table — minimum 8 rows)

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- PMMA is preferred for **temporary and interim prostheses** because of its ease of fabrication, low cost, and ability to be relined.
- Bis-GMA composites are chosen for **permanent restorations** where occlusal load-bearing and aesthetics are paramount.
- Composites demand **moisture control and incremental curing** to minimize polymerization stress.

**Trade-off (1 mark):**
Strength ↑ , Shrinkage ↓ , Processability ↓, Cost ↑ as we move from PMMA → Composite.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Describe the heat-cure polymerization cycle for PMMA crown and bridge resin. Explain why a long, controlled cycle is essential.**

**Model Solution:**

**Standard Long-Curing Cycle (3 marks):**

1. **Flask closure and bench-cure (1 hour)** — allows the dough to reach uniform consistency.
2. **Water bath at 74 °C for 90 minutes** — initiates slow polymerization below the boiling point of MMA (100.3 °C), preventing monomer vaporization and porosity.
3. **Boiling phase at 100 °C for 30–60 minutes** — completes the conversion of residual monomer.
4. **Slow cooling to room temperature** (1 hour) — prevents internal stresses and crazing.

Total cycle time ≈ **3.5–4 hours**.

**Why a controlled cycle is essential (4 marks):**

1. **Porosity prevention:** Rapid heating above 100 °C causes monomer to vaporize, creating **gaseous porosity** that weakens the crown.
2. **Residual monomer reduction:** A long cycle ensures **degree of conversion > 99 %**, reducing leaching of unreacted MMA (which can cause mucosal irritation and dimensional instability).
3. **Stress relief:** Controlled heating and cooling minimize **internal locked-in stresses** that would otherwise cause warpage and dimensional change.
4. **Molecular weight optimization:** Slow thermal activation yields **higher molecular weight chains**, directly improving strength and fatigue resistance.
5. **Marginal fit preservation:** Uniform shrinkage (and compensation by investing expansion) is essential for accurate marginal fit of crowns.

(Any 4 points, 1 mark each)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Crown and Bridge Resins):**

- **Crown and bridge resins are dental polymeric materials** for fabricating prosthetic crowns and fixed partial dentures.
- **PMMA = poly(methyl methacrylate)**; monomer = MMA (methyl methacrylate, $M_0 = 100.12$ g/mol).
- **PMMA is the workhorse of provisional restorations**; **Bis-GMA composites dominate definitive resin-bonded crowns**.
- **Theoretical polymerization shrinkage of MMA → PMMA ≈ 20 %**; observed clinical shrinkage = **6–8 %**.
- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) of PMMA ≈ 105 °C** — important for thermal stability and sterilization.
- **Free-radical polymerization** = initiation (BPO 70 °C or amine + BPO at RT) → propagation → termination.
- **Bis-GMA composite composition**: matrix (Bis-GMA + TEGDMA, 10–25 %) + filler (silica/glass, 60–80 %) + silane coupling agent + CQ photoinitiator + amine co-initiator + radiopacifier.
- **Silane coupling agent = γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (γ-MPS)** — bridges inorganic filler to organic matrix.
- **Rule of mixtures** gives composite modulus: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- **3-point flexure formula (ISO 4049):** $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}$; minimum flexural strength for restorative composite = **50 MPa**.
- **Heat-cure cycle**: 74 °C / 90 min + 100 °C / 30 min — long cycle prevents porosity and ensures low residual monomer.
- **Cold-cure (auto-polymerizing)** resins use **tertiary amine + BPO** at room temperature — faster but weaker with higher residual monomer (3–5 %).
- **Light-cure** uses **camphorquinone (CQ) + tertiary amine** activated at **470 nm blue light** — depth of cure ≈ 2 mm per exposure.
- **CAD/CAM PMMA blanks** allow digital milling of provisional crowns with **superior fit (marginal gap < 100 µm)**.
- **PFM (Porcelain-Fused-to-Metal)** remains the gold standard for permanent crowns (compressive strength 800–1500 MPa).
- **PEEK** (polyetheretherketone) is an emerging high-performance polymer crown material with **E ≈ 3–4 GPa** and excellent biocompatibility.
- **Masticatory force** in molar region = **70–150 N**; material must withstand this cyclic load for **> 10⁶ cycles** without fatigue failure.
- **Differentiate carefully**: polymerization shrinkage (volume reduction during cure) ≠ setting expansion (thermal/contraction during cooling).
- **Clinical success metric**: marginal gap of a well-fitting crown should be **< 120 µm** (CDA specification).
- **KTU 2024 examiner's pet topics**: classification diagrams, comparison tables, polymerization shrinkage derivations, ISO standards (ISO 4049, ISO 9917).

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 3, TOPIC: CROWN AND BRIDGE RESINS**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (PBPOT304), NEP 2020 outcome-based pedagogy, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, and Board Examiner evaluation patterns.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Teeth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plastic Teeth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastic teeth** are artificial denture teeth fabricated from polymeric materials — primarily **polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)** and its modified copolymers — used as replacements for natural teeth in complete and partial denture prostheses. They are manufactured either as **factory-made (ready-made) stock teeth** by industrial injection-molding/pressing processes, or as **custom-made (customized) teeth** fabricated chair-side/lab-side using heat-cure or auto-cure acrylic resin dough.

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PBPOT304, Module 3)**, plastic teeth are classified under *denture base polymers and accessory polymeric components*, and are standardized under **ADA Specification No. 15** (American Dental Association) and **ISO 3336** for synthetic polymer teeth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> *Plastic teeth are pre-formed tooth shapes made from cross-linked acrylic polymers (chiefly PMMA) that are chemically or mechanically retained to a polymethyl methacrylate denture base to restore masticatory function, speech, and aesthetics in edentulous patients.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Epoxy Keychain" Concept

Think of a plastic tooth like a **factory-molded plastic LEGO brick** snapped onto a larger plastic baseplate:
- The **LEGO brick** = the plastic tooth (PMMA, pre-shaped with cusps, ridges, and shade).
- The **baseplate** = the acrylic denture base (pink, gum-colored PMMA).
- The **snap-fit / chemical weld** = the interfacial bond formed when the tooth's undersurface (ridge-lap area) is dissolved by methyl methacrylate (MMA) monomer during packing, creating a **co-polymerized monolithic union**.

Just as a LEGO brick won't fall off a baseplate if pressed correctly during assembly, a plastic tooth chemically fuses with the denture base when the monomer softens the ridgelap surface and creates cross-linked continuity. A **porcelain tooth**, in contrast, is like a **ceramic tile glued to plastic** — it relies on mechanical undercuts (diatoric holes) and a chemical bonding agent, since it cannot be solvent-softened.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:**
> Examiners frequently test the *bonding mechanism* — solvent welding via MMA diffusion for plastic teeth vs. mechanical retention via pins/diatorics for porcelain teeth. This single distinction is worth **3 marks minimum** in Part A questions.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU Highlighted)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymer Base** | Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) | ADA Spec. 15 |
| **Filler Type** | Inorganic (silica) or organic (cross-linker EGDMA) | ISO 3336 |
| **Hardness (Knoop)** | 18–22 KHN | ADA Spec. 15 |
| **Compressive Strength** | **≥ 70 MPa** | ISO 3336 |
| **Density** | 1.18–1.20 g/cm³ | Manufacturer datasheet |
| **Shade Range** | 16 VITA shades + 3 bleach shades | VITA classical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution of Plastic Teeth

The transition from porcelain to plastic teeth represents one of the most significant material advances in prosthodontics:

1. **Pre-1930s**: Only porcelain (ceramic) teeth were available — hard, brittle, noisy on contact ("clicking dentures").
2. **1930s**: Dr. George S. Schwegler and Dr. L. J. Porter (1934) introduced **acrylic resin teeth** as an alternative — softer, silent, bondable.
3. **1940s–1950s**: Standardization through **ADA Specification No. 15** (1953, revised 1992).
4. **1970s onward**: Introduction of **cross-linked PMMA** (with EGDMA), **IPN (Interpenetrating Polymer Network)** teeth, and **composite teeth** with inorganic fillers.
5. **2000s–Present**: **Nanohybrid composite teeth**, **double-cross-linked teeth**, and **polymer-ceramic hybrid teeth** (e.g., SR Phonares II, Vita MFT).

### 2.2 Composition of Plastic Teeth (Layered Architecture)

Modern plastic teeth are **multi-layered composites** to simulate the gradient optical properties of natural dentition:

| Layer | Function | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **Enamel Layer (Outer)** | Translucent, wear-resistant | Highly cross-linked PMMA + nano-silica filler (5–15 wt%) |
| **Dentin Layer (Body)** | Opaque, color-saturated | PMMA + pigmented urethane dimethacrylate (UDMA) |
| **Cervical/Neck Layer** | Yellowish, gingival blend | Heavily pigmented PMMA copolymer |
| **Ridgelap (Underside)** | Bondable surface for denture base | Linear (uncross-linked) PMMA — **deliberately designed to be soluble in MMA monomer** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ridgelap Principle (High-Yield KTU Concept):**
> The undersurface (ridgelap) of every plastic tooth is intentionally made of **uncross-linked or lightly cross-linked PMMA**. When the denture pack is processed, **MMA monomer diffuses into this layer, swells it, and creates a co-polymerized gradient (interpenetration zone ≈ 50–200 µm)**. This is the *sole reason* plastic teeth bond chemically to the denture base — porcelain teeth cannot do this.

### 2.3 Types of Plastic Teeth — KTU Classification Matrix

#### A. Based on Manufacturing Process

1. **Conventional (Standard) Plastic Teeth** — PMMA with 0–2% EGDMA cross-linker. Average wear resistance.
2. **Cross-Linked Plastic Teeth** — PMMA + 5–15% EGDMA or TEGDMA cross-linker. Improved wear and stain resistance.
3. **IPN (Interpenetrating Polymer Network) Teeth** — Two interlocked polymer networks (e.g., PMMA + polyurethane). High impact strength.
4. **Double Cross-Linked Teeth** — PMMA + cross-linker + inorganic microfillers. Maximum wear resistance.
5. **Composite / Nano-Composite Teeth** — PMMA matrix + 30–60 wt% pre-polymerized resin fillers or nano-silica. Hardest plastic teeth available.
6. **Hybrid (Polymer-Ceramic) Teeth** — Polymer matrix with embedded ceramic micro-particles (e.g., SR Phonares).

#### B. Based on Mold Type
- **Anatomical (Cusp) Teeth** — Cuspal inclinations of 20°–45°. Used for balanced occlusion in complete dentures.
- **Semi-anatomical (Cuspal 10°–20°)** — Compromise teeth.
- **Non-anatomical (Zero-degree / Monoplane)** — Flat occlusal surfaces, used in flat-cuspid (Pound's) or lingualized occlusion.
- **Tubeless (Pinless) Teeth** — No diatorics; rely solely on chemical bond.
- **Diatoric Teeth** — Have retention holes/pins on ridgelap (used for porcelain or older plastic teeth).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Polymerization & Properties of Plastic Teeth

> [!NOTE]
> Plastic teeth don't usually involve heavy numerical computation in KTU exams, but the following formulas are **frequently asked for 3-mark derivations** in the polymer chemistry context of Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree of Polymerization (Xn)** | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{[M]_0}{[M]_0 - [M]}$ | dimensionless | Determines chain length of PMMA |
| 2 | **Conversion (%) at Gel Point** | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\overline{f}}$ | dimensionless | Flory-Stockmayer theory; $\overline{f}$ = functionality of monomer |
| 3 | **Volumetric Shrinkage of MMA→PMMA** | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{monomer} - V_{polymer}}{V_{monomer}} \times 100 \approx 21\%$ | % | Critical for denture fit and tooth-to-base gap |
| 4 | **Vickers / Knoop Hardness** | $HV = \dfrac{1.854 \cdot F}{d^2}$ | kg/mm² | Wear resistance indicator |
| 5 | **Cross-link Density (ν)** | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N_A}{M_c}$ | mol/m³ | Where $M_c$ = molecular weight between cross-links |
| 6 | **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ (Fox equation) | K | Determines service temperature; PMMA Tg ≈ 105 °C |
| 7 | **Wear Rate (Archard)** | $V = \dfrac{k \cdot F \cdot L}{H}$ | mm³ | $k$ = wear coefficient, $F$ = force, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness |
| 8 | **Water Sorption (ISO 10477)** | $W_{sp} = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | µg/mm³ or % | Plastic teeth sorption ≤ 32 µg/mm³ per ADA 15 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU TIP — Use `\vert` in tables, never `|`:**
> When writing the **Fox equation** in KTU answer sheets, prefer the explicit form $w_1$ + $w_2$ = 1 (mass fraction condition) and avoid using absolute value bars like `|x|`, which break markdown tables. Substitute with `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dental Prosthodontics**: 95%+ of complete dentures worldwide use plastic teeth (PMMA-based).
- **Maxillofacial Prosthetics**: Custom plastic teeth are used in obturators and speech-aid prostheses.
- **Implant-Supported Hybrid Prostheses**: Acrylic teeth are processed onto milled Ti/Co-Cr bars (e.g., All-on-4).
- **Pediatric Dentistry**: Modified PMMA teeth used in pediatric denture for cleft palate cases.
- **Biomedical Research**: Plastic teeth serve as standardized wear-test specimens (e.g., Leinfelder wear tester, Ivoclar 4-body wear test).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Polymerization Shrinkage of MMA → PMMA (The Tooth-to-Base Gap Problem)

**Why this matters:** When a plastic tooth is processed into a denture base, the base undergoes **~21% volumetric shrinkage** as MMA polymerizes. This creates a **microscopic gap** at the tooth-base interface, which is the single most common cause of debonding.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define volumetric shrinkage (Sv)} \quad S_v &= \frac{V_{MMA}^{liquid} - V_{PMMA}^{solid}}{V_{MMA}^{liquid}} \times 100 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Use known densities} \quad \rho_{MMA} &= 0.943 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad \rho_{PMMA} = 1.18 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\
\\
\text{Step 3: For } n = 1 \text{ mol of MMA (100.12 g)} \quad V_{MMA} &= \frac{100.12}{0.943} = 106.17 \text{ cm}^3 \\
V_{PMMA} &= \frac{100.12}{1.18} = 84.85 \text{ cm}^3 \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Substitute into shrinkage equation} \quad S_v &= \frac{106.17 - 84.85}{106.17} \times 100 \\
S_v &= \frac{21.32}{106.17} \times 100 \\
S_v &\approx 20.08\% \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Engineering interpretation} \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Essentially, } 21\% \text{ of the original monomer volume is "lost" during} \\
&\text{polymerization as van der Waals gaps close to covalent bond lengths.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This **~21% shrinkage** is concentrated near the tooth ridgelap interface. To compensate, manufacturers:
- Pre-roughen the ridgelap to increase surface area.
- Use **chemical adhesion promoters** (e.g., dichloromethane, MMA monomer wetting).
- Recommend **high-pressure injection molding** instead of compression packing to reduce shrinkage voids.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Degree of Cross-Linking vs. Wear Resistance (Fox-Flory Relationship)

The **Fox equation** predicts the glass transition temperature (Tg) of a copolymer blend, which directly correlates with the wear resistance of a plastic tooth.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fox Equation:} \quad \frac{1}{T_g} &= \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}} \\
\\
\text{Where:} \quad w_1, w_2 &= \text{mass fractions of components} \\
T_{g1}, T_{g2} &= \text{glass transition temperatures (in Kelvin)} \\
\\
\text{Given for a PMMA-EGDMA cross-linked tooth:} \quad & \\
w_1 = 0.90 \text{ (PMMA)}, \quad T_{g1} &= 378 \text{ K (105 °C)} \\
w_2 = 0.10 \text{ (EGDMA network)}, \quad T_{g2} &= 393 \text{ K (120 °C)} \\
\\
\text{Step 1: Substitute values} \quad \frac{1}{T_g} &= \frac{0.90}{378} + \frac{0.10}{393} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Compute each term} \quad \frac{0.90}{378} &= 0.002381 \text{ K}^{-1} \\
\frac{0.10}{393} &= 0.000254 \text{ K}^{-1} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Sum} \quad \frac{1}{T_g} &= 0.002381 + 0.000254 = 0.002635 \text{ K}^{-1} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Take reciprocal} \quad T_g &= \frac{1}{0.002635} = 379.5 \text{ K} \\
T_g &\approx 106.5 \text{ °C} \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Engineering interpretation} \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Adding 10\% EGDMA cross-linker raises Tg by only 1.5 °C, but} \\
&\text{dramatically improves wear resistance (×2 to ×4) due to network} \\
&\text{topology. Tg must exceed 37 °C (mouth temperature) to avoid} \\
&\text{softening — comfortably satisfied.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Plastic vs. Porcelain Teeth (Mandatory KTU Comparison Table)

| Property | Plastic Teeth (PMMA) | Porcelain Teeth (Feldspathic) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bond to Denture Base** | Chemical (monomer welding) | Mechanical (pins/diatorics) + silane coupling | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Hardness (Knoop)** | 18–22 KHN | 400–460 KHN | ⭐⭐ |
| **Compressive Strength** | 70–100 MPa | 300–500 MPa | ⭐⭐ |
| **Impact Strength** | High (8–12 kJ/m²) | Very low (<2 kJ/m², brittle) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Wear on Opposing Dentition** | Low (gentle on enamel) | High (enamel abrasion) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Adjustability (Grinding)** | Easy with burs | Very difficult (cracks/chips) | ⭐⭐ |
| **Esthetics (Translucency)** | Moderate | Excellent | ⭐⭐ |
| **Stain Resistance** | Moderate (porous over time) | Excellent | ⭐⭐ |
| **Clicking Sound** | Silent | Noisy | ⭐ |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High | ⭐⭐ |
| **Indications** | Complete dentures, implant hybrids | Long-span fixed partial dentures (rare) | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Manufacturing Flow of Plastic Teeth (Injection Molding Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw MMA Monomer] --> B[Pre-polymerization to Syrup]
    B --> C[Add Initiator BPO 0.5%]
    C --> D[Add Pigments and Cross-linker EGDMA]
    D --> E[Pour into Multi-cavity Steel Mold]
    E --> F[Close Mold and Heat at 100C]
    F --> G[Injection or Compression Molding]
    G --> H[Cooling and Cure Cycle]
    H --> I[Deflash and Trim Flash Lines]
    I --> J[Quality Inspection Shade and Hardness]
    J --> K[Pack and Sterilize]
    K --> L[Ship to Dental Labs]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification Hierarchy of Plastic Teeth

```mermaid
    A[Plastic Teeth] --> B[Based on Polymer Type]
    A --> C[Based on Mold Morphology]
    A --> D[Based on Bonding Method]

    C --> C2[Semi-anatomical Cuspal 10 to 20 deg]
    C --> C3[Non-anatomical Zero Degree]

    D --> D1[Diatoric Retention Holes]
    D --> D2[Pin Retention]
    D --> D3[Tubeless Bond by MMA Welding]

    style A fill:#fff4e1
    style B fill:#ffe1e1
    style C fill:#e1f0ff
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Tooth-Base Interface Bonding Mechanism

```mermaid
    A[Plastic Tooth Ridgelap Uncross-linked PMMA] -->|MMA Monomer Diffusion| B[Swollen Interphase 50 to 200 micrometer]
    B -->|Heat Activation| C[Co-polymerization]
    C --> D[Chemical Bond PMMA-PMMA Network]
    D --> E[Monolithic Denture]

    A2[Porcelain Tooth Glazed Surface] -->|No Diffusion| B2[Mechanical Interlock Only]
```
