# Module 4: Chemistry of Peptides — Section 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Question Bank

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "peptide bond". Illustrate with an example the formation of a dipeptide from two amino acids.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A peptide bond is the amide linkage **–CO–NH–** formed when the α-carboxyl group (–COOH) of one amino acid condenses with the α-amino group (–NH₂) of another, releasing a molecule of water.
- **Example reaction (1 mark):** Glycine + Alanine → Glycyl-Alanine + H₂O.
- **Equation & labelling (1 mark):** Write the full structural equation, clearly marking the newly formed peptide bond.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the partial double-bond character of the peptide bond and its consequences on protein structure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Resonance statement (1 mark):** The peptide C–N bond shows resonance between **O=C–N–H** and **O⁻–C=N⁺–H**, giving ~40 % double-bond character.
- **Planarity consequence (1 mark):** The six atoms Cα–C(=O)–N–H–Cα are **coplanar**, restricting rotation about the C–N bond.
- **Biological consequence (1 mark):** This rigidity forces the polypeptide to adopt defined secondary structures (α-helix, β-sheet) stabilized by hydrogen bonding.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either (a) OR (b)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **Edman degradation** method for the sequential determination of amino acids in a peptide. List its advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Edman degradation uses **phenyl isothiocyanate (PITC)** at pH 9 to cleave one N-terminal residue at a time from a peptide chain, without disrupting the rest of the peptide bonds. The cleaved residue is converted to a **phenylthiohydantoin (PTH) amino acid** and identified by HPLC.

2. **Three-step cyclic reaction (3 marks):**
   - **Coupling:** PITC + peptide → phenylthiocarbamoyl peptide.
   - **Cleavage:** TFA → anilinothiazolinone (ATZ) derivative + shortened peptide.
   - **Conversion:** ATZ (in aqueous acid) → stable PTH-amino acid.
   - The cycle repeats on the new N-terminus; each cycle identifies one residue.

3. **Advantages (1 mark):**
   - Reveals the *sequence* (not just the identity) of N-terminal residues.
   - Automatable (e.g., Applied Biosystems sequencer).
   - Works on as little as 1–10 picomoles of peptide.

4. **Limitations (1 mark):**
   - Practical only for peptides **≤ 50 residues** due to cumulative side reactions.
   - Proline-rich or modified N-termini may give ambiguous results.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **Sanger's N-terminal reagent** method. How does it differ from Edman degradation in principle?

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sanger's reagent (1 mark):** **1-Fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene (FDNB)** — a yellow, UV-active aryl halide that reacts selectively with the free N-terminal α-amino group of a peptide.

2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Peptide + FDNB (NaHCO₃ buffer, pH ~9, 40 °C) → **DNP-peptide** (yellow).
   - Acid hydrolysis (6 M HCl, 110 °C, 24 h) cleaves *all* peptide bonds, releasing free amino acids.
   - The **DNP-labeled N-terminal residue** is extracted into ether and identified by **paper chromatography or TLC** against known DNP-amino acid standards.

3. **Comparison with Edman (2 marks):**

   | Feature | Sanger (FDNB) | Edman (PITC) |
   |---|---|---|
   | Information obtained | **Only N-terminus** | **Full N→C sequence** |
   | Peptide bonds cleaved during analysis | **All (destructive)** | **Only one per cycle (mild)** |
   | Number of cycles | **One** | **Many (repetitive)** |
   | Detection | UV-visible (yellow) | HPLC / MS |
   | Sample amount | Milligrams | Picomoles |

4. **Significance (1 mark):** Sanger used this method to determine the complete sequence of **insulin (1955)** — the first protein ever sequenced — earning the 1958 Nobel Prize.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **Merrifield Solid-Phase Peptide Synthesis (SPPS)**. State its advantages over classical solution-phase synthesis.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (1 mark):** Introduced by **R. B. Merrifield (1963)**; the growing peptide is anchored via its **C-terminus** to an insoluble **chloromethylated polystyrene resin** (1–2 % cross-linked with divinylbenzene). All reactions occur on the *solid* support, allowing simple filtration/washing to remove byproducts.

2. **Synthetic cycle (3 marks):**
   - **Attach:** First Fmoc-amino acid is esterified to the resin (DIPEA, DMF).
   - **Deprotect:** Fmoc group removed with **20 % piperidine in DMF** → free α-NH₂.
   - **Couple:** Next Fmoc-amino acid activated with **HBTU/HOBt/DIPEA** → amide bond forms.
   - **Wash:** DMF, DCM washes remove excess reagents.
   - **Repeat** until all residues are coupled.
   - **Cleave:** Final peptide is released from the resin using **TFA + scavengers** (TIS, water).

3. **Coupling chemistry note (1 mark):** The formation of the peptide bond still follows the same **nucleophilic acyl substitution** mechanism discussed in Section 3.1; the resin simply immobilizes the growing chain.

4. **Advantages over solution-phase (2 marks):**
   - **No intermediate purification:** excess reagents and byproducts are washed away.
   - **Automation possible** (Applied Biosystems 430A, etc.).
   - **High yields** (≥ 99 % per coupling achievable with optimized activators).
   - **Racemization suppressed** under Fmoc-strategy conditions.
   - **Faster overall** (50-mer peptide in < 1 week vs. months in solution phase).

5. **Limitations (1 mark):**
   - Limited to peptides ≤ ~50 residues; longer chains suffer cumulative yield loss.
   - "Difficult sequences" (e.g., hydrophobic stretches) can aggregate on the resin.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **biological and biomedical significance** of peptides with suitable examples. Give two examples of bioactive peptides and their functions.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Significance overview (1 mark):** Peptides are biological **signalling molecules, hormones, antibiotics, and structural components**. They bridge the gap between amino acids (building blocks) and proteins (functional macromolecules).

2. **Bioactive peptide examples (4 marks):**

   | Peptide | Sequence length | Function | Engineering/Medical Use |
   |---|---|---|---|
   | **Insulin** | 51 aa (A: 21, B: 30) | Hormone regulating blood glucose | **Type-1 & Type-2 diabetes therapy** (recombinant human insulin) |
   | **Oxytocin** | 9 aa | Uterine contraction, social bonding | **Labor induction, postpartum hemorrhage** control |
   | **Vasopressin (ADH)** | 9 aa | Water reabsorption in kidneys, vasoconstriction | **Diabetes insipidus treatment** (Desmopressin analog) |
   | **Glutathione (GSH)** | 3 aa (γ-Glu–Cys–Gly) | Master antioxidant, redox buffer | **Hepatoprotective drug, anti-aging cosmeceuticals** |
   | **Cyclosporin A** | 11 aa (cyclic) | Immunosuppressant | **Organ transplant rejection prevention** |
   | **Enkephalins** | 5 aa | Endogenous opioid analgesia | **Pain management research** |

3. **Biomedical applications (2 marks):**
   - **Peptide drugs:** Liraglutide (GLP-1 analog for diabetes), Octreotide (somatostatin analog for acromegaly), Bivalirudin (anticoagulant).
   - **Tissue engineering:** RADA16 self-assembling peptide scaffolds for spinal-cord injury repair.
   - **Vaccines:** Synthetic peptide epitopes (e.g., **EpitoME** for SARS-CoV-2 spike protein).

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Peptide chemistry is the cornerstone of modern **biopharmaceuticals, biomaterials, and diagnostics**, making it a critical topic for biomedical and biopolymer engineering students.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw resonance structures** of the peptide bond → loses 2 marks easily.
> 2. **Wrong direction of sequence:** Always write **N-terminus → C-terminus** (left to right). Writing it backwards is an instant zero.
> 3. **Confusing Edman with Sanger:** Students often interchange FDNB and PITC. Remember: **FDNB = Sanger (one-shot, yellow); PITC = Edman (cyclic, white powder)**.
> 4. **Not stating bond-length values:** Vague answers like "the bond is short" fetch no marks; quote **1.32 Å vs. 1.47 Å**.
> 5. **Skipping the "planar six-atom" justification** for rigidity — must explicitly say *the six atoms Cα–C(=O)–N–H–Cα are coplanar*.
> 6. **In Merrifield SPPS answers:** failing to mention the role of **piperidine (Fmoc deprotection)** and **TFA (final cleavage)** costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Peptide bond** = **–CO–NH–** amide linkage, formed by **condensation** (loss of H₂O, 18 Da per bond).
- **Resonance:** O=C–N–H ↔ O⁻–C=N⁺–H gives **partial double-bond character (~40 %)** and **planarity**.
- **C–N bond length** = **1.32 Å**; **C–Cα** = 1.53 Å; **Cα–N** = 1.47 Å.
- **Six atoms** of the peptide unit (**Cα, C, O, N, H, Cα**) are **coplanar**; **ω ≈ 180° (trans)**; **φ, ψ** are free.
- **N-terminus** = free –NH₂ (left); **C-terminus** = free –COOH (right). Sequence is always **N → C**.
- **Classification:** Dipeptide (2), Tripeptide (3), Oligo (3–10), Polypeptide (10–50), Protein (>50).
- **Nomenclature rule:** Replace "–ine" with "–yl" for all except the **C-terminal** residue.
- **Ninhydrin** → Ruhemann's purple (570 nm) for free α-amino groups; **Biuret** → violet (540 nm) for ≥ 2 peptide bonds.
- **Sanger's reagent (FDNB)** → identifies **only N-terminal** residue (yellow DNP-AA, destructive).
- **Edman degradation (PITC)** → identifies **N-terminal residue sequentially** in cycles; limit ~50 residues.
- **Merrifield SPPS** → C-terminal anchored to **polystyrene resin**; Fmoc strategy uses **piperidine** (deprotect) and **HBTU/HOBt** (couple); final cleavage with **TFA**.
- **Mass of polypeptide** = $110n - 18(n-1) = 92n + 18$ Da (using average residue mass 110 Da).
- **Bioactive peptides:** Insulin (51 aa, glucose regulation), Oxytocin (9 aa, uterine contraction), Glutathione (3 aa, antioxidant), Cyclosporin A (11 aa, immunosuppressant).
- **Engineering applications:** Peptide drugs (GLP-1 analogs), peptide hydrogels (RADA16) for tissue engineering, peptide-based biosensors and vaccines.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam mantra:** *"Resonance → Planarity → Rigidity → Helix"*. Master this single chain of logic and you can answer 70 % of the peptide-related theory questions.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Polypeptides** are short or long, unbranched biopolymer chains formed by the linear condensation (dehydration) linkage of two or more **α-amino acid** monomer units through amide linkages known as **peptide bonds** (–CO–NH–). When the number of amino acid residues in such a chain exceeds a biologically functional threshold (typically > 50–100 residues) and the chain folds into a specific, biologically active three-dimensional conformation, the macromolecule is classified as a **protein**.

Mathematically, the general formation reaction for a polypeptide is:

$$
\text{H}_2\text{N–CHR–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR'–COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR–CO–NH–CHR'–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Where R and R' represent the variable side chains that distinguish the 20 standard α-amino acids encoded by the genetic code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between *polypeptide* (a chain) and *protein* (a functional, folded macromolecule) is a frequently tested conceptual point. The KTU board expects students to clarify that **a protein is a functional unit, while a polypeptide refers strictly to the primary covalent chain**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of protein synthesis like constructing a meaningful sentence in the English language:

- **Amino acids** → individual **letters** of the alphabet (26 letters, but biology uses 20 standard ones).
- **Polypeptide chain** → a **word** formed by joining letters in a specific sequence.
- **Protein (folded)** → a **sentence or paragraph** that has grammatical structure, punctuation (folding), and conveys specific meaning (biological function).

Just as rearranging the same letters gives different words with different meanings, the same 20 amino acids can be sequenced in trillions of ways to create millions of unique proteins, each with a highly specific job in the cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Block:**
> - **Residue:** An amino acid unit after it has lost a water molecule during peptide bond formation.
> - **N-terminus:** The end of the chain bearing a free –NH₂ group.
> - **C-terminus:** The end of the chain bearing a free –COOH group.
> - **Monomer:** α-amino acid (general formula H₂N–CHR–COOH).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The α-Amino Acid Backbone

Every standard α-amino acid carries:
- One **amino group (–NH₂)** attached to the α-carbon.
- One **carboxyl group (–COOH)** attached to the α-carbon.
- One **hydrogen atom (–H)** attached to the α-carbon.
- One **variable R-group (side chain)** — this is the only structural difference between amino acids.

The α-carbon is the central stereocenter (except in glycine, where R = H), making all standard amino acids **chiral** and optically active (L-configuration in natural proteins).

### 2.2 Peptide Bond: The Covalent Linkage

The peptide bond is formed by a **condensation (dehydration synthesis)** reaction between the –COOH of one amino acid and the –NH₂ of the next. The reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable in aqueous solution and is therefore driven by **ATP hydrolysis** in living cells (catalyzed by the ribosome).

**Critical geometric features of the peptide bond (Kendrew & Pauling, 1939):**
- It is a **partial double bond** (≈ 40 % double-bond character) due to resonance between C=O and C–N.
- This resonance forces the six atoms **Cα–C(=O)–N(H)–Cα** to be **coplanar** (rigid planar amide group).
- The bond is **trans**-configured in natural proteins (cis only at proline residues).
- Bond length of C–N is ≈ **1.32 Å** (intermediate between single 1.47 Å and double 1.27 Å).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Planarity Matters:** The partial double-bond character restricts rotation around the C–N bond. Therefore, the only rotatable bonds in a polypeptide backbone are the **phi (φ)** angle around N–Cα and the **psi (ψ)** angle around Cα–C(=O). These two torsion angles per residue are what define the secondary structure of proteins (Ramachandran plot analysis).

### 2.3 Hierarchical Levels of Protein Structure

Proteins are described at four levels of structural organization, each adding a layer of complexity:

| Level | Description | Stabilizing Interactions | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (1°)** | Linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds | **Covalent peptide bonds** | Insulin A-chain sequence |
| **Secondary (2°)** | Local regular folding into α-helices or β-sheets | **Hydrogen bonds** between backbone C=O and N–H | α-keratin (helix); silk fibroin (sheet) |
| **Tertiary (3°)** | 3D folding of an entire polypeptide chain | Hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, **disulfide bridges (S–S)**, ionic bonds, van der Waals | Myoglobin |
| **Quaternary (4°)** | Assembly of multiple folded polypeptide subunits | Same as tertiary, plus subunit–subunit interfaces | Hemoglobin (4 subunits: 2α + 2β) |

### 2.4 Classification of Proteins

**A. Based on Shape:**
- **Fibrous proteins:** Long, parallel polypeptide chains; insoluble; structural role (collagen, keratin, silk fibroin, elastin).
- **Globular proteins:** Polypeptide chain folded into a compact spheroid; soluble; dynamic roles (enzymes, hormones, antibodies, hemoglobin).

**B. Based on Composition:**
- **Simple proteins:** Yield only α-amino acids on hydrolysis (e.g., albumin, globulin).
- **Conjugated proteins:** Contain a non-protein **prosthetic group** (e.g., glycoprotein, lipoprotein, nucleoprotein, phosphoprotein, metalloprotein, chromoprotein, flavoprotein).

### 2.5 Denaturation

**Denaturation** is the disruption of the native 2°, 3°, and 4° structures of a protein **without breaking peptide bonds**. The primary sequence remains intact. Denaturants include:
- Heat (thermal denaturation)
- pH extremes (acid/alkali)
- Heavy metal ions (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺)
- Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone)
- Urea, guanidinium chloride
- Radiation (UV, X-ray)

> [!NOTE]
> **Renaturation Note:** Some denatured proteins (e.g., ribonuclease) can spontaneously refold into their native state once the denaturant is removed — this classic experiment by **Christian Anfinsen (1972, Nobel Prize)** proved that the **primary sequence alone determines the 3D structure** of a protein.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| General amino acid formula | $\text{H}_2\text{N–CH(R)–COOH}$ | — | α-carbon is the chiral center |
| Residue molecular weight (avg.) | $M_0 \approx 110$ | g/mol | Average of 20 standard AAs (water already removed) |
| Average residue mass | $\overline{M_r} \approx 110$ | Da (Dalton) | Used for protein MW estimation |
| Protein molecular weight | $M_w = n \cdot 110$ | g/mol | Where n = number of residues |
| Degree of polymerization | $DP = n = (M_w / 110)$ | dimensionless | Number of amino acid units |
| Water molecules released | $n - 1$ | — | For a chain of n residues |
| Peptide bond geometry: ω angle | $\omega = 180°$ | degrees | Trans-planar (Cα–C–N–Cα) |
| Rotatable backbone angles | $\phi \text{ (phi)}, \psi \text{ (psi)}$ | degrees | Two per residue (Ramachandran plot) |
| α-Helix parameters | 3.6 residues/turn, pitch 5.4 Å, rise 1.5 Å/residue | Å, residues | Pauling–Corey model (1951) |
| β-Sheet: H-bond pattern | Inter-strand N–H···O=C | — | Parallel vs. antiparallel |
| Isoelectric point (no ionizable R) | $pI = (pK_{a1} + pK_{a2})/2$ | — | pH at which net charge = 0 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Water Released During Polypeptide Formation

**Problem:** A polypeptide chain contains *n* amino acid residues joined by peptide bonds. Show that the total number of water molecules eliminated during synthesis is *(n − 1)*, and derive the relationship between the polymer molecular weight and the monomer molecular weight.

**Step 1 — Setting up the monomer count.**
Let the polypeptide be assembled linearly from *n* amino acid monomers. The first amino acid contributes its full –NH₂ and –COOH ends; each subsequent monomer contributes one new residue.

**Step 2 — Counting bond formation events.**
Each peptide bond formation consumes **one water molecule** (condensation reaction):
- 1st bond formed → 1 H₂O released
- 2nd bond formed → 1 H₂O released
- ...
- (n − 1)th bond formed → 1 H₂O released

**Step 3 — Summing the water release.**
Summing the geometric sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total H}_2\text{O released} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} 1 \\
&= (n - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass balance derivation.**
Let $M_0$ be the average molecular weight of a free amino acid monomer (≈ 110 g/mol for residue average, ≈ 136 g/mol for the intact average with side chains). The total mass of the polymer is the sum of monomer masses minus the mass of released water.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{polymer}} &= n \cdot M_0 - (n - 1) \cdot M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \\
M_{\text{polymer}} &= n \cdot M_0 - (n - 1) \cdot 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplification (residue-basis).**
In biochemistry, $M_0$ is often expressed as the **residue weight** (water already accounted for, i.e., $M_{\text{residue}} = M_0 - 18$). Then the formula collapses to:

$$
M_{\text{polymer}} = n \cdot M_{\text{residue}} \quad \text{(with } M_{\text{residue}} \approx 110 \text{ Da)}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Molecular Weight of Insulin

**Problem:** Human insulin is a protein hormone composed of two polypeptide chains: the **A-chain (21 residues)** and the **B-chain (30 residues)**, connected by disulfide bonds. The two chains are synthesized separately and then joined. Using an average residue molecular weight of 110 Da, calculate:

(a) The total number of amino acid residues.
(b) The approximate molecular weight of insulin.
(c) The number of water molecules lost during biosynthesis.

**Step 1 — Total residue count.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= n_A + n_B \\
n &= 21 + 30 \\
n &= 51 \text{ residues}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weight estimation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{insulin}} &= n \cdot 110 \text{ Da} \\
M_{\text{insulin}} &= 51 \times 110 \\
M_{\text{insulin}} &= 5610 \text{ Da} \approx 5.61 \text{ kDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The experimentally reported value is **5808 Da** — our estimate is within ≈ 3.4 % error, validating the average-residue approximation.

**Step 3 — Water molecules released.**

For the **A-chain** alone (21 residues):

$$
n_A - 1 = 21 - 1 = 20 \text{ water molecules}
$$

For the **B-chain** alone (30 residues):

$$
n_B - 1 = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ water molecules}
$$

Total water released during biosynthesis (excluding formation of the 3 disulfide bonds, which do not release water):

$$
\boxed{\text{Total H}_2\text{O} = 20 + 29 = 49 \text{ water molecules}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Number of Possible Sequence Combinations

**Problem:** Show that even a small protein of 100 residues can have astronomically many possible sequences.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{sequences}} &= 20^{n} \\
N_{\text{sequences}} &= 20^{100} \\
\log_{10}(N) &= 100 \cdot \log_{10}(20) \\
\log_{10}(N) &= 100 \times 1.3010 \\
\log_{10}(N) &= 130.10 \\
N_{\text{sequences}} &\approx 1.26 \times 10^{130}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is greater than the estimated number of atoms in the observable universe (≈ 10⁸⁰), illustrating the **enormous combinatorial capacity** of proteins.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Peptide Bond Formation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AA1: H2N-CHR1-COOH] --> C{Condensation Reaction}
    A2[AA2: H2N-CHR2-COOH] --> C
    C -- Remove H2O --> D[Peptide: H2N-CHR1-CO-NH-CHR2-COOH]
    D --> E[Free H2O molecule released]
    C -. Catalyzed by .-> F[Ribosome + ATP]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Folding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Primary Structure: AA1-AA2-AA3-...-AAn] -->|H-bonds along backbone| S[Secondary Structure]
    S --> S1[Alpha-Helix: 3.6 residues/turn]
    S --> S2[Beta-Sheet: inter-strand H-bonds]
    S --> S3[Random Coil: no regular pattern]
    S -->|Folds into 3D shape| T[Tertiary Structure]
    T -->|Stabilized by R-group interactions| T1[Disulfide Bridges]
    T --> T2[Hydrophobic Core]
    T --> T3[Ionic Salt Bridges]
    T --> T4[van der Waals Forces]
    T -->|Multiple subunits assemble| Q[Quaternary Structure]
    Q --> Q1[Hemoglobin: 2 alpha + 2 beta subunits]
    Q --> Q2[DNA Polymerase holoenzyme]
```

### 4.3 Protein Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PR[Proteins] --> SH{Shape}
    SH --> FIB[Fibrous: structural, insoluble]
    SH --> GLO[Globular: functional, soluble]
    PR --> CO{Composition}
    CO --> SIM[Simple: amino acids only]
    CO --> CON[Conjugated: protein + prosthetic group]
    CON --> C1[Glycoprotein: carbohydrate]
    CON --> C2[Lipoprotein: lipid]
    CON --> C3[Metalloprotein: metal ion]
    CON --> C4[Nucleoprotein: nucleic acid]
    FIB --> F1[Collagen, Keratin, Silk]
    GLO --> G1[Enzymes, Hormones, Antibodies]
```

### 4.4 Denaturation Pathway Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Native Folded Protein] -- Heat / pH / Heavy metals / Urea --> D[Denatured State]
    D -- Remove denaturant --> R[Renatured Protein]
    D -- Irreversible damage --> I[Permanently Inactive]
    N -. Primary sequence intact .-> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with one suitable example each:*
*(i) Polypeptide*
*(ii) Protein*
*(iii) Peptide bond*

**Model Answer:**
- **Polypeptide:** A linear chain of α-amino acid residues linked by peptide bonds, formed via condensation polymerization. Example: oxytocin (9 residues).
- **Protein:** A biologically functional macromolecule composed of one or more polypeptide chains folded into a specific 3D conformation. Example: hemoglobin.
- **Peptide bond:** The amide linkage (–CO–NH–) joining two amino acid residues with elimination of water. It exhibits partial double-bond character due to resonance, enforcing planarity on the six backbone atoms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between fibrous and globular proteins. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fibrous | Globular |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Long, parallel, thread-like | Compact, spheroidal |
| Solubility | Water-insoluble | Water-soluble |
| Function | Structural / mechanical | Dynamic / functional |
| Examples | Collagen, keratin, silk fibroin | Hemoglobin, myoglobin, insulin, lysozyme |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a neat labeled diagram, describe the **four levels of protein structure**. Mention the type of bonding/interactions that stabilize each level.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the phenomenon of **denaturation of proteins**. List any four denaturating agents. Discuss whether denaturation is always reversible with a suitable example.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Levels of Protein Structure:**

1. **Primary structure (1°):** The linear sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, determined by the gene. Stabilized entirely by **covalent peptide bonds**. Example: the sequence of 51 residues in insulin (A-chain: 21; B-chain: 30). The primary structure determines all higher levels of organization.

2. **Secondary structure (2°):** Local regular folding patterns maintained by **hydrogen bonds** between the backbone C=O of one peptide bond and the N–H of another. Two main types:
   - **α-helix:** Right-handed helix with 3.6 residues per turn, pitch 5.4 Å, stabilized by intra-chain H-bonds between residue *i* and *i + 4*.
   - **β-pleated sheet:** Polypeptide strands lie side-by-side, linked by inter-strand H-bonds; can be parallel or antiparallel.

3. **Tertiary structure (3°):** The overall 3D folding of a single polypeptide chain into a compact, biologically active shape. Stabilized by:
   - Hydrophobic interactions (nonpolar R-groups cluster inward)
   - Hydrogen bonds (R-group to R-group, or R-group to backbone)
   - Ionic (salt) bridges between oppositely charged R-groups
   - **Disulfide bridges (–S–S–)** between two cysteine residues
   - van der Waals forces

4. **Quaternary structure (4°):** The spatial arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits (each with its own 1°, 2°, 3° structure) into a single functional protein. Stabilized by the same non-covalent interactions as tertiary structure, plus subunit–subunit interfaces. Example: **hemoglobin (Hb)** — tetrameric assembly of 2 α-chains and 2 β-chains, each containing a heme prosthetic group.

**(b) Denaturation:**

Denaturation is the disruption of the native 2°, 3°, and 4° structures of a protein **without hydrolyzing the peptide bonds** of the primary chain. The protein loses its biological activity but the amino acid sequence remains intact.

**Four denaturing agents:**
1. **Heat** — disrupts hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions (e.g., boiling an egg).
2. **pH extremes** — alter ionization of acidic/basic R-groups, breaking ionic bridges.
3. **Heavy metal ions** (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Ag⁺) — react with –SH, –NH₂, –COOH groups, forming insoluble complexes.
4. **Organic solvents** (ethanol, acetone) — interfere with hydrophobic interactions.
5. *(Bonus)* Urea and guanidinium chloride — disrupt H-bonds.
6. *(Bonus)* Radiation (UV, X-ray) — disrupts aromatic residues and disulfide bonds.

**Reversibility:**
Denaturation is **not always reversible**. It is reversible when only weak non-covalent interactions are disrupted (e.g., **ribonuclease** refolds spontaneously after urea removal — Anfinsen's experiment, 1972 Nobel Prize). It becomes **irreversible** when covalent changes occur, such as coagulation of egg albumin on boiling (new disulfide and hydrophobic interactions lock the denatured state).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Classify proteins based on **(i) shape** and **(ii) composition**, giving suitable examples for each class.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* What is a **peptide bond**? Explain its geometry and the concept of **partial double-bond character**. Discuss the Ramachandran plot briefly.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Proteins:**

**(i) Based on Shape:**

- **Fibrous proteins:** Elongated, thread-like molecules. Polypeptide chains run parallel and are held together by cross-links. Insoluble in water. Function: structural support.
  - Examples: **collagen** (tendons, bone matrix), **keratin** (hair, nails, wool), **silk fibroin** (silk fibers), **elastin** (elastic tissue).

- **Globular proteins:** Polypeptide chain folded into a compact, near-spherical 3D shape. Soluble in water or aqueous salt solutions. Function: dynamic roles.
  - Examples: **hemoglobin** (O₂ transport), **myoglobin** (O₂ storage in muscle), **insulin** (hormone), **lysozyme** (enzyme), **antibodies** (immune defense).

**(ii) Based on Composition:**

- **Simple proteins:** Yield only α-amino acids on complete hydrolysis. No non-protein moiety.
  - Examples: **albumin** (egg white, blood plasma), **globulin** (blood serum), **histones** (DNA-binding in nucleus).

- **Conjugated proteins:** Contain a non-protein **prosthetic group** attached to the polypeptide chain.
  - **Glycoprotein** — carbohydrate prosthetic group (e.g., mucin).
  - **Lipoprotein** — lipid (e.g., LDL, HDL for cholesterol transport).
  - **Phosphoprotein** — phosphate group (e.g., casein in milk).
  - **Metalloprotein** — metal ion (e.g., hemoglobin contains Fe²⁺; ceruloplasmin contains Cu²⁺).
  - **Nucleoprotein** — nucleic acid (e.g., chromatin: DNA + histones).
  - **Chromoprotein** — colored pigment (e.g., hemoglobin, cytochrome).
  - **Flavoprotein** — flavin nucleotide (e.g., FAD in succinate dehydrogenase).

**(b) Peptide Bond — Geometry & Partial Double-Bond Character:**

A **peptide bond** is the amide linkage (–C(=O)–N(H)–) formed by condensation between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of another, releasing one water molecule.

**Geometry of the Peptide Bond:**

1. The peptide bond is **planar** — the six atoms O, C, N, H, Cα(i), and Cα(i+1) all lie in a single plane.
2. The bond is **trans** in natural proteins (ω = 180°), minimizing steric clashes between adjacent R-groups. Cis-configurations (ω = 0°) are rare and occur only at proline residues.
3. The C–N bond length is **1.32 Å**, intermediate between a C–N single bond (1.47 Å) and a C=N double bond (1.27 Å).

**Partial Double-Bond Character — Resonance Explanation:**

The lone pair on the nitrogen atom is delocalized into the carbonyl π-system, giving rise to two resonance structures:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Structure I:} \quad & \text{O=C–N(H)} \\
\text{Structure II:} \quad & \text{⁻O–C=N(H)⁺}
\end{aligned}
$$

The real structure is a **resonance hybrid** of both forms. The resonance gives:
- ≈ 40 % double-bond character to C–N,
- Restricted rotation around C–N,
- Increased planarity,
- Enhanced stability of the amide group,
- A net **dipole moment** across the peptide bond.

**Ramachandran Plot — Brief Discussion:**

Since rotation around the peptide bond (C–N) is restricted, the only rotatable backbone bonds are the **φ (phi)** angle about the **N–Cα** bond and the **ψ (psi)** angle about the **Cα–C** bond. The **Ramachandran plot** is a 2D scatter plot of ψ (y-axis) versus φ (x-axis), showing the sterically allowed and disallowed conformational regions for each amino acid residue. Most residues fall in allowed regions corresponding to **α-helix** (φ ≈ −60°, ψ ≈ −45°), **β-sheet** (φ ≈ −120°, ψ ≈ +120°), or **left-handed helix**. Glycine, lacking a side chain, occupies a much wider region; proline is highly restricted.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "polypeptide" and "protein":** A polypeptide is a chain; a protein is a folded, functional unit. Many students lose marks by using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting that denaturation does NOT break peptide bonds:** The primary structure stays intact — a very common error.
> 3. **Wrong water count:** When asked for water molecules released, students often write *n* instead of *(n − 1)*. Always remember the first peptide bond needs two amino acids, so for n residues there are n − 1 bonds.
> 4. **Amino acid backbone:** Students frequently forget the α-hydrogen or misplace the position of the R-group. The α-carbon bears: –NH₂, –COOH, –H, and –R.
> 5. **Geometry of peptide bond:** Stating "the peptide bond is a single bond" loses marks — it is a **partial double bond** with 40 % double-bond character. This is a high-weight KTU question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Monomer unit:** α-amino acid (general formula H₂N–CHR–COOH, **20 standard types**).
- **Polymerization type:** Condensation (dehydration) — releases one H₂O per peptide bond.
- **Linkage:** Peptide bond (–CO–NH–), with **partial double-bond character (40 %)**, making the six backbone atoms **planar**.
- **Water count formula:** For a polypeptide of *n* residues → *(n − 1)* water molecules released.
- **Average residue MW:** ≈ **110 Da**; protein MW ≈ n × 110 Da.
- **Sequence combinations:** 20ⁿ possible sequences for *n* residues — astronomically large even for small proteins.
- **Four structural levels:** 1° (sequence, peptide bonds) → 2° (α-helix/β-sheet, H-bonds) → 3° (3D fold, hydrophobic + S–S + ionic) → 4° (multi-subunit assembly).
- **α-Helix:** 3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 Å pitch, 1.5 Å rise/residue, intra-chain H-bond between *i* and *i + 4*.
- **β-Sheet:** Inter-strand H-bonds; can be parallel or antiparallel.
- **Disulfide bridges:** Form only between two **cysteine** residues; covalent, very strong.
- **Classification:** Fibrous vs. Globular (shape); Simple vs. Conjugated (composition).
- **Denaturation:** Disruption of 2°/3°/4° structures; primary structure intact; **not always reversible** (Anfinsen's ribonuclease experiment shows it can be).
- **Ramachandran plot:** φ vs. ψ torsion angles map the sterically allowed conformations of each residue.
- **Isoelectric point (pI):** pH at which the protein has zero net charge; minimum solubility and mobility occur at this pH — exploited in electrophoresis and precipitation techniques.
- **Biological functions of proteins:** catalysis (enzymes), transport (Hb), structure (collagen), defense (antibodies), motion (actin/myosin), regulation (hormones, transcription factors), and storage (ferritin).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Organisation in Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Organisation in Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Organisation in Proteins** refers to the hierarchical, four-tiered architecture by which a linear chain of amino acids (polypeptide) folds and assembles into a biologically active, three-dimensional macromolecule. This hierarchy is conventionally classified as **Primary**, **Secondary**, **Tertiary**, and **Quaternary** structures, each level being stabilized by a distinct set of physico-chemical interactions.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBPOT304, Module 4), the structural organisation in proteins is examined under the lens of:
- The **covalent** backbone (peptide bonds).
- The **non-covalent** folding forces (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic interactions, van der Waals forces).
- The **covalent cross-links** (disulfide bridges).
- The role of **denaturation and renaturation** in biopolymer engineering.

In strict IUPAC and Lehninger-biochemistry terminology, a **protein** is a biological macromolecule composed of one or more polypeptide chains, each chain being a linear polymer of **α-amino acid** residues linked by **peptide (amide) bonds**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Origami Crane Factory":**
> Imagine proteins as a factory producing delicate origami cranes. 
> 1. **Primary structure** = The straight, unfolded strip of paper with creases marked at specific points (the amino acid sequence encoded in DNA). 
> 2. **Secondary structure** = Folding the strip along its length into simple repeatable patterns (spirals or zig-zags). 
> 3. **Tertiary structure** = Bending and twisting that pre-folded strip into the full 3D crane shape. 
> 4. **Quaternary structure** = Clipping several finished cranes together to form a hanging mobile.
> 
> If you snip the paper (denature), the crane loses its shape and can no longer fly — even though the paper is still intact. This is exactly how proteins work in the body: **function follows form**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the most frequently quoted KTU-relevant metrics for protein structural analysis:

- **Average molecular weight of an amino acid residue** ≈ **110 Da**.
- **Bond length of a peptide bond (C–N)** ≈ **1.32 Å** (intermediate between a single bond 1.49 Å and a double bond 1.27 Å — indicating **partial double-bond character**).
- **Trans configuration** of the peptide bond is **energetically favoured** over *cis* (the *cis* form is sterically forbidden for all residues except **proline**, where ~30 % may be *cis*).
- **The α-helix** has **3.6 amino acid residues per turn** and a **rise per residue of 1.5 Å** (pitch = **5.4 Å**).
- **The β-strand** (antiparallel) has a **rise per residue of 3.4 Å** and a **repeat distance of 7.0 Å**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners expect students to draw and label the **α-helix and β-pleated sheet**, mark the **hydrogen bonds** (between C=O of residue *i* and N–H of residue *i+4* in an α-helix), and tabulate the **differences between the four structural levels**. Memorize the *3.6 residues/turn* number — it is a high-frequency short-answer topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture — The Four Levels

Proteins do not jump from a random coil to a functional 3D shape in a single step. The process is **strictly hierarchical** and **thermodynamically driven** (driven by the minimization of Gibbs free energy, $\Delta G < 0$).

### 2.1.1 Primary Structure ($1^{\circ}$)
- **Definition:** The unique, linear sequence of amino acid residues covalently linked by **peptide (amide) bonds** in a polypeptide chain.
- **Backbone chemistry:** Formed via a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** between the α-carboxyl group (–COOH) of one amino acid and the α-amino group (–NH₂) of the next, releasing one H₂O molecule per bond.
- **The peptide bond is rigid and planar** due to its partial double-bond character (resonance between C=O and C=N forms).
- **Information source:** Genetically encoded by the **nucleotide sequence of the corresponding mRNA** (via the genetic code, triplet codons).

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU favourite):** Human insulin has 51 residues arranged in two chains: Chain A (21 residues) and Chain B (30 residues), connected by disulfide bridges. Sickle-cell anaemia results from a **single amino-acid substitution** (Glu → Val at position 6 of the β-globin chain) — proving that *primary structure dictates all higher-order folding*.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Structure ($2^{\circ}$)
- **Definition:** Regular, recurring spatial arrangements of the polypeptide backbone, stabilized primarily by **hydrogen bonds between the carbonyl oxygen (C=O) of one peptide bond and the amide hydrogen (N–H) of another**.
- The side chains (R groups) are **not** involved in the bonding — they project outward.
- The two classical motifs are:
  1. **α-Helix (Pauling & Corey, 1951)** — a right-handed helix with **3.6 residues per turn**, pitch **5.4 Å**, rise per residue **1.5 Å**. Stabilized by **i → i+4** hydrogen bonds. R-groups project outward, away from the helical axis.
  2. **β-Pleated Sheet** — polypeptide strands (β-strands) laid side-by-side, forming a zig-zag pleated sheet. Hydrogen bonds are **inter-strand** (perpendicular to the strand direction). Two types: **parallel** and **antiparallel**.
  3. **β-Turns (reverse turns)** — 4-residue motifs that reverse chain direction, stabilized by a **1→3** hydrogen bond (C=O of residue *i* to N–H of residue *i+3*). Common in globular proteins.
  4. **Random coil / extended chain** — regions lacking regular hydrogen-bonding patterns.

### 2.1.3 Tertiary Structure ($3^{\circ}$)
- **Definition:** The overall three-dimensional folding of a single polypeptide chain in space, including the spatial arrangement of its secondary-structure elements and side chains.
- Stabilized by **multiple types of interactions** (see table 2.1).
- Determines the **biological function** of the protein.
- Examples: **Myoglobin** (single polypeptide with one heme), **lysozyme**, **ribonuclease A**.

### 2.1.4 Quaternary Structure ($4^{\circ}$)
- **Definition:** The spatial arrangement and interaction of **two or more independently folded polypeptide subunits (protomers)** in a multi-subunit protein complex.
- **Not present in all proteins** — e.g., myoglobin is monomeric, while haemoglobin is tetrameric.
- Stabilized by the **same forces as tertiary structure**, plus the **symmetric arrangement** of subunits.
- Examples: **Haemoglobin** (α₂β₂, 4 subunits), **DNA polymerase**, **virus capsids**, **immunoglobulins** (IgG has 4 chains — 2 heavy + 2 light).

## 2.2 Stabilizing Forces — A Complete Classification

The **single most high-yield topic** for KTU 2024 from this module is the identification of forces stabilizing each structural level. Memorize Table 2.1.

### Table 2.1 — Forces Stabilizing Protein Structure

| Structural Level | Primary Stabilizing Force | Bond Type | Energy Scale (kJ mol⁻¹) | Cleavable By |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary ($1^{\circ}$) | **Peptide (amide) bond** C–N | Covalent (partial double) | ≈ 200 – 400 | Strong acid/base + heat (hydrolysis) |
| Secondary ($2^{\circ}$) | **Hydrogen bond** (C=O···H–N) | Non-covalent, dipole-dipole | ≈ 4 – 20 | Heat, urea, guanidinium chloride |
| Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | **Hydrophobic interactions** | Entropy-driven non-covalent | ≈ 4 – 12 | Heat, detergents, organic solvents |
| Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | **Ionic / salt bridges** (± charge) | Electrostatic | ≈ 12 – 30 | pH change, high salt |
| Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | **Hydrogen bonds (side chain)** | Non-covalent | ≈ 4 – 20 | Heat, urea |
| Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | **Van der Waals (London dispersion)** | Non-covalent, transient dipole | ≈ 0.4 – 4 | Heat |
| Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | **Disulfide bridge** (S–S) | Covalent | ≈ 200 | Reducing agents (β-mercaptoethanol, DTT) |
| Quaternary ($4^{\circ}$) | Same as tertiary, **plus subunit-subunit interfaces** | Non-covalent + S–S | Variable | Heat, pH, dissociating agents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly state that disulfide bridges stabilize *secondary* structure. **They do not.** Disulfide bridges form between two **cysteine side chains** (–SH → –S–S–) that are *far apart in the sequence* but *close in 3D space*. They therefore stabilize the **tertiary** (and sometimes quaternary) structure.

## 2.3 The Thermodynamic Driver — Hydrophobic Collapse

A frequently asked **14-mark analytical question** in KTU asks *"Why do proteins fold?"*

The answer lies in the **hydrophobic effect**:
- Non-polar (hydrophobic) side chains (e.g., Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met) **cluster together in the interior** of the protein, away from the aqueous cytosol.
- This **burial of hydrophobic surface area** releases ordered water molecules from the solvation shell back into the bulk solvent, **increasing the entropy of water**.
- Since $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, a large positive $\Delta S_{\text{water}}$ drives $\Delta G < 0$, making folding **spontaneous**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** "**HIDES**" — **H**ydrophobic **I**nteractions **D**rive **E**ntropic **S**tabilization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a biochemistry/polymer module, "derivations" are predominantly **stoichiometric, geometric, and energetic**. Below are the four most exam-relevant step-by-step derivations, all of which KTU examiners have asked either as a 3-mark short problem or as a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Peptide Bonds & Water Released

**Problem Statement:**
A polypeptide contains *n* amino acid residues. Derive an expression for (a) the number of peptide bonds formed, and (b) the number of water molecules released during its synthesis from free amino acids.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial condition.**
Each free amino acid carries one free α-amino group (–NH₂) and one free α-carboxyl group (–COOH). There are *n* such molecules, so initially there are *n* free –NH₂ groups and *n* free –COOH groups.

**Step 2 — Recognize that a peptide bond is formed by one condensation reaction.**
A single condensation reaction between one –NH₂ and one –COOH forms **one peptide bond** and **releases one H₂O molecule**.

$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CHR–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR'–COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR–CO–NH–CHR'–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3 — Count the bonds.**
To join *n* amino acids in a **single linear chain**, exactly *(n − 1)* peptide bonds are required. (Just as *n* railway coaches need *n − 1* couplers to form one train.)

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{peptide}} = n - 1\,}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometry of water.**
By 1:1 stoichiometry, the same number of water molecules is liberated.

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{water}} = n - 1\,}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (typical KTU 3-mark).**
A tripeptide (n = 3, e.g., glutathione) has

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{peptide}} &= 3 - 1 = 2 \\
N_{\text{water}} &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

A decapeptide (n = 10) has **9 peptide bonds** and **9 water molecules** released.

**Step 6 — Approximate molecular weight.**
Using $M_{\text{res}} \approx 110$ Da and subtracting the mass of lost water ($18$ Da per peptide bond):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{protein, approx}} &= (n \times 110) - (n-1) \times 18 \\
&= 110n - 18n + 18 \\
&= 92n + 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical verification for insulin (51 residues):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{insulin, approx}} &= 92 \times 51 + 18 \\
&= 4692 + 18 \\
&= 4710\ \text{Da}
\end{aligned}
$$

The experimentally reported value for human insulin is **5808 Da** (the discrepancy is due to the two inter-chain disulfide bridges and the presence of three extra residues, which we are ignoring for the linear approximation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Geometry of the α-Helix

**Problem Statement:**
Show that for an ideal α-helix with rise per residue $h = 1.5$ Å and 3.6 residues per turn, the pitch $p$ is 5.4 Å.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let $n_t$ = number of residues per turn = **3.6**.
Let $h$ = vertical rise (translation along the helical axis) per residue = **1.5 Å**.
Let $p$ = pitch of the helix (vertical distance per complete turn).

**Step 2 — Geometric relationship.**
The pitch is simply the rise per residue multiplied by the number of residues in one complete turn:

$$p = n_t \times h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 3.6 \times 1.5\ \text{Å} \\
&= 5.4\ \text{Å}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with experimental data.**
X-ray diffraction of α-keratin and synthetic poly-alanine yields a measured pitch of $5.4 \pm 0.1$ Å, confirming the Pauling–Corey prediction.

**Step 5 — Hydrogen-bonding pattern.**
In the α-helix, the C=O of residue $i$ hydrogen-bonds to the N–H of residue $i + 4$, leaving a loop of 4 residues, which corresponds geometrically to **1.5 turns** of the helix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Residues per H-bond loop} &= 4 \\
\text{Turns spanned} &= \frac{4}{3.6} = 1.111\ldots \text{ (rounded to 1.1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a very common KTU follow-up question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bond Character of the Peptide Bond

**Problem Statement:**
The peptide C–N bond has an experimental length of 1.32 Å. A pure C–N single bond is 1.49 Å long, and a pure C=N double bond is 1.27 Å long. Calculate the **percentage double-bond character** of the peptide bond.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up a linear interpolation.**
The observed bond length falls between the two extremes. Let $f_d$ be the fraction of double-bond character.

$$L_{\text{obs}} = (1 - f_d) \cdot L_{\text{single}} + f_d \cdot L_{\text{double}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.32 &= (1 - f_d) \cdot 1.49 + f_d \cdot 1.27 \\
1.32 &= 1.49 - 1.49 f_d + 1.27 f_d \\
1.32 - 1.49 &= f_d (1.27 - 1.49) \\
-0.17 &= -0.22 \cdot f_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $f_d$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{0.17}{0.22} \\
&= 0.7727\ldots \\
&\approx 0.77
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**

$$\boxed{\,\%\ \text{double-bond character} \approx 77\ \%\,}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
About three-quarters of the peptide bond is in the C=N resonance form. This confers:
- **Planarity** of the six atoms (Cα, C, O, N, H, Cα) around the peptide unit.
- **Restricted rotation** (the dihedral angle ω is essentially fixed at 180° in the *trans* form).
- **Resistance to free rotation**, forcing the chain to behave as a series of rigid planar plates joined at the Cα atoms.

This derivation is a KTU-favourite 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stoichiometry of Disulfide Bridge Formation

**Problem Statement:**
Two free cysteine side chains (–SH) form a disulfide bridge (–S–S–) by an oxidation reaction. Write the balanced equation and calculate the number of disulfides formed when a protein of *c* cysteine residues is fully oxidized.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the half-reaction for one cysteine.**

$$\text{R–SH} \longrightarrow \text{R–S}^\bullet + \text{H}^+ + e^-$$

**Step 2 — Two radicals combine to form the disulfide.**

$$2\ \text{R–SH} \longrightarrow \text{R–S–S–R} + 2\ \text{H}^+ + 2\ e^-$$

**Step 3 — Net balanced reaction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\ \text{R–SH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\ \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{R–S–S–R} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalent forms using $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ or $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ as the oxidant are also accepted.)

**Step 4 — Count the maximum number of disulfides.**
For *c* cysteine residues, the maximum number of disulfides (assuming they all pair intramolecularly and no free –SH remains) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{SS}} &= \frac{c}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU 3-mark).**
Insulin has 6 cysteines (3 per chain A, 1 per chain B, and 2 inter-chain linkages — actually 2 intra in chain A and 2 inter between A and B). The exact pairing is:
- **1 intra-chain disulfide in chain A** (Cys6–Cys11)
- **1 intra-chain disulfide in chain A** (Cys7–Cys7 is incorrect — actually Cys7–Cys7 is a homodimer; the correct insulin has 1 intra A and 2 inter A–B = **3 disulfides**)

With 6 cysteines, $N_{\text{SS}} = 3$ ✓ — confirming the stoichiometry.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison matrix is the **single most heavily tested KTU 2024 exam artefact** from this module. KTU examiners have asked students to "compare and contrast" these levels in **at least one question per semester**.

| Feature | Primary ($1^{\circ}$) | Secondary ($2^{\circ}$) | Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | Quaternary ($4^{\circ}$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **What it describes** | Amino acid sequence | Local backbone folding | 3D fold of one chain | 3D arrangement of multiple chains |
| **Bond type** | Covalent (peptide) | Non-covalent (H-bond) | Non-covalent + S–S | Non-covalent + S–S |
| **Side chains involved?** | Yes (define identity) | No (R-groups project out) | Yes (R-group interactions) | Yes (inter-subunit interfaces) |
| **Stabilization energy** | **~200 – 400 kJ mol⁻¹** | **~4 – 20 kJ mol⁻¹** (H-bond) | **~4 – 200 kJ mol⁻¹** (mixed) | **~4 – 200 kJ mol⁻¹** (mixed) |
| **Helical / sheet motifs** | No | **Yes** (α-helix, β-sheet) | No (but composed of them) | No (but composed of them) |
| **Examples** | Sequence of insulin | Helical region of myoglobin | Folded globular myoglobin | Haemoglobin α₂β₂ tetramer |
| **Disruption by urea** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Disruption by reducing agent** | No (unless disulfides) | No | Yes (S–S cleavage) | Yes (S–S cleavage) |
| **Determined by** | mRNA codons | Backbone H-bond geometry | Hydrophobic collapse + side chains | Subunit-subunit recognition |
| **Lost on denaturation?** | **No** (sequence is preserved) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Sequence preserved?** | — | Depends on $1^{\circ}$ | Depends on $1^{\circ}$ | Depends on $1^{\circ}$ |

---

## 3.6 Denaturation & Renaturation — Quantitative View

**Denaturation** is the loss of $2^{\circ}, 3^{\circ},$ and $4^{\circ}$ structure **without cleavage of the peptide backbone**. The $1^{\circ}$ structure remains intact.

**Common denaturants (KTU short-answer favourite):**

| Denaturant | Mechanism | Effective Against |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat** | Disrupts H-bonds, increases entropy | $2^{\circ}, 3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ |
| **pH extremes** | Protonates/deprotonates side chains, breaks salt bridges | $3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ |
| **Urea (8 M)** | Forms H-bonds with backbone, disrupts internal H-bonds | $2^{\circ}, 3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ |
| **Guanidinium chloride (6 M)** | Similar to urea but stronger | $2^{\circ}, 3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ |
| **SDS (detergent)** | Hydrophobic tail mimics interior, unfolds protein | $3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ |
| **β-Mercaptoethanol / DTT** | Reduces disulfide bridges | $3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ (S–S) |
| **Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺)** | Bind –SH groups | $3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ (S–S) |
| **Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone)** | Disrupt hydrophobic core | $3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous KTU-relevant experiment:** Christian Anfinsen's **ribonuclease A renaturation experiment (1972 Nobel Prize)** demonstrated that the primary structure **carries all the information** required to refold a denatured protein back to its native state. RNase A was denatured with **8 M urea + β-mercaptoethanol** (which broke both H-bonds and disulfides); after removing both reagents, the protein **spontaneously refolded** into the active enzyme with the correct 4 disulfide bonds reforming in the right places.

    subgraph ALPHA["ALPHA HELIX SCHEMATIC PAULING COREY 1951"]
        AH1["R1 SIDE CHAIN OUTWARD"] --- AH2["N H C ALPHA C O BACKBONE"]

        AH3 --- AH4["3.6 RESIDUES PER TURN"]
        AH4 --- AH5["PITCH 5.4 ANGSTROM"]
        AH5 --- AH6["RIGHT HANDED HELIX"]
    end

    subgraph BETA["BETA PLEATED SHEET SCHEMATIC"]
        BP1["STRAND A N TO C DIRECTION"] -- H BONDS PERPENDICULAR --> BP2["STRAND B C TO N ANTIPARALLEL"]
        BP2 -- H BONDS PERPENDICULAR --> BP3["STRAND C N TO C PARALLEL"]
        BP3 --> BP4["RISE PER RESIDUE 3.4 ANGSTROM"]

> **Hand-drawing tip for KTU answer scripts:** When drawing the α-helix, depict a **right-handed spiral** with a clear **i → i+4 H-bond loop**. Mark the **C=O** of residue *i* and the **N–H** of residue *i+4* with a dashed line representing the H-bond. For β-sheets, use **broad arrows** to indicate strand direction (N→C terminus). Always label the H-bonds with the symbol "H-bond" — markers are rewarded.
