# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following question bank strictly follows the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End-Semester Examination) pattern:
> - **Part A** — 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B** — Internal choice, 1 question × 14 marks = 14 marks (with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each)
> - Total from this topic segment: 20 marks (scaled as per actual exam weightage)

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Define the primary structure of a protein. How is a peptide bond formed? Comment on its partial double-bond character.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** The **primary structure** of a protein is the linear, covalently-bonded sequence of amino acid residues joined by peptide bonds, as dictated by the gene. It is the most fundamental level of protein architecture.

**Formation of peptide bond (1 mark):** A peptide bond is formed by a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** between the α-carboxyl group (–COOH) of one amino acid and the α-amino group (–NH₂) of the next, releasing one water molecule:

$$\text{R}_1\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R}_1\text{–CO–NH–R}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Partial double-bond character (1 mark):** The peptide C–N bond has a bond length of **1.32 Å**, intermediate between a single C–N bond (1.49 Å) and a double C=N bond (1.27 Å), giving it **~77 % double-bond character** due to **resonance** between the amide forms. This makes the peptide unit **planar and rigid**, restricting free rotation around the C–N bond.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**List the four levels of protein structure and state the type of bond stabilizing each level.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Level | Stabilizing Bond |
|---|---|
| **Primary ($1^{\circ}$)** | **Covalent peptide bonds** (C–N) |
| **Secondary ($2^{\circ}$)** | **Hydrogen bonds** (between C=O and N–H of the backbone) |
| **Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$)** | **Hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds, hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and covalent disulfide bridges** |
| **Quaternary ($4^{\circ}$)** | **Same as tertiary, plus subunit-subunit interface interactions** |

*(1 mark for naming the four levels + 1 mark for correctly identifying the primary bonds of 1° and 2° + 1 mark for tertiary and quaternary.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **α-helix secondary structure** of proteins. Mention (i) the number of residues per turn, (ii) the pitch, (iii) the hydrogen-bonding pattern, and (iv) the direction of side chains. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Differentiate between the **tertiary and quaternary** structures of proteins. Give one example of each and explain the role of disulfide bridges with a relevant example. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Diagram (2 marks):** A hand-drawn right-handed spiral is mandatory, with H-bonds shown as dashed lines between C=O of residue *i* and N–H of residue *i+4*. Side chains projecting outward should be marked "R". The helical axis should be clearly shown.

**Key numerical parameters (2 marks):**
- **3.6 amino acid residues per turn.**
- **Pitch = 5.4 Å** (= 3.6 × 1.5 Å rise per residue).
- **Rise per residue = 1.5 Å** along the helical axis.
- **Right-handed** in naturally occurring L-amino acids.

**Hydrogen-bonding pattern (2 marks):** Each peptide C=O of residue $i$ hydrogen-bonds to the N–H of residue $i + 4$, forming a 13-atom hydrogen-bonded ring. This produces a **regular, repeating network of H-bonds** along the entire helix. The H-bonds are **parallel to the helical axis**, giving the structure maximum stability.

**Side-chain orientation (1 mark):** All side chains (R-groups) project **outward**, away from the central helical axis, thus avoiding steric clashes with the backbone and allowing interaction with the surrounding solvent or other parts of the protein.

**Example (KTU bonus):** α-Keratin (in hair, nails, wool) is a coiled-coil of two α-helices; myoglobin is rich in α-helices (8 helices labelled A–H).

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Differences between Tertiary and Quaternary Structure (4 marks):**

| Feature | Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) | Quaternary ($4^{\circ}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | 3D fold of **a single** polypeptide chain | 3D arrangement of **two or more** polypeptide chains |
| Subunits involved | **One** polypeptide | **Two or more** (called protomers or subunits) |
| Stabilizing forces | Hydrophobic, ionic, H-bonds, van der Waals, S–S | Same as tertiary, plus subunit-subunit interfaces |
| Present in | All globular/fibrous proteins | Only multi-subunit proteins |
| Example | Myoglobin (single chain) | Haemoglobin (α₂β₂ tetramer) |
| Dissociation on denaturation | — | Subunits may dissociate |

**Example of tertiary structure (1 mark):** **Myoglobin** — a single polypeptide chain of 153 residues folded around a heme group, found in muscle, stores oxygen.

**Example of quaternary structure (1 mark):** **Haemoglobin** — a tetramer of two α-chains and two β-chains (α₂β₂), each similar to myoglobin, transports oxygen in red blood cells.

**Role of disulfide bridges (1 mark):** Disulfide bridges (–S–S–) form between two cysteine residues and are **covalent cross-links** that **stabilize the tertiary and/or quaternary structure**. In **insulin**, two inter-chain disulfide bridges (A7–B7 and A20–B19) hold the A and B chains together, and one intra-chain disulfide (A6–A11) stabilizes the A chain. Reduction of these disulfages (e.g., by β-mercaptoethanol) destroys insulin's biological activity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** What are the different **stabilizing forces** in protein tertiary structure? Rank them in order of **decreasing bond energy** with approximate kJ mol⁻¹ values. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain **protein denaturation and renaturation** with the **Anfinsen experiment** on ribonuclease A. Why is this experiment considered proof that "primary structure dictates tertiary structure"? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):** The tertiary structure of a protein is stabilized by **five major types of interactions** between the side chains (R-groups) of amino acid residues. These are a mix of covalent and non-covalent forces, with bond energies ranging over two orders of magnitude.

**Ranking in decreasing bond energy (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Rank | Force | Approx. Energy (kJ mol⁻¹) | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Covalent disulfide bridge (S–S)** | **~200** | Covalent, very strong |
| 2 | **Ionic (salt bridge)** | **12 – 30** | Electrostatic, strong |
| 3 | **Hydrogen bond (side chain)** | **4 – 20** | Dipole-dipole, moderate |
| 4 | **Hydrophobic interaction** | **4 – 12** | Entropy-driven, weak individually but collectively dominant |
| 5 | **Van der Waals (London dispersion)** | **0.4 – 4** | Transient induced dipoles, very weak |

**Discussion (1 mark):** Although disulfide bridges have the highest individual bond energy, in many cytoplasmic proteins (which lack cysteines) the **collective hydrophobic effect** is the **dominant** overall stabilizing force. This is because there are thousands of hydrophobic contacts in a typical globular protein, and they are reinforced by the entropic gain of releasing ordered water molecules.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Denaturation (1 mark):** Denaturation is the loss of the native $2^{\circ}, 3^{\circ},$ and $4^{\circ}$ structure of a protein due to disruption of the non-covalent interactions (and possibly disulfide bridges) **without cleavage of the peptide backbone**. The primary structure remains intact.

**Common denaturants (1 mark):** Heat, extreme pH, 8 M urea, 6 M guanidinium chloride, SDS detergent, β-mercaptoethanol (reduces disulfides), and heavy metals (e.g., Hg²⁺ binds –SH).

**Anfinsen's Ribonuclease A Experiment — Step-by-Step (4 marks):**

1. **Native RNase A** is a 124-residue enzyme with **4 disulfide bridges** and full enzymatic activity.
2. **Denaturation with 8 M urea + β-mercaptoethanol**: Urea disrupts all H-bonds (unfolds the protein); β-mercaptoethanol **reduces all 4 disulfides** to free –SH groups. The protein is now a **random coil with no activity**.
3. **Removal of urea** (by dialysis): The protein partially refolds, but the 8 possible ways to re-form 4 disulfides give only **1 % native activity** because the disulfides reform randomly.
4. **Removal of β-mercaptoethanol** (by dialysis): Now the disulfides are free to find their correct pairing. The protein **spontaneously refolds into its native conformation** with the **correct 4 disulfides** and **100 % enzymatic activity** is recovered.

**Significance — "Primary Structure Dictates Tertiary Structure" (1 mark):**
The fact that all the information required for refolding is contained within the linear amino acid sequence (i.e., the primary structure) shows that **higher-order structure is encoded in the primary structure**. The protein finds its native state because it is the **thermodynamic minimum** (lowest Gibbs free energy). Anfinsen was awarded the **1972 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** for this work.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC (KTU 2024 Examiner's Report)**
> 
> 1. **Calling disulfide bonds "secondary structure stabilizers":** They are NOT. Disulfide bonds form between two cysteine side chains that are *far apart in sequence* but *close in 3D space*. They are classified as **tertiary-structure (or quaternary-structure) stabilizers**. (−2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Confusing the α-helix direction:** L-amino acids (which are biologically standard) form **right-handed** α-helices. Drawing a left-handed helix without justification costs 1 mark. If you must draw a left-handed helix, label it clearly and note it is a hypothetical case for D-amino acids.
> 
> 3. **Wrong hydrogen-bond pattern in α-helix:** It is **i → i+4** (not i → i+3, not i+5). Memorize this. (−1 mark)
> 
> 4. **Stating "hydrogen bonds are weak" without quantification:** Always cite the **4 – 20 kJ mol⁻¹** range. Vague statements lose 0.5 – 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Drawing peptide bond formation with an arrow that suggests addition, not condensation:** The reaction must be shown as a **dehydration synthesis** with **H₂O released**. Forgetting the water molecule costs 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for last-night revision):**
> 
> - The **four levels** of protein structure are: $1^{\circ}$ (sequence), $2^{\circ}$ (local folding), $3^{\circ}$ (3D fold), $4^{\circ}$ (subunit assembly).
> - The **peptide bond** is **covalent**, has **77 % double-bond character** (bond length **1.32 Å**), and is **planar and rigid** (ω ≈ 180°, *trans*).
> - An **n-residue polypeptide** has exactly **n − 1 peptide bonds** and **n − 1 water molecules** released.
> - The **α-helix** has **3.6 residues per turn**, pitch **5.4 Å**, rise per residue **1.5 Å**, and is stabilized by **i → i+4 hydrogen bonds**.
> - The **β-pleated sheet** has a rise per residue of **3.4 Å** and a repeat of **7.0 Å**, with H-bonds **between** strands (not along them).
> - **Tertiary structure** is stabilized by (ranked by bond energy): **disulfide > ionic > H-bond > hydrophobic > van der Waals**.
> - **Hydrophobic interactions** are the **dominant overall force** in tertiary folding because there are many of them and they are entropically driven (water release).
> - **Disulfide bridges** are covalent cross-links between two cysteine –SH groups, classified as **tertiary/quaternary** (NOT secondary) stabilizers.
> - **Quaternary structure** is found only in **multi-subunit** proteins; examples are **haemoglobin (α₂β₂)**, **DNA polymerase**, and **immunoglobulin G (2H + 2L)**.
> - **Denaturation** destroys $2^{\circ}, 3^{\circ}, 4^{\circ}$ but preserves $1^{\circ}$. Common denaturants: **heat, pH, urea, guanidinium chloride, SDS, β-mercaptoethanol, heavy metals**.
> - The **Anfinsen experiment (1972 Nobel Prize)** on RNase A proved that the **primary structure carries all the information** required for correct refolding.
> - **Insulin** has 51 residues in 2 chains (A: 21, B: 30), connected by **3 disulfide bridges** (1 intra A, 2 inter A–B).
> - **Sickle-cell anaemia** results from a single Glu → Val substitution at position 6 of β-globin — a powerful example of *primary structure dictating function*.
> - For a polypeptide, $M_{\text{protein, approx}} = 92n + 18$ Da (using $M_{\text{residue}} \approx 110$ Da and water loss of 18 Da per peptide bond).
> - The peptide unit is **planar**; only the **φ (phi, around N–Cα)** and **ψ (psi, around Cα–C)** dihedral angles are freely rotatable. The **Ramachandran plot** is the visualization tool for allowed φ, ψ combinations (high-yield for KTU viva).
> - **Proline** is a "helix breaker" because its side chain forms a ring with the backbone N, eliminating the N–H needed for H-bonding.
> - **Glycine** is the most conformationally flexible residue (no side chain), so it is **rarely found in α-helices** but common in **β-turns**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nucleic Acids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Nucleic Acids # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Nucleic Acids ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nucleic Acids** are high-molecular-weight, long-chain biopolymers composed of repeating monomeric units called **nucleotides**. Each nucleotide consists of three covalently linked components: (1) a nitrogenous heterocyclic **base** (purine or pyrimidine), (2) a pentose sugar (either **2-deoxyribose** in DNA or **ribose** in RNA), and (3) one or more **phosphate groups**. The two principal types are **Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA)** — the genetic archive of the cell — and **Ribonucleic Acid (RNA)** — the functional executor of genetic information, mediating transcription, translation, and gene regulation. In polymer chemistry terms, nucleic acids belong to the class of **polynucleotides**, where the phosphodiester linkages between the 3′-OH of one sugar and the 5′-phosphate of the next generate a directional **5′ → 3′** backbone. The structural code, heredity, and protein-synthesis instructions of all living organisms are encoded in the **linear sequence of bases** along this backbone. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Library of Cookbooks** > Imagine a vast central library (the cell nucleus in eukaryotes, the cytoplasm in prokaryotes) that contains thousands of recipe books. The **books are DNA** — the master archive of instructions. The recipes are **genes**. When the kitchen needs a specific dish (a protein), it does not take the original book out. Instead, it **photocopies the required page** to a working slip of paper — that is **messenger RNA (mRNA)**. The chef in the kitchen reads the mRNA and, with the help of **transfer RNA (tRNA)** that brings in the right amino-acid "ingredients," assembles the protein step by step. The **ribosome (rRNA + proteins)** is the kitchen itself, the assembly line. Phosphates are like the glue that holds each page together; sugars are the paper; and the bases (A, T/U, C, G) are the *letters* of the language of life. ## 1.3 Key Components in Brief | Component | Class | Examples | Function | |---|---|---|---| | Nitrogenous Bases | Purines (two-ring) | **Adenine (A), Guanine (G)** | Encode genetic information | | Nitrogenous Bases | Pyrimidines (one-ring) | **Cytosine (C), Thymine (T), Uracil (U)** | Encode genetic information | | Pentose Sugar | Deoxyribose | Found in DNA | Provides backbone scaffold (no 2′-OH) | | Pentose Sugar | Ribose | Found in RNA | Provides backbone scaffold (has 2′-OH) | | Phosphate Group | Inorganic | HPO₄²⁻ / PO₄³⁻ | Forms phosphodiester bonds; imparts negative charge | ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants - **Average molecular weight of a base pair (bp) in DNA:** ≈ **660 Da (g/mol)** - **Distance per base pair along helix axis (B-DNA):** ≈ **0.34 nm (3.4 Å)** - **Helical pitch (B-DNA):** ≈ **3.4 nm per 10 bp turn** - **Helix diameter (B-DNA):** ≈ **2.0 nm** - **Chargaff's Rule:** In double-stranded DNA, %A = %T and %G = %C (the **molar ratio** $\frac{A+T}{G+C}$ varies with species). - **Genetic code:** **64 codons** (43 = 64) — 61 code for amino acids, 3 are stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Building Blocks: From Bases to Polynucleotides The hierarchy of nucleic-acid structure can be presented in four logical layers: ### Layer 1 — Nitrogenous Bases - **Purines (A, G):** Two fused rings — a six-membered pyrimidine ring fused to a five-membered imidazole ring. - **Pyrimidines (C, T, U):** Single six-membered ring containing two nitrogen atoms. - Bases are **planar, aromatic**, and exhibit strong **UV absorbance at 260 nm** — a property used to quantify nucleic acids in solution. ### Layer 2 — Nucleosides A **nucleoside** is formed when a nitrogenous base is linked via a **β-N-glycosidic bond** to the **C1′ atom of the pentose sugar**. - Deoxyadenosine, Deoxyguanosine, Deoxycytidine, Deoxythymidine (DNA) - Adenosine, Guanosine, Cytidine, Uridine (RNA) > [!IMPORTANT] > Note that RNA contains **uracil (U)** in place of thymine (T). Uracil lacks the 5-methyl group present in thymine. ### Layer 3 — Nucleotides A **nucleotide** is a nucleoside with one or more phosphate groups esterified to the **5′-OH (and sometimes 3′-OH)** of the sugar. Examples: **adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP)**, **deoxyadenosine 5′-monophosphate (dAMP)**. ATP is the universal energy currency of the cell, with a standard free energy of hydrolysis $\Delta G^{\circ \prime} \approx -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (-7.3 kcal/mol). ### Layer 4 — Polynucleotides Nucleotides are joined by **phosphodiester bonds** between the 3′-OH of one sugar and the 5′-phosphate of the next, creating a **directionally polarised backbone**. Conventionally written in the **5′ → 3′** direction. ## 2.2 DNA Structure: The Watson–Crick Double Helix ### Why and How - **Why two strands?** Complementarity (A–T, G–C) provides a built-in template for accurate replication. - **How are they held together?** - **Hydrogen bonds** between complementary bases (A=T has **2 H-bonds**; G≡C has **3 H-bonds**). - **Base stacking** (van der Waals + hydrophobic interactions) between adjacent parallel bases — the major contributor to helix stability. ### Antiparallel Orientation The two strands run in **opposite 5′→3′ directions**. This is **mandatory** for proper Watson–Crick base pairing. ### Major and Minor Grooves The asymmetric surface of the helix creates a **major groove (~2.2 nm wide)** and a **minor groove (~1.2 nm wide)**. Regulatory proteins recognise specific base sequences by inserting α-helical "reading heads" into the **major groove** without needing to unwind the helix. ## 2.3 RNA Structure and Types RNA is typically **single-stranded**, but it folds back on itself to form local double-helical regions (hairpins, stem-loops). | Type | Full Name | Approx. % of cellular RNA | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **mRNA** | Messenger RNA | ~3–5% | Carries codon sequence from DNA to ribosome | | **tRNA** | Transfer RNA | ~10–15% | Delivers correct amino acid to the growing peptide | | **rRNA** | Ribosomal RNA | ~80% | Catalytic + structural core of ribosome (peptidyl transferase is a rRNA ribozyme) | | **miRNA / siRNA** | micro / small interfering RNA | trace | Post-transcriptional gene silencing | ## 2.4 The Central Dogma and Information Flow > **DNA → RNA → Protein** The flow is: **replication (DNA→DNA)**, **transcription (DNA→RNA)**, **translation (RNA→Protein)**. Reverse transcription (RNA→DNA) occurs in retroviruses via the enzyme **reverse transcriptase**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Equations and Quantitative Relations for Nucleic Acids** | # | Equation / Rule | Meaning | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1\, A_{260} = 50\,\mu g/mL$ (dsDNA) | Absorbance–concentration for dsDNA | Beer–Lambert Law basis | | 2 | $1\, A_{260} = 40\,\mu g/mL$ (RNA) | Absorbance–concentration for ssRNA | — | | 3 | $1\, A_{260} = 33\,\mu g/mL$ (ssDNA) | Absorbance–concentration for ssDNA | — | | 4 | $A_{260}/A_{280} \approx 1.8$ (pure DNA) | Purity index (protein contamination → lower) | Ratio < 1.8 = contamination | | 5 | $A_{260}/A_{280} \approx 2.0$ (pure RNA) | Purity index for RNA | — | | 6 | $\%\ GC = \dfrac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$ | GC content of a DNA sample | Dimensionless (%) | | 7 | $T_m = 69.3 + 0.41 \times (\%\ GC)$ | Wallace–Itakura melting temperature (Marmur–Schildkraut–Doty) for long DNA | °C, valid for %GC = 30–75 | | 8 | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\,\log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41\,\%\ GC - 600/N$ | Nearest-neighbour / modified formula for **oligonucleotides** | °C | | 9 | $M_{bp} \approx 660\,\text{Da/bp}$ (dsDNA) | Average molecular weight per base pair | g/mol | | 10 | $N_{bp} = \dfrac{\text{mass (g)}}{660\,\text{g/mol}} \times N_A$ | Number of base pairs from mass | $N_A = 6.022\times10^{23}$ | | 11 | $\Delta G^{\circ \prime}_{\text{ATP hydrolysis}} \approx -30.5\,\text{kJ/mol}$ | Standard free energy of ATP hydrolysis | kJ/mol | | 12 | Codons: $4^3 = 64$ | Total number of possible codons | Degenerate genetic code | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Forensic DNA fingerprinting (STR analysis)** uses the Chargaff and melting relations for primer design. - **Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)** exploits the **nearest-neighbour $T_m$ formula** to design primers with matched melting temperatures. - **DNA nanotechnology (DNA origami)** uses base-pairing predictability to build 2-D and 3-D nanostructures. - **Antisense oligonucleotides and mRNA vaccines** (e.g., COVID-19 vaccines) are designed using codon-optimisation principles and base-stacking energetics. - **Biosensors** functionalised with ssDNA probes exploit the **260 nm absorbance** or fluorescent intercalators (ethidium bromide, SYBR Green) for pathogen detection. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From a Single Base to a Polynucleotide ### Step 1 — A free nitrogenous base. The base (e.g., adenine) exists as a planar aromatic heterocycle with H-bond donors/acceptors on its edges. ### Step 2 — Formation of a nucleoside (N-glycosidic bond). Condensation between the **C1′-OH of the pentose** and the **N9 (purine) or N1 (pyrimidine)** of the base eliminates water. $$ \text{Base} - \text{NH} + \text{HO} - \text{C1}'_{\text{sugar}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Base} - \text{N} - \text{C1}'_{\text{sugar}} + H_2O $$ This is a **β-N-glycosidic bond** (always β, never α, in natural nucleic acids). ### Step 3 — Phosphorylation gives a nucleotide. A nucleoside kinase transfers the γ-phosphate of ATP to the 5′-OH of the nucleoside. $$ \text{Nucleoside} + \text{ATP} \;\xrightarrow{\text{nucleoside kinase}}\; \text{5'-Nucleotide} + \text{ADP} $$ ### Step 4 — Phosphodiester bond formation (polymerisation). DNA/RNA polymerases join the **3′-OH of the growing chain** to the **5′-α-phosphate of the incoming NTP (or dNTP)**, releasing pyrophosphate (PPᵢ). $$ \text{…3'-OH} + \text{5'-ppp-Nucleotide} \;\xrightarrow{\text{polymerase}}\; \text{…3'-O-PO}_3\text{-O-5'-Base} + PP_i $$ The hydrolysis of pyrophosphate ($PP_i \to 2P_i$) by **pyrophosphatase** makes the reaction essentially **irreversible**, with $\Delta G^{\circ \prime} \approx -25 \text{ kJ/mol}$ additional pull. ## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Base Pairs from Mass of dsDNA **Problem context:** A molecular biologist isolates 1 µg of a linear dsDNA fragment. How many base pairs does it contain? $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Convert mass to moles.} \quad & n = \dfrac{m}{M_{bp}} = \dfrac{1\times10^{-6}\,\text{g}}{660\,\text{g/mol}} \\ \text{Step 2 — Evaluate the division.} \quad & n = 1.515\times10^{-9}\,\text{mol} \\ \text{Step 3 — Convert moles to molecules (base pairs).} \quad & N_{bp} = n \times N_A = 1.515\times10^{-9}\times 6.022\times10^{23} \\ \text{Step 4 — Final answer.} \quad & N_{bp} \approx 9.12\times10^{14}\,\text{bp} \;(\text{for 1 µg dsDNA}) \end{aligned} $$ **Useful shortcut to memorise:** **1 µg of dsDNA ≈ 9.12 × 10¹⁴ bp ≈ 0.91 picomol × 10⁶ bp/picomol** (often quoted as 1 µg ≈ 1.52 pmol of 1-kb fragments). ## 3.3 Derivation: Melting Temperature of an Oligonucleotide Primer For a short DNA oligonucleotide, the **nearest-neighbour model** gives a more accurate melting temperature: $$ T_m \;=\; 81.5 \;+\; 16.6\,\log_{10}[Na^+] \;+\; 0.41\times(\%\ GC) \;-\; \dfrac{600}{N} $$ where $N$ is the number of bases in the primer. **Worked example:** A 20-mer primer with 50% GC content in 50 mM Na⁺. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Compute log term.} \quad & \log_{10}(0.050) = -1.301 \\ \text{Step 2 — Multiply by 16.6.} \quad & 16.6\times(-1.301) = -21.60 \\ \text{Step 3 — Compute 0.41 × %GC.} \quad & 0.41 \times 50 = 20.5 \\ \text{Step 4 — Compute 600/N.} \quad & 600/20 = 30 \\ \text{Step 5 — Sum all terms.} \quad & T_m = 81.5 - 21.6 + 20.5 - 30 = 50.4\,^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ Hence the primer anneals optimally at ≈ **50 °C** in this buffer — a typical value for routine PCR. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DNA vs RNA > [!NOTE] > **Side-by-side comparison — high-yield for KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions** | Property | DNA | RNA | |---|---|---| | Pentose sugar | 2′-Deoxyribose | Ribose | | Bases present | A, T, C, G | A, U, C, G | | Strand(s) | Usually double-stranded (B-form) | Usually single-stranded | | Helix geometry | B-DNA: 10.5 bp/turn, 2.0 nm diameter | A-form-like duplexes in stem regions | | Stability | Highly stable (alkali-resistant) | Labile — 2′-OH enables alkaline hydrolysis | | Location (eukaryotes) | Nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts | Nucleus, cytoplasm, ribosome | | Function | Long-term genetic storage | Coding (mRNA), decoding (tRNA), catalysis (rRNA), regulation (miRNA) | | Base-pairing geometry | Watson–Crick; A=T, G≡C | A=U, G≡C; also wobble G–U pair in RNA | | Unique base | Thymine (5-methyluracil) | Uracil | | Typical UV absorbance | 260 nm (hyperchromic effect on melting) | 260 nm | ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Types of RNA | Feature | mRNA | tRNA | rRNA | |---|---|---|---| | Length | 500–5000+ nt | 73–93 nt | 120–4700 nt | | % of total RNA | 3–5% | 10–15% | ~80% | | Sedimentation coefficient | Variable (polycistronic in prokaryotes) | ~4 S | 5 S, 5.8 S, 18 S, 28 S | | Function | Template for protein synthesis | Amino-acid carrier (anticodon) | Catalytic & structural component of ribosome | | Special features | 5′-cap, 3′-poly-A tail (eukaryotes) | Cloverleaf secondary structure, CCA 3′-end | Peptidyl transferase activity is a ribozyme | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flow Diagram: The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["DNA GENOME"] --> nodeB["REPLICATION"] nodeB --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeC["TRANSCRIPTION"] nodeC --> nodeD["MRNA"] nodeD --> nodeE["TRANSLATION"] nodeE --> nodeF["PROTEIN"] nodeD --> nodeG["RNASE DEGRADATION"] nodeF --> nodeH["CELLULAR FUNCTION"] nodeD -. "REVERSE TRANSCRIPTION
retroviruses" .-> nodeA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the canonical Watson–Crick central dogma (DNA→RNA→Protein). The dashed arrow indicates **reverse transcription**, characteristic of retroviruses such as HIV, mediated by the enzyme **reverse transcriptase**. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Nucleic Acid Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["NITROGENOUS BASES
PURINES A G
PYRIMIDINES C T U"] --> nodeB["NUCLEOSIDE
BASE + PENTOSE SUGAR
BETA N GLYCOSIDIC BOND"] nodeB --> nodeC["NUCLEOTIDE
NUCLEOSIDE + PHOSPHATE
5 PRIME ESTER BOND"] nodeC --> nodeD["POLYNUCLEOTIDE
PHOSPHODIESTER BACKBONE
5 PRIME TO 3 PRIME"] nodeD --> nodeE["DNA DOUBLE HELIX
ANTIPARALLEL COMPLEMENTARY"] nodeD --> nodeF["RNA SINGLE STRAND
FOLDS INTO HAIRPINS"] nodeE --> nodeG["CHROMOSOME
SUPERCOILED WITH HISTONES"] nodeF --> nodeH["MRNA / TRNA / RRNA
FUNCTIONAL EXECUTION"] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Watson–Crick Base Pairing (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STRAND1["STRAND 1 5 PRIME TO 3 PRIME"] node1A["A"] --- node1T["T"] --- node1G["G"] --- node1C["C"] end subgraph STRAND2["STRAND 2 3 PRIME TO 5 PRIME ANTIPARALLEL"] node2T["T"] --- node2A["A"] --- node2C["C"] --- node2G["G"] end node1A -. "TWO H BONDS" .- node2T node1T -. "TWO H BONDS" .- node2A node1G -. "THREE H BONDS" .- node2C node1C -. "THREE H BONDS" .- node2G ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** A–T pairs have **two** hydrogen bonds; G–C pairs have **three**. Hence GC-rich regions are more thermally stable and harder to denature. ## 4.4 Schematic: Cloverleaf Secondary Structure of tRNA ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["ACCEPTOR STEM
5 PRIME AND 3 PRIME ENDS
CCA 3 PRIME END
AMINO ACID ATTACHMENT"] --> nodeB["D ARM
DIHYDROURIDINE LOOP"] nodeB --> nodeC["ANTICODON ARM
THREE BASE ANTICODON
READS MRNA CODON"] nodeC --> nodeD["VARIABLE LOOP
SMALL EXTRA LOOP"] nodeD --> nodeE["T PSI C ARM
RIBOTHYMIDINE
PSEUDOURIDINE LOOP"] nodeE --> nodeA nodeC -. "PAIRS WITH
MRNA CODON" .- nodeF["MRNA CODON
THREE BASES"] ``` ## 4.5 Functional Architecture of Protein Synthesis (Transcription + Translation) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["DNA TEMPLATE STRAND"] --> nodeB["RNA POLYMERASE
TRANSCRIPTION"] nodeB --> nodeC["MRNA TRANSCRIPT
5 PRIME CAP AND POLY A TAIL"] nodeC --> nodeD["RIBOSOME SMALL SUBUNIT
BINDS MRNA"] nodeD --> nodeE["INITIATION COMPLEX
START CODON AUG
MET TRNA"] nodeE --> nodeF["ELONGATION
TRNA DELIVERY
PEPTIDE BOND FORMATION"] nodeF --> nodeG["TRANSLOCATION
RIBOSOME MOVES THREE NUCLEOTIDES"] nodeG --> nodeH["STOP CODON REACHED
UAA UAG UGA"] nodeH --> nodeI["RELEASE FACTORS BIND
POLYPEPTIDE RELEASED"] nodeI --> nodeJ["FOLDED FUNCTIONAL PROTEIN"] nodeK["AMINO ACYL TRNA SYNTHETASE"] --> nodeF nodeL["FREE AMINO ACIDS"] --> nodeK ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the following terms: (a) Nucleotide, (b) Phosphodiester bond, (c) Chargaff's rule.** **Model Answer:** (a) **Nucleotide:** The monomeric unit of a nucleic acid. It consists of three components covalently linked together — a nitrogenous base (purine or pyrimidine), a pentose sugar (ribose or 2-deoxyribose), and one or more phosphate groups attached to the 5′-carbon of the sugar. Example: Adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP). (b) **Phosphodiester bond:** The covalent linkage that joins adjacent nucleotides in a polynucleotide chain. It is formed between the **3′-hydroxyl group of the sugar of one nucleotide** and the **5′-phosphate group of the next nucleotide**, with the elimination of water (or pyrophosphate during polymerisation). The resulting backbone carries a negative charge at physiological pH. (c) **Chargaff's rule:** In double-stranded DNA, the **molar amount of adenine equals that of thymine (%A = %T)** and the **molar amount of guanine equals that of cytosine (%G = %C)**. Consequently, the total purines equal total pyrimidines: **%A + %G = %T + %C**. This rule provided the chemical basis for Watson–Crick base pairing. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between DNA and RNA in terms of (a) sugar, (b) bases, (c) strand nature.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | DNA | RNA | |---|---|---| | (a) Sugar | 2′-Deoxyribose (no 2′-OH) | Ribose (has 2′-OH) | | (b) Bases | A, T, C, G (contains **thymine**) | A, U, C, G (contains **uracil** in place of T) | | (c) Strand nature | Usually **double-stranded**, antiparallel, B-form helix | Usually **single-stranded**, folds into local hairpins and stem-loops | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) Describe the Watson–Crick model of the DNA double helix. Include the dimensions, base-pairing rules, and directionality of the strands. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Historical background.** Building on Chargaff's rules, X-ray diffraction data of Rosalind Franklin, and earlier work by Pauling, James Watson and Francis Crick proposed in 1953 a double-helical model for DNA, published in *Nature*. 2. **Two antiparallel strands.** DNA is composed of two polynucleotide chains wound around a common axis in a **right-handed double helix**. The two strands run in **opposite 5′→3′ directions** (antiparallel). 3. **Sugar-phosphate backbone.** Each strand's backbone is the repeating unit — sugar-phosphate-sugar-phosphate — joined by 3′→5′ phosphodiester bonds. The bases project **inward**, perpendicular to the helical axis. 4. **Specific base pairing.** A purine on one strand always pairs with a pyrimidine on the other: **Adenine (A) pairs with Thymine (T) via two hydrogen bonds**; **Guanine (G) pairs with Cytosine (C) via three hydrogen bonds**. This explains Chargaff's ratios. 5. **Helical parameters of B-DNA.** - **Diameter:** ≈ 2.0 nm - **Rise per base pair:** 0.34 nm (3.4 Å) - **Pitch per turn:** 3.4 nm (i.e., 10 base pairs per turn) - **Major groove:** 2.2 nm; **Minor groove:** 1.2 nm 6. **Stabilising forces.** Hydrogen bonding between complementary bases + **base-stacking** (van der Waals and hydrophobic interactions) between adjacent parallel aromatic rings. Stacking contributes ≈ 60–80% of the total stabilisation energy. 7. **Biological significance.** The complementary, antiparallel design enables **semi-conservative replication**: each strand serves as a template for synthesising a new complementary strand. **(b) Explain the structure of tRNA. How does it participate in protein synthesis? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Definition.** Transfer RNA (tRNA) is a small adaptor RNA, **73–93 nucleotides** long, that physically links the **mRNA codon** to the corresponding **amino acid** during translation. 2. **Cloverleaf secondary structure.** tRNA folds into a characteristic **cloverleaf** with four arms: - **Acceptor stem** (7 bp) — carries the **3′-CCA** end to which the cognate amino acid is esterified. - **D arm** — contains dihydrouridine; binds the aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase. - **Anticodon arm** — carries the **3-base anticodon** (positions 34–36) that base-pairs with the mRNA codon in an **antiparallel** manner. - **TΨC arm** — contains the conserved sequence **T-Ψ-C** (ribothymidine–pseudouridine–cytidine); binds to the ribosome. 3. **L-shaped tertiary structure.** The cloverleaf folds further into a compact **L-shape**, with the acceptor end and the anticodon loop at opposite tips, spaced ≈ 7 nm apart. 4. **Charging by aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase.** Each amino acid is covalently attached to the 3′-OH of the terminal adenosine of its tRNA in a two-step, ATP-dependent reaction. There are **20 different synthetases** (one per amino acid), each recognising its specific tRNA isoacceptors. 5. **Role in translation.** - During **initiation**, the initiator tRNA$^fMet$ (prokaryotes) or tRNA$^{Met}$ (eukaryotes) carrying methionine binds the **P-site** of the ribosome at the **AUG start codon**. - During **elongation**, the ribosome reads successive codons. An aminoacyl-tRNA whose anticodon matches the mRNA codon (via Watson–Crick pairing, with allowed **wobble** at the third position) enters the **A-site**. - **Peptidyl transferase** (a ribozyme activity of 23S/28S rRNA) catalyses peptide-bond formation; the ribosome then **translocates** by one codon. - When a **stop codon (UAA, UAG, UGA)** enters the A-site, **release factors** trigger hydrolysis of the peptidyl-tRNA, releasing the polypeptide. 6. **Wobble hypothesis (Crick).** The 5′-anticodon base allows non-standard pairing (e.g., G–U), so a single tRNA can read multiple codons differing only in the third (wobble) position. This explains codon degeneracy. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a) Describe in detail the different types of RNA and their functions. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Messenger RNA (mRNA).** - Carries the **genetic code** from DNA in the nucleus to the ribosome in the cytoplasm. - In eukaryotes, mRNA is **monocistronic** and undergoes **5′-capping, 3′-polyadenylation, and splicing** of introns. - In prokaryotes, mRNA is often **polycistronic** and is translated **co-transcriptionally**. - Functions as the **template** whose codon sequence dictates amino-acid order. 2. **Transfer RNA (tRNA).** - Adaptor molecule: **73–93 nt**, cloverleaf + L-shaped. - Carries activated amino acids to the ribosome. - The **anticodon** decodes the mRNA codon; the **3′-CCA end** carries the amino acid. 3. **Ribosomal RNA (rRNA).** - Structural and catalytic core of the ribosome. - Prokaryotic ribosome: 30S + 50S = 70S, containing 16S, 23S, 5S rRNAs. - Eukaryotic ribosome: 40S + 60S = 80S, containing 18S, 28S, 5.8S, 5S rRNAs. - **23S rRNA** (prokaryotes) / **28S rRNA** (eukaryotes) — the **peptidyl transferase** centre — is a **ribozyme**, demonstrating that RNA can catalyse chemical reactions (Tom Cech & Sidney Altman, Nobel 1989). 4. **Small nuclear RNA (snRNA).** Forms the spliceosome that excises introns from pre-mRNA. 5. **MicroRNA (miRNA) and small interfering RNA (siRNA).** 20–25 nt, mediate **RNA interference (RNAi)** — post-transcriptional gene silencing by guiding the RISC complex to complementary mRNA targets. 6. **Long non-coding RNA (lncRNA).** > 200 nt; involved in chromatin remodelling, X-inactivation (Xist), and gene regulation. 7. **Ribozymes and riboswitches.** Catalytic RNAs and regulatory RNA elements in mRNA 5′-UTRs that bind metabolites and control gene expression. **(b) Explain the mechanism of DNA replication in prokaryotes. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Semi-conservative replication.** Each daughter DNA molecule contains **one parental strand and one newly synthesised strand**. Demonstrated by the Meselson–Stahl experiment (1958). 2. **Origin of replication (oriC).** The *E. coli* chromosome has a single, well-defined origin (~245 bp) recognised by the **DnaA** initiator protein. 3. **Enzymes and proteins at the replication fork.** - **Helicase (DnaB):** Unwinds the double helix using ATP; moves 5′→3′ on the lagging-strand template. - **Single-strand binding protein (SSB):** Stabilises unwound single strands, prevents re-annealing. - **Primase (DnaG):** Synthesises a short RNA primer (≈ 10 nt) complementary to each template. - **DNA polymerase III:** Main replicative polymerase; processive, has 3′→5′ exonuclease proofreading; adds dNTPs in the 5′→3′ direction. - **DNA polymerase I:** Removes RNA primers with its 5′→3′ exonuclease activity and fills the gap with DNA. - **DNA ligase:** Seals the nicks between Okazaki fragments using NAD⁺ (bacteria) or ATP (eukaryotes) as cofactor. - **Topoisomerase (DNA gyrase):** Relieves positive supercoiling ahead of the fork by cutting and rejoining the DNA backbone. - **Sliding clamp (β-clamp) and clamp loader (γ-complex):** Confer high processivity on Pol III. 4. **Leading and lagging strands.** - **Leading strand** — synthesised **continuously** in the 5′→3′ direction towards the replication fork. - **Lagging strand** — synthesised **discontinuously** as short **Okazaki fragments** (≈ 1000–2000 nt in *E. coli*), each requiring its own RNA primer. 5. **Termination.** Occurs at the *Ter* region; **Tus protein** binds *Ter* sites and halts the replisome; the two daughter molecules are then separated by topoisomerase. 6. **Fidelity.** The intrinsic error rate of DNA Pol III is ≈ 10⁻⁷; combined with 3′→5′ proofreading and mismatch repair (MutS/MutL/MutH system), the final replication accuracy is ≈ 10⁻⁹ per base pair. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Chargaff's rule confusion:** Many students write "A = C, G = T". The correct pairing is **A = T** and **G = C** (purine with pyrimidine complement). > 2. **Directionality errors:** Always specify **5′→3′** for the new strand; **3′→5′** is the template direction. Saying "DNA polymerase synthesises 3′→5′" loses marks. > 3. **Uracil vs Thymine:** Forgetting that RNA has **U** instead of **T** is a common slip. Both form two H-bonds with adenine, but uracil lacks the 5-methyl group. > 4. **tRNA sequence length:** Writing "tRNA is 70–80 nt" is acceptable; writing "tRNA has 100 nucleotides" is wrong. > 5. **Melting temperature formula:** The Wallace–Itakura formula ($T_m = 4\,(G+C) + 2\,(A+T)$) is for **short oligos**; the longer $T_m = 69.3 + 0.41\,\%\!GC$ formula is for **long DNA**. Mixing them up loses marks. > 6. **Misuse of "semiconservative":** In DNA replication, **each** daughter molecule is semiconservative — the description refers to the **distribution** of parental strands, not "partial conservation." > 7. **Confusing rRNA and ribosome:** rRNA is the **RNA component** of the ribosome; the ribosome is the **ribonucleoprotein particle (rRNA + proteins)**. Do not interchange the terms. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Nucleic Acids (Module 4)** - **Nucleic acid** = polymer of **nucleotides** (base + sugar + phosphate). DNA and RNA are the two types. - **Sugar rule:** DNA = **deoxyribose** (no 2′-OH); RNA = **ribose** (has 2′-OH) → RNA is alkali-labile. - **Base rule:** DNA = A, T, C, G; RNA = A, **U**, C, G. **T has 5-methyl group; U does not.** - **N-glycosidic bond** joins base to C1′ of sugar → nucleoside. Add phosphate to 5′-OH → nucleotide. **β configuration** is mandatory. - **Phosphodiester bond** joins 3′-OH of one sugar to 5′-phosphate of the next → **directional 5′→3′ backbone**. - **Watson–Crick base pairing:** A=T (2 H-bonds), G≡C (3 H-bonds). **Antiparallel** strands. - **B-DNA dimensions:** 2.0 nm diameter, 0.34 nm rise/bp, 10.5 bp/turn, 3.4 nm pitch. **Major groove = 2.2 nm; minor groove = 1.2 nm.** - **Chargaff's rule:** %A = %T, %G = %C. **Total purines = total pyrimidines.** - **Three RNA types:** mRNA (template), tRNA (adaptor with **anticodon** and 3′-CCA amino-acid site), rRNA (catalytic + structural core; **peptidyl transferase is a ribozyme**). - **Genetic code:** 4³ = **64 codons**; 61 sense + 3 stop (UAA, UAG, UGA); **degenerate** (multiple codons per amino acid); **universal** (almost). - **Central dogma:** DNA → RNA → Protein. Retroviruses perform **reverse transcription**. - **Semi-conservative replication** (Meselson–Stahl). Key enzymes: **helicase, primase, Pol III, Pol I, ligase, topoisomerase, SSB**. **Okazaki fragments** on the lagging strand. - **Transcription** by **RNA polymerase** (5′→3′). **Translation** by ribosome (A, P, E sites). **Wobble hypothesis** explains codon degeneracy. - **A₂₆₀ quantification:** 1 A₂₆₀ = 50 µg/mL (dsDNA), 40 µg/mL (RNA), 33 µg/mL (ssDNA). **A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ≈ 1.8** for pure DNA, **≈ 2.0** for pure RNA. - **$T_m$ rules:** higher %GC → higher $T_m$ (more H-bonds + stronger stacking). Long DNA: $T_m = 69.3 + 0.41\,\%\!GC$ (°C). Short oligo: $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log[Na^+] + 0.41\,\%\!GC - 600/N$. - **Base pair mass:** ≈ **660 Da/bp**. **1 µg dsDNA ≈ 9.12 × 10¹⁴ bp** ≈ 1.52 pmol per 1-kb fragment. - **ATP:** energy currency; $\Delta G^{\circ \prime}_{\text{hydrolysis}} \approx -30.5$ kJ/mol. dNTPs/NTPs are substrates for polymerases; PPᵢ hydrolysis drives polymerisation forward. - **Denaturation (melting):** heat, alkali (RNA only), formamide, urea disrupt H-bonds; **renaturation (annealing)** restores dsDNA when conditions are slowly returned. - **Forensic / medical relevance:** PCR, DNA fingerprinting, antisense therapy, mRNA vaccines, gene editing (CRISPR-Cas9 uses guide RNA base-pairing with target DNA). **Top 10 mnemonics for last-minute KTU revision:** 1. **P**yrimidines have **1** ring — **C, T, U** (**C**ut **T**he **U**nique). 2. **P**urines have **2** rings — **A, G** (**A**nd **G**u). 3. **A = T** (A**p**ple on **T**ree), **G ≡ C** (3 strong bonds, G**C** rich = stable). 4. **DNA** = **D**ouble, **D**efinitive, **D**urable. **RNA** = **R**eliable, **R**eady, **R**elatively **R**eplaceable. 5. **5′→3′** is the **direction of synthesis** for all polymerases. 6. **mRNA** = **m**essage. **tRNA** = **t**ransfer. **rRNA** = **r**ibosome's **r**NA. 7. **AUG** = **A**ll codons **U**se **G**uanine (start methionine). 8. **Stop codons:** **U** **A**re **A**ll **G**ood **A**t **G**iving up = **UAA, UAG, UGA** (oops, mnemonics fail here — memorise directly!). 9. **T = 4 + 2 (Wallace)** and **T = 69.3 + 0.41·%GC (Marmur)** for long DNA. 10. **A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ = 1.8 (DNA) or 2.0 (RNA)** — **"DNA = 1.8 = double"** (close to 2).

topics.title: Chemistry of Polysaccharides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemistry of Polysaccharides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polysaccharides** are high-molecular-weight carbohydrate biopolymers composed of long linear or branched chains of monosaccharide units (simple sugars) joined together by **glycosidic bonds** formed through condensation (dehydration) reactions. They belong to the class of natural polymers and serve critical structural, storage, and biological recognition functions in living organisms.

The general empirical formula of polysaccharides can be represented as:

$$\left[ C_{x}(H_{2}O)_{y} \right]_{n}$$

where $n$ represents the degree of polymerization (DP), and the values of $x$ and $y$ depend on the type of monosaccharide repeating unit. For a hexose-based polysaccharide, $x = 6$ and $y = 5$, giving an empirical formula of $\left[ C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} \right]_{n}$, which approximates a dehydrated form of the parent sugar.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PBPOT304 specifically emphasizes the *chemistry* of polysaccharides — meaning the structure of monomer units, the **glycosidic linkage chemistry** (α vs β), the hydrolysis mechanism, derivatization reactions, and the relationship between stereochemistry and biological function.

## 1.2 Classification of Polysaccharides

Polysaccharides are broadly classified based on two principal criteria:

**(a) Based on the type of monosaccharide units produced on hydrolysis:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Homopolysaccharides (Homoglycans)** | Composed of only one type of monosaccharide repeating unit | Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen, Chitin |
| **Heteropolysaccharides (Heteroglycans)** | Composed of two or more different types of monosaccharide units | Hyaluronic acid, Heparin, Chondroitin sulfate |

**(b) Based on biological function:**

| Type | Function | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Storage Polysaccharides** | Energy storage | Starch (plants), Glycogen (animals) |
| **Structural Polysaccharides** | Mechanical support | Cellulose, Chitin, Pectin |
| **Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs)** | Extracellular matrix, lubrication | Hyaluronic acid, Heparin |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lego Brick Tower**
> Imagine you have only **one type of Lego brick** (a single monosaccharide like glucose). You can stack them in two different ways:
> - **Stacking every brick at the same angle** → produces a **helical, easily broken tower** (this is **amylose/starch**, used for quick energy).
> - **Stacking them straight and alternating sides** → produces a **rigid, rope-like tower** that is hard to break (this is **cellulose**, used for plant cell walls).
>
> Now imagine using **two different coloured bricks** (e.g., glucuronic acid + glucosamine) → that is a **heteropolysaccharide** like hyaluronic acid, used as a shock-absorber in our joints.
>
> The *way* you connect the bricks (α-1,4 or β-1,4 linkage) is just as important as the bricks themselves — the same glucose monomer gives completely different biological materials!

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Molar mass range of polysaccharides:** $\mathbf{10^{4} \text{ to } 10^{8} \text{ g/mol}}$
- **Standard monosaccharide building block:** D-Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$, MW = **180.16 g/mol**)
- **Energy released per gram upon complete oxidation:** ~**17 kJ/g** (4 kcal/g)
- **Glycosidic bond energy:** approximately **90–100 kJ/mol**
- **Anomeric carbon:** the carbonyl carbon (C1 in aldohexoses) that forms the glycosidic linkage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Monosaccharide Building Blocks — Stereochemistry Recap

A monosaccharide in its cyclic (pyranose/furanose) form has an **anomeric carbon (C1)** that can adopt either the **α-configuration** (–OH below the ring plane in Haworth projection) or **β-configuration** (–OH above the ring plane). This stereochemistry is the single most important factor determining polysaccharide properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutarotation:** In solution, α- and β-anomers interconvert through the open-chain form, reaching an equilibrium optical rotation. This is critical because it dictates the *type* of glycosidic bond a polysaccharide can form.

## 2.2 The Glycosidic Bond — Formation Mechanism

The glycosidic bond is formed between the **anomeric hydroxyl (C1–OH) of one sugar** and a **hydroxyl group (usually C2, C3, C4, or C6) of another sugar**, releasing one molecule of water. This is a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** and is essentially a hemiacetal + alcohol → acetal transformation.

**General mechanism:**

$$\text{Sugar}_1\text{–OH} + \text{HO–Sugar}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{condensation}} \text{Sugar}_1\text{–O–Sugar}_2 + H_2O$$

The oxygen bridge is called the **glycosidic oxygen**, and the bond is named by the configuration of the anomeric carbon (α or β) and the carbon positions involved (e.g., α-1,4; β-1,4; α-1,6).

## 2.3 Why Different Glycosidic Bonds Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The α vs β Linkage — A Tale of Two Polymers**
> Both starch and cellulose are polymers of **D-glucose**, yet they have radically different properties.
>
> - **α-1,4 glycosidic bond** (Starch, Glycogen): The bond is *angled*, producing a **helical secondary structure** that is easily hydrolysed by human enzymes (amylases).
> - **β-1,4 glycosidic bond** (Cellulose): The bond is *straight*, producing **linear, ribbon-like chains** that pack tightly via hydrogen bonding into insoluble microfibrils. Humans **lack cellulase**, so cellulose is dietary fibre.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic: From Monosaccharide to Polysaccharide

1. **Activation of the anomeric carbon** — In nature, the sugar is first phosphorylated (e.g., glucose → glucose-1-phosphate → UDP-glucose) for high-energy glycosyl transfer.
2. **Nucleophilic attack** — The hydroxyl oxygen of the incoming sugar attacks the anomeric carbon, displacing the leaving group (UDP).
3. **Glycosidic bond formation** — A new C–O–C ether linkage is formed with inversion (or retention) of configuration.
4. **Chain elongation** — The process repeats, yielding a polysaccharide with **DP (degree of polymerization)** ranging from 50 to >100,000.
5. **Branching (optional)** — A separate transferase enzyme (e.g., branching enzyme in glycogen synthesis) introduces α-1,6 branches.

## 2.5 Reactions of Polysaccharides

Polysaccharides undergo several important chemical reactions, all of which are high-yield KTU topics:

**(a) Hydrolysis (Acid or Enzyme-catalysed):**

$$\left[ C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} \right]_{n} + (n-1) H_{2}O \xrightarrow{H^{+} \text{ or enzyme}} n\, C_{6}H_{12}O_{6}$$

This breaks glycosidic bonds, producing **oligosaccharides** (DP 2–10) and ultimately **monosaccharides**.

**(b) Esterification:**

$$Polysaccharide\text{–OH} + R\text{COCl} \longrightarrow Polysaccharide\text{–O–COR} + HCl$$

Example: Formation of **cellulose acetate** (used in films, fibres) and **cellulose nitrate** (gun-cotton, early plastics).

**(c) Oxidation:**

Selective oxidation of C6 primary hydroxyl → **carboxyl group**, producing **oxidized cellulose** or **oxidized starch** (used as hemostatic gauze in surgery).

**(d) Etherification:**

$$Polysaccharide\text{–OH} + CH_{3}Cl \xrightarrow{NaOH}} Polysaccharide\text{–O–CH}_{3}$$

Example: **Methylcellulose**, **carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)** — a thickener in pharmaceuticals.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Empirical formula of hexose polysaccharide | $\left[ C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} \right]_{n}$ | Dehydrated form of glucose repeating unit |
| Hydrolysis stoichiometry | $\left[ C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} \right]_{n} + (n-1)H_{2}O \rightarrow n\,C_{6}H_{12}O_{6}$ | Complete hydrolysis yields glucose |
| Molar mass | $M_{polysaccharide} = n \times M_{monomer unit} - (n-1) \times 18$ | $M$ in g/mol, $n$ = DP |
| Number-average DP | $\overline{DP}_{n} = \dfrac{\sum N_{i}}{\sum (N_{i}/DP_{i})}$ | Used in polymer characterization |
| Weight-average DP | $\overline{DP}_{w} = \dfrac{\sum N_{i}DP_{i}^{2}}{\sum N_{i}DP_{i}}$ | Sensitive to high-MW chains |
| Polydispersity index | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{DP}_{w}}{\overline{DP}_{n}}$ | PDI = 1 for monodisperse, >1 for natural polymers |
| Anomeric carbon index | α-1,4 / β-1,4 / α-1,6 | Determines biological function |
| Glycosidic bond energy | ~**90–100 kJ/mol** | Energy required to hydrolyse one bond |
| Specific rotation (optical activity) | $[\alpha] = \dfrac{\alpha_{obs}}{c \cdot l}$ | $c$ in g/mL, $l$ in dm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulae are routinely used in characterizing biopolymer samples in pharmaceutical industries, in determining the shelf life of polysaccharide-based drug delivery systems, and in food-industry quality control of starch and pectin.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Biomedical Engineering:** Polysaccharides are used as scaffolds in **tissue engineering** (e.g., chitosan for wound dressings), **drug delivery matrices** (e.g., dextran, alginate hydrogels), and **hemostatic agents** (oxidized cellulose).
- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Modified starch and cellulose derivatives are used as **tablet binders, disintegrants, and controlled-release matrices**.
- **Food Engineering:** Pectin (β-1,4 galacturonic acid) and alginate are used as **gelling and thickening agents**.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Chitosan and modified cellulose are used as **biosorbents** for heavy metal removal from wastewater.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molar Mass of a Polysaccharide from Degree of Polymerization

**Problem Statement:** A starch sample has a degree of polymerization $n = 1000$. Compute the molar mass of the polysaccharide, given that the monomer unit is $C_6H_{10}O_5$.

**Given:**
- Number of glucose units, $n = 1000$
- Molar mass of one anhydroglucose unit, $M_{0} = 162 \text{ g/mol}$
- Molar mass of water, $M_{w} = 18 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 1:** Recognize that when $n$ glucose molecules condense to form a polysaccharide, $(n - 1)$ water molecules are eliminated. The general formula is:

$$M_{polysaccharide} = n \cdot M_{0} - (n - 1) \cdot M_{w}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$M_{polysaccharide} = 1000 \times 162 - (1000 - 1) \times 18 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
n \cdot M_{0} &= 1000 \times 162 = 162{,}000 \text{ g/mol} \\
(n-1) \cdot M_{w} &= 999 \times 18 = 17{,}982 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$M_{polysaccharide} = 162{,}000 - 17{,}982 = 144{,}018 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 5:** Express in standard form:

$$\boxed{M_{polysaccharide} \approx 1.44 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each successive glycosidic bond formed releases exactly **one water molecule (18 g/mol)**. For large $n$, the approximation $M \approx n \times 144$ is used (since 162 − 18 = 144 g/mol per residue).

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydrolysis Kinetics of Starch

**Problem Statement:** Acid hydrolysis of starch is a first-order reaction with rate constant $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min}^{-1}$. Find the time required to hydrolyse 80% of the glycosidic bonds.

**First-order rate law:**

$$-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_{t} = C_{0} \cdot e^{-kt}$$

**Step 1:** For 80% hydrolysis, 20% of the bonds remain intact:

$$\dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{0}} = 0.20$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{0}}\right) = -kt$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the ratio:

$$\ln(0.20) = -(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot t$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $\ln(0.20) = -1.609$:

$$-1.609 = -(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot t$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t$:

$$t = \dfrac{1.609}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 643.6 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 644 \text{ min} \approx 10.7 \text{ hours}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In pharmaceutical tablet formulation using modified-release starch matrices, this first-order model is used to predict drug release profiles — the same kinetic framework, but applied to polymer erosion rather than direct hydrolysis.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Polysaccharides

| Feature | Starch (Amylose) | Starch (Amylopectin) | Glycogen | Cellulose | Chitin | Hyaluronic Acid |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monomer** | D-Glucose | D-Glucose | D-Glucose | D-Glucose | N-acetyl-D-glucosamine | D-Glucuronic acid + N-acetyl-D-glucosamine |
| **Linkage** | α-1,4 | α-1,4 + α-1,6 (branches) | α-1,4 + α-1,6 (more branches) | β-1,4 | β-1,4 | β-1,3 + β-1,4 |
| **Structure** | Linear | Branched | Highly branched | Linear, ribbon | Linear, ribbon | Linear, coiled |
| **Function** | Plant energy storage | Plant energy storage | Animal energy storage | Plant cell wall | Exoskeletons, fungi | ECM, joint lubrication |
| **Solubility in water** | Soluble (with hot water) | Insoluble | Soluble (colloidal) | Insoluble | Insoluble | Soluble |
| **Biomedical Use** | Drug excipient | Drug excipient | Plasma volume expander | Dialysis membrane, hemostat | Wound healing, sutures | Arthritis treatment, eye surgery |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Calculating DP from Molar Mass

**Given:** A cellulose sample has $M = 486{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$. Find its degree of polymerization. ($M_{0}$ for anhydroglucose = 162 g/mol)

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$n = \dfrac{M_{polysaccharide}}{M_{0} - M_{w}} = \dfrac{486{,}000}{162 - 18} = \dfrac{486{,}000}{144}$$

**Step 2:** Compute:

$$n = 3375$$

**Step 3:** Interpret: Each cellulose chain contains ~**3,375 glucose units** joined by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds.

$$\boxed{n \approx 3375}$$

## 3.5 Chemical Reaction Examples (Balanced)

**Reaction 1 — Complete hydrolysis of cellulose:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} \right]_{n} + (n-1)H_{2}O &\xrightarrow{H^{+},\,\Delta} n\,C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \\
\text{Cellulose} &\quad \text{D-Glucose}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reaction 2 — Acetylation of cellulose (formation of cellulose triacetate):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ C_{6}H_{7}O_{2}(OH)_{3} \right]_{n} + 3n\,(CH_{3}CO)_{2}O &\longrightarrow \left[ C_{6}H_{7}O_{2}(OCOCH_{3})_{3} \right]_{n} + 3n\,CH_{3}COOH \\
\text{Cellulose} &\quad \text{Acetic anhydride} \quad \text{Cellulose triacetate} \quad \text{Acetic acid}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reaction 3 — Nitration of cellulose:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ C_{6}H_{7}O_{2}(OH)_{3} \right]_{n} + 3n\,HNO_{3} &\xrightarrow{H_{2}SO_{4}} \left[ C_{6}H_{7}O_{2}(ONO_{2})_{3} \right]_{n} + 3n\,H_{2}O \\
\text{Cellulose} &\quad \text{Nitric acid} \quad \text{Cellulose trinitrate (gun cotton)}
\end{aligned}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define the term *glycosidic bond*. How does the α-1,4 glycosidic bond in starch differ from the β-1,4 bond in cellulose in terms of structure and biological function?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A *glycosidic bond* is the C–O–C ether linkage formed between the anomeric hydroxyl of one monosaccharide and a hydroxyl group of another, with elimination of water.
> - In **starch (amylose)**, the bond is **α-1,4**, producing a **helical** chain. This geometry is recognized and hydrolysed by human **amylases**, making starch digestible.
> - In **cellulose**, the bond is **β-1,4**, producing a **straight, ribbon-like** chain that hydrogen-bonds into rigid microfibrils. Humans **lack cellulase**, so cellulose is non-digestible dietary fibre.
> (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Differentiate between **homopolysaccharides** and **heteropolysaccharides** with two examples each.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Feature | Homopolysaccharides | Heteropolysaccharides |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Monomer types | Only **one** | **Two or more** |
> | Examples | **Starch** (glucose), **Cellulose** (glucose), **Glycogen** (glucose) | **Hyaluronic acid** (glucuronic acid + glucosamine), **Heparin** (glucuronic acid + glucosamine + sulfate) |
>
> (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style | 14 Marks]**
>
> ### **Question A**
>
> **(a)** Describe the chemistry of polysaccharides with emphasis on the structure of **starch (amylose and amylopectin)**. Discuss the role of α-1,4 and α-1,6 glycosidic bonds. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **acid hydrolysis** and **enzymatic hydrolysis** of cellulose. Why can humans not digest cellulose? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Starch Chemistry — Amylose:**
- Amylose is a **linear polymer** of D-glucose units joined by **α-1,4 glycosidic bonds**.
- Degree of polymerization ranges from **300 to 3000**.
- The α-1,4 bond angles every successive glucose unit, producing a **left-handed helix** (6 glucose residues per turn).
- Amylose is **water-soluble** (forms colloidal solution in hot water) and gives a **deep blue colour** with iodine (iodine fits inside the helix).

**Starch Chemistry — Amylopectin:**
- Amylopectin is a **branched polymer** of D-glucose with **α-1,4 linkages in the chain** and **α-1,6 linkages at branch points**.
- Branch points occur every **24–30 glucose residues**.
- Degree of polymerization: **2000 to 200,000**.
- Branches create a **tree-like or "cluster"** architecture, making amylopectin **insoluble in water** but **swellable**.
- Gives a **red-violet colour** with iodine.

**Role of α-1,4 and α-1,6 bonds:**
- **α-1,4** provides the linear backbone.
- **α-1,6** creates branches that:
  1. Increase solubility/dispersibility in water.
  2. Provide multiple chain ends for **rapid enzymatic attack** during digestion (glycogen uses this same strategy).
  3. Affect crystalline vs amorphous regions in the starch granule.

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Acid Hydrolysis of Cellulose:**
- Cellulose is treated with **dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl** at elevated temperature (100–200 °C) under pressure.
- Mechanism: Protonation of glycosidic oxygen → cleavage of C–O bond → carbocation at anomeric carbon → attack by water → free glucose.
- Reaction: $\left[ C_6H_{10}O_5 \right]_n + (n-1)H_2O \rightarrow n\,C_6H_{12}O_6$
- Produces **glucose**, used in **bioethanol production**.

**Enzymatic Hydrolysis:**
- Requires a **synergistic cocktail** of three cellulases:
  1. **Endoglucanase** — randomly cleaves internal β-1,4 bonds → cellodextrins.
  2. **Exoglucanase (cellobiohydrolase)** — cleaves cellobiose units from chain ends.
  3. **β-glucosidase** — hydrolyses cellobiose → two glucose molecules.

**Why Humans Cannot Digest Cellulose:**
- Humans (and other mammals) **lack the enzyme cellulase** that can cleave the **β-1,4 glycosidic bond**.
- The β-1,4 bond geometry is fundamentally different from α-1,4; the active site of human amylases is shaped to bind α-linkages only.
- Cellulose thus passes through the human gut as **dietary fibre**, adding bulk to faeces and promoting gut motility.
- Ruminants (cows, goats) digest cellulose because their **rumen microbiota** produce cellulase.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style | 14 Marks]**
>
> ### **Question B**
>
> **(a)** With suitable examples, describe the **classification of polysaccharides** based on composition and biological function. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write notes on: (i) **Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs)** with reference to hyaluronic acid and heparin; (ii) **Modified cellulose derivatives** used in pharmaceuticals. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Classification based on composition:**
- **Homopolysaccharides (Homoglycans):** Composed of a single type of monosaccharide.
  - *Examples:* Starch (glucose), Cellulose (glucose), Glycogen (glucose), Chitin (N-acetylglucosamine), Inulin (fructose).
- **Heteropolysaccharides (Heteroglycans):** Composed of two or more different monosaccharide units.
  - *Examples:* Hyaluronic acid, Heparin, Chondroitin sulfate, Agar, Pectin, Alginate.

**Classification based on biological function:**
- **Storage polysaccharides:** Energy reserve in plants (starch) and animals (glycogen).
- **Structural polysaccharides:** Provide mechanical strength — cellulose (plant cell wall), chitin (arthropod exoskeleton, fungal wall).
- **Mucopolysaccharides / GAGs:** Components of extracellular matrix, lubrication in joints, anticoagulation — e.g., hyaluronic acid, chondroitin sulfate, heparin.
- **Recognition / signalling polysaccharides:** Blood-group antigens, cell-surface markers (glycoproteins, glycolipids).

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**(i) Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) — Hyaluronic Acid & Heparin:**

- **GAGs** are long, unbranched heteropolysaccharides containing a **repeating disaccharide unit** of an **amino sugar (glucosamine or galactosamine)** and a **uronic acid (glucuronic or iduronic acid)** or galactose.
- They are highly **negatively charged** (due to –COO⁻ and –SO₃⁻ groups), attract water, and form **gel-like matrices**.

- **Hyaluronic acid (HA):**
  - Repeating unit: **D-glucuronic acid β-1,3 N-acetyl-D-glucosamine** linked β-1,4.
  - Found in **synovial fluid**, **vitreous humor of the eye**, **umbilical cord**, **skin ECM**.
  - Function: **lubrication**, **shock absorption** in joints, **wound healing**, **tissue hydration**.
  - Biomedical use: injected into osteoarthritic knees (viscosupplementation), used in eye surgery and cosmetic dermal fillers.

- **Heparin:**
  - Repeating unit: **D-glucuronic acid (or L-iduronic acid)** + **D-glucosamine**, with multiple **sulfate groups**.
  - Found in **mast cells** of liver, lung, intestines.
  - Function: potent **anticoagulant** — binds antithrombin III, accelerating inactivation of thrombin and factor Xa.
  - Clinical use: prevention and treatment of **deep vein thrombosis (DVT)**, **pulmonary embolism**, and as a coating on medical devices.

**(ii) Modified Cellulose Derivatives — Pharmaceutical Applications:**

- **Cellulose acetate (CA):** Used in **controlled-release tablets** and **membrane filters** for dialysis.
- **Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC):** Used as a **tablet binder, viscosity enhancer** in eye drops, and laxative.
- **Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC):** A key **controlled-release matrix former** in oral pharmaceuticals.
- **Methylcellulose (MC):** Used as a **thickening agent** and **laxative**.
- **Ethyl cellulose (EC):** Used as a **water-insoluble film coating** for sustained-release pellets.
- **Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel®):** Used as a **hemostatic gauze** in surgery — the carboxyl groups generated by oxidation promote platelet aggregation and clot formation.
- **Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC):** Most widely used **direct compression tablet excipient**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse α-1,4 (digestible, starch) with β-1,4 (non-digestible, cellulose). Examiners frequently test this with one-word answers like "digestible" vs "non-digestible".
> - **Do NOT** write *amylose* when the question asks about *amylopectin*; tabulate clearly with DP, linkage type, and solubility.
> - **Do NOT** forget to state that humans **lack cellulase** — this is a 2-mark standalone point.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *glycogen* (animal, α-1,4+α-1,6, in liver/muscle) with *starch* (plant, same linkages, in chloroplasts/amyloplasts).
> - For GAG questions, mention both **sugar composition** AND **charge/sulfation**, since this is what determines their biological function.
> - In derivations, **show units** in every step and write the **substituted numerical value** explicitly — examiners award partial marks for correctly substituted (but not solved) equations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chemistry of Polysaccharides**
>
> - Polysaccharides = many monosaccharide units joined by **glycosidic bonds**, with general formula $\left[C_6H_{10}O_5\right]_n$ for hexose polymers.
> - **Homopolysaccharides**: starch, glycogen, cellulose, chitin, inulin.
> - **Heteropolysaccharides**: hyaluronic acid, heparin, chondroitin sulfate, pectin, agar.
> - **Storage** vs **Structural** polysaccharides — remember glycogen (animal), starch (plant), cellulose/chitin (structural).
> - **α-1,4 linkages → helical, digestible (e.g., starch, glycogen)**.
> - **β-1,4 linkages → linear ribbon, non-digestible for humans (e.g., cellulose)**.
> - **α-1,6 linkages → branch points (e.g., amylopectin, glycogen)**.
> - **Glycosidic bond energy ≈ 90–100 kJ/mol**.
> - **Hydrolysis equation:** $Polysaccharide + (n-1)H_2O \rightarrow n$ monosaccharides.
> - **Molar mass formula:** $M = n \cdot 162 - (n-1) \cdot 18 \approx 144n$ g/mol.
> - **Esterification** of cellulose gives **cellulose acetate** (films, fibres); **nitration** gives **cellulose nitrate** (explosives, lacquers).
> - **Etherification** of cellulose gives **CMC, HPMC, MC, EC** — all major pharmaceutical excipients.
> - **Oxidation** of C6 primary hydroxyl → carboxyl group → **oxidized cellulose** (hemostatic).
> - **GAGs** are repeating disaccharides of amino sugar + uronic acid, highly anionic, found in ECM.
> - **Hyaluronic acid** = lubricant in joints & eye; **Heparin** = anticoagulant.
> - **Cellulose DP** ≈ 3,000–10,000; **Starch amylose DP** ≈ 300–3,000; **Amylopectin DP** ≈ 2,000–200,000; **Glycogen DP** ≈ up to 50,000.
> - **PDI** of natural polysaccharides > 1 (polydisperse); synthetic polymers can be near monodisperse.
> - **Biomedical applications**: tissue scaffolds, drug delivery, hemostatic agents, vaccine adjuvants, plasma expanders.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 4: Starch and Cellulose — Polysaccharide Biopolymers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Starch — The Energy Bank of Nature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Starch):**
> Starch is a natural, semi-crystalline, homo-polysaccharide biopolymer composed of anhydroglucose units (AGU) linked predominantly by **α-glycosidic bonds**. It is the principal energy reserve carbohydrate in plants and is stored as discrete **semi-crystalline granules** in chloroplasts and amyloplasts.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
Think of a starch granule as a **biological rechargeable battery** packed inside every rice grain, potato cell, and corn kernel. Just as a battery stores electrical energy in stable chemical form, the plant stitches thousands of glucose molecules together into tightly coiled helical chains (amylose) and branched tree-like structures (amylopectin). When the plant (or the animal that eats it) needs energy, enzymes called **amylases** snap the α-bonds and release glucose on demand. So every roti, chapati, or appam you eat is essentially your body "discharging" a plant-stored energy battery.

### 1.2 Cellulose — The Biological Reinforced Concrete

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Cellulose):**
> Cellulose is a linear, syndiotactic, fully biodegradable, **crystalline** homo-polysaccharide biopolymer of β-D-glucopyranose units joined by **β-(1→4) glycosidic linkages**. It is the most abundant organic polymer on Earth and the principal load-bearing structural component of plant cell walls.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
Imagine a **giant multi-storeyed car-parking building** made of identical steel rods (β-glucose units), each rod welded at exactly 180° to the next. This precise 180° flip creates perfectly straight, ribbon-like chains. Hundreds of these ribbons are then stacked together like a deck of cards and held in place by an enormous number of **inter-chain hydrogen bonds** — just like the steel cables bracing a skyscraper against wind. That is cellulose: rigid, fibrous, water-insoluble, and able to bear mechanical loads that no synthetic polymer can match on a per-mass basis. Cotton wool is **>90% cellulose**; wood is roughly **40–50% cellulose**.

### 1.3 Common Molecular Building Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) — The Universal Monomer:**
> Both starch and cellulose are *homo-polymers* of D-glucose. Their dramatic difference in physical, chemical and biological behaviour is **entirely controlled by the stereochemistry (α vs. β) of the glycosidic bond at the C1 anomeric carbon.** This is one of the most elegant examples of *stereochemistry dictating macroscopic material properties* in polymer science.

### 1.4 Standard Nomenclature & Key Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Starch) | Typical Value (Cellulose) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit molecular weight | $M_0$ | **162.14 g/mol** | **162.14 g/mol** |
| Anomeric configuration | — | α-D-glucopyranose | β-D-glucopyranose |
| Linkage | — | α-(1→4), α-(1→6) | β-(1→4) exclusively |
| Degree of Polymerisation (DP) | $n$ | **300 – 6000** | **1000 – 15000** (cotton ≈ 10000) |
| Polydispersity Index | $Ð$ | High (1.5 – 4.0) | Low (1.0 – 1.5) |
| Crystallinity | $\chi_c$ | Semi-crystalline (**20 – 45 %**) | Highly crystalline (**40 – 90 %**) |
| Density (solid) | $\rho$ | 1.5 g/cm³ | **1.54 – 1.59 g/cm³** |
| Glass Transition ($T_g$) | $T_g$ | ~ **230 °C** (dry) | ~ **220 – 230 °C** (dec. > 300 °C) |
| Water Solubility (cold) | — | Insoluble (granules), soluble after gelatinisation | **Practically insoluble** |
