# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Starch — Two-Component Architecture

Starch is **not** a single molecule; it is a physical blend of two structurally and functionally distinct glucose polymers:

### (a) Amylose — The Linear Reserve (typically 20 – 30 %)

- Linear chain of α-D-glucopyranose units linked by **α-(1→4) glycosidic bonds**.
- The α-linkage forces the chain into a **left-handed single helix** with **6 glucose units per turn** and a pitch of approximately **0.81 nm**.
- The helical cavity can complex with **iodine** (giving the classic deep-blue starch–iodine test), fatty acids, alcohols, and certain drugs → important in pharma encapsulation.
- DP typically **300 – 3000**; molecular weight **~ 5 × 10⁴ – 1.6 × 10⁵ g/mol**.

### (b) Amylopectin — The Branched Reserve (typically 70 – 80 %)

- Backbone of α-(1→4) D-glucose units with periodic **α-(1→6) branch points** occurring every **24 – 30** glucose residues.
- These branches create a **tree-like (dendrimer-like) architecture** with three structural tiers:
  1. **A-chains** (outermost, unbranched)
  2. **B-chains** (intermediate, bearing A or B chains)
  3. **C-chain** (single, reducing-end chain)
- Very high DP: **2000 – 200,000**; molecular weight up to **~10⁸ g/mol**.
- Outer branches form **crystalline lamellae** alternating with amorphous branch zones → produces the characteristic "growth ring" structure of the starch granule seen under polarised light as the **Maltese cross**.

### 2.2 Cellulose — The Uniform Linear Fibril

Cellulose is composed **exclusively** of β-D-glucopyranose units linked by **β-(1→4) glycosidic bonds**. Because each successive glucose is rotated **180°** with respect to its neighbour, the true chemical repeat unit is **cellobiose** (two glucose units).

### Hierarchical Structure of Cellulose (Kleppe / KTU-recommended model)

1. **Glucose monomer** (C₆H₁₂O₆).
2. **Cellobiose dimer** (the true repeat unit, M₀ = **342.3 g/mol**).
3. **Elementary fibril** — ~ 36 parallel cellulose chains held by H-bonds; cross-section ≈ 3 nm × 5 nm.
4. **Microfibril** — bundles of elementary fibrils (10 – 30 nm).
5. **Macrofibril** — microfibril aggregates (0.5 µm wide, visible in optical microscopy).
6. **Fibre** — macroscopic cell-wall assembly (cotton fibre, wood tracheid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cellulose is so strong:** Each anhydroglucose unit carries **three free –OH groups** (at C2, C3, C6). When hundreds of chains are aligned, these OHs form an extensive **intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen-bond network**, plus weak **C–H···O** contacts. The result is a composite with **tensile strength** comparable to aluminium on a weight basis (specific strength ~ 0.5–0.7 GPa·cm³/g).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Equation Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most-asked numerical concepts in KTU ESE for polysaccharide chapters.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Repeat-unit MW (anhydroglucose) | $M_0 = 162.14$ g/mol | Applies to BOTH starch and cellulose |
| 2 | Repeat-unit MW (cellobiose) | $M_{0,\text{cell}} = 324.28$ g/mol | True repeat of cellulose |
| 3 | Number-average MW | $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_n$ | $n$ = number of molecules |
| 4 | Weight-average MW | $\bar{M}_w = M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_w$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction |
| 5 | Polydispersity Index | $Ð = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \dfrac{\bar{DP}_w}{\bar{DP}_n}$ | Monodisperse → $Ð=1$ |
| 6 | Mass fraction of component | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum m_i}$ | e.g., amylose mass in starch |
| 7 | Hydrolysis water uptake | $M_{\text{poly}} + (n-1)H_2O \rightarrow n\,M_{\text{glucose}}$ | $(n-1)$ water molecules per chain |
| 8 | Crystallinity (XRD) | $\chi_c = \dfrac{A_{\text{cryst}}}{A_{\text{cryst}} + A_{\text{amorph}}} \times 100\%$ | $A$ = XRD peak area |
| 9 | Glycogen equivalent weight | $M_{\text{equiv}} = M_0 + 18$ (per glucose) | For DP from end-group assay |
| 10 | Helix pitch (amylose) | $p = 0.81$ nm | 6 AGU per turn |
| 11 | Cellulose I β lattice spacings | $d_{101} \approx 0.59$ nm, $d_{10\bar{1}} \approx 0.53$ nm | From WAXS |
| 12 | Degree of substitution (deriv.) | $DS = \dfrac{\sum n_i \cdot i}{n_{\text{AGU}}}$ | $n_i$ = number of OHs substituted |

### 2.4 Comparative Property Matrix (Frequently Asked in ESE)

| Property | Amylose | Amylopectin | Cellulose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linkage | α-(1→4) | α-(1→4) + α-(1→6) | β-(1→4) |
| Chain shape | Linear helix | Branched | Straight, ribbon |
| DP | 300 – 3000 | 2000 – 2 × 10⁵ | 1000 – 15000 |
| Iodine test | **Deep blue** (λ_max ≈ 620 nm) | **Red-purple** (λ_max ≈ 540 nm) | None |
| Water solubility | Soluble (hot water, after gelatinisation) | Insoluble (granular) | **Insoluble in all common solvents** |
| Crystallinity | Low (film-cast) | Semi-crystalline in granule | **High (Cellulose I)** |
| Biodegradability | Rapid (α-amylase in humans) | Rapid | Very slow (no human cellulase) |
| Industrial role | Film former, encapsulation | Thickener, stabiliser | Fibres, paper, biocomposites |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Starch:** Biodegradable packaging (e.g., PLA/TPS blends), pharmaceutical tablet disintegrant/binder, controlled drug release (microspheres), ethanol fermentation feedstock, food thickener (E1400–E1404 modified starches).
- **Cellulose:** Paper/pulp, regenerated fibres (rayon, lyocell, cellophane), cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) for green nanocomposites, dialysis membranes (Cuprophan® = regenerated cellulose), hydrogels, food fibre additive (E460), scaffolds in tissue engineering.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number-Average Molecular Weight from Degree of Polymerisation

**Starting relationship (KTU standard definition):**

$$
\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i \, M_i}{\sum N_i}
$$

For a **homopolymer** in which every chain consists of the same repeat unit of molar mass $M_0$, the molar mass of the $i$-th chain is:

$$
M_i = i \cdot M_0
$$

where $i$ is the degree of polymerisation (DP) of that individual chain.

**Substitute $M_i$ into the numerator:**

$$
\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i \cdot (i \cdot M_0)}{\sum N_i}
$$

**Factor the constant $M_0$ outside the sum:**

$$
\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \frac{\sum N_i \, i}{\sum N_i}
$$

**Recognise the term $\dfrac{\sum N_i \, i}{\sum N_i}$ as the number-average DP, $\bar{DP}_n$:**

$$
\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_n \qquad \text{...(Proved)}
$$

The analogous argument applied to mass-weighting gives:

$$
\bar{M}_w = M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_w
$$

and the **polydispersity index** follows immediately:

$$
Đ = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{\bar{DP}_w}{\bar{DP}_n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $Đ = 1$ means every chain is exactly the same length (theoretically only possible for proteins and certain bioengineered polymers). For native starch, $Đ$ is large because biosynthesis produces chains of widely varying length. For native cellulose from a single plant source, $Đ$ is much closer to 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of Glucose Released on Complete Hydrolysis

Consider a starch sample of mass $m_s$ g containing one chain of DP $= n$ (representative). The hydrolysis reaction in water is:

$$
\left(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5\right)_n + (n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6
$$

Mass balance on the anhydroglucose (AGU) unit:

- Mass of 1 AGU = 162.14 g/mol
- Mass of 1 glucose released = 180.16 g/mol
- Each bond hydrolysis adds **18.02 g/mol (1 water)** to the products.

**Mass of glucose produced from a single chain of DP $n$:**

$$
m_{\text{glucose}} = n \times 180.16 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Mass of the same chain before hydrolysis:**

$$
m_{\text{polymer}} = n \times 162.14 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Mass gain (water consumed) — useful for tracking reaction extent:**

$$
\Delta m = m_{\text{glucose}} - m_{\text{polymer}} = n \times (180.16 - 162.14) = 18.02\,n \text{ g}
$$

**Generalisation: Glucose yield from $m_s$ g of polymer with $N$ chains:**

$$
Y_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{180.16 \, n \, N}{162.14 \, n \, N} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 111.1\,\% \text{ on a mass basis}
$$

(Always slightly > 100 % because water is added chemically.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crystallinity Index from X-Ray Diffractogram (Segal Method, KTU-recommended)

The **Segal empirical crystallinity index (CrI)** is widely used in cellulose KTU numericals:

$$
\text{CrI} = \frac{I_{200} - I_{\text{am}}}{I_{200}} \times 100\%
$$

where:
- $I_{200}$ = intensity (counts) of the crystalline peak at $2\theta \approx 22.5°$ (cellulose I).
- $I_{\text{am}}$ = intensity at the amorphous trough, $2\theta \approx 18.0°$.

**Worked numerical — frequently asked:**

> *A cellulose fibre gives XRD intensities $I_{200} = 850$ counts and $I_{\text{am}} = 150$ counts. Calculate the Segal crystallinity index.*

**Step 1 — Plug values into the Segal equation:**

$$
\text{CrI} = \frac{850 - 150}{850} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the difference:**

$$
\text{CrI} = \frac{700}{850} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Final division:**

$$
\text{CrI} = 0.8235 \times 100 = 82.35\%
$$

The fibre is therefore **highly crystalline** (typical of native cotton, which is ~ 80 – 90 %).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Polymer?"

| Diagnostic Test | Starch (Amylose) | Starch (Amylopectin) | Cellulose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Iodine drop test | Deep blue | Red-violet | **No colour** |
| Solubility in hot water | Yes (gel) | Swells, partial | **Insoluble** |
| Solubility in Schweizer's reagent (Cu(NH₃)₄²⁺) | No | No | **Yes** (forms complex) |
| Hydrolysis by human α-amylase | **Yes** | Yes (slower) | **No** (no enzyme) |
| Hydrolysis by cellulase | No | No | **Yes** |
| Birefringence (polarised light) | Maltese cross (granules) | Maltese cross | Strong, fibrillar |
| Anomeric C1 (NMR, ppm in D₂O) | **~ 100.0** (α) | ~ 100.0 (α) | **~ 103.5** (β) |
| ¹³C CPMAS chemical shift of C1 (solid) | 99 – 101 ppm | 99 – 101 ppm | **104 – 106 ppm** |
| Strongest XRD reflection (2θ) | 15°, 17°, 23° (B-type) | same | **22.5° (Iβ), 34.5°** |
| TGA: char residue at 600 °C | ~ 10 % | ~ 10 % | **~ 5–15 %** (high) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Branch Density of Amylopectin

A useful KTU 5-mark problem: given the average chain length between branch points, calculate the mole percent of α-(1→6) bonds.

**Let:**
- $\bar{N}_b$ = average number of AGU between successive α-(1→6) branch points (typically 24–30 in normal starches, ~ 20 in waxy maize).
- Total AGU per amylopectin molecule = $N_{\text{tot}}$ (typically 2 × 10⁴).

**Number of branch points per molecule:**

$$
N_{\text{bp}} = \frac{N_{\text{tot}}}{\bar{N}_b}
$$

**Number of α-(1→6) bonds per molecule = $N_{\text{bp}}$** (each branch consumes one α-1,6 bond).

**Total glycosidic bonds = $(N_{\text{tot}} - 1)$**.

**Mole percent of α-(1→6) bonds:**

$$
x_{1\rightarrow 6} = \frac{N_{\text{bp}}}{N_{\text{tot}} - 1} \times 100\%
$$

For typical values ($N_{\text{tot}} = 2 \times 10^4$, $\bar{N}_b = 25$):

$$
x_{1\rightarrow 6} = \frac{800}{19999} \times 100 \approx 4.0\%
$$

This 4 % is responsible for the dramatic change in physical behaviour compared to amylose.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Differentiate between the structures of starch and cellulose with suitable diagrams. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme):**

| Aspect | Starch | Cellulose |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer | α-D-glucopyranose | β-D-glucopyranose |
| Linkage type | α-(1→4) and α-(1→6) | β-(1→4) only |
| Polymer architecture | Mixture of linear (amylose) + branched (amylopectin) | Strictly linear |
| Steric geometry | Helical, semi-crystalline | Straight ribbon, highly crystalline |
| Function in nature | Energy storage | Structural support |
| Iodine test | Blue-violet (amylose) | Negative |

*(1 mark each for monomer, linkage and function/geometry; 1 bonus mark for Iodine test.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Explain the iodine test for starch. Why does amylopectin give a red-violet colour while amylose gives a deep blue colour? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The iodine test relies on the formation of a **polyiodide charge-transfer complex** within the hydrophobic helical cavity of starch.

- **Amylose** has a long, uninterrupted single helix that accommodates long linear polyiodide chains (I₃⁻, I₅⁻…), producing a charge-transfer absorption at **~620 nm (deep blue)**.  **(1 mark)**
- **Amylopectin's** helical segments are **short** (DP ~ 15–20 between branches) and sterically interrupted; the polyiodide chains that fit are therefore shorter, shifting the absorption to **~540 nm (red-violet)**.  **(1 mark)**
- **Cellulose** has straight, non-helical chains → no cavity → **no complex formation → no colour**.  **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

> **Q: (a)** Describe the chemical structure, composition and granule morphology of starch. Discuss the role of amylose and amylopectin in determining its physico-chemical properties.  **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** A commercial waxy maize starch sample has an amylopectin weight fraction of 0.98 and an average DP of 1.2 × 10⁵. Compute its number-average molecular weight, weight-average molecular weight (assume $Đ = 1.6$) and the degree of crystallinity if the XRD peak area for the crystalline region is 4800 units and the amorphous trough area is 2200 units.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
- **Chemical structure (2 marks):** Starch = α-D-glucopyranose polymer. Two components. Amylose = linear, α-(1→4), DP 300–3000, helical with 6 AGU/turn. Amylopectin = α-(1→4) backbone + α-(1→6) branches every 24–30 residues, DP up to 2 × 10⁵, tree-like A-B-C chain hierarchy.
- **Granule morphology (2 marks):** Semi-crystalline granules, size 1–100 µm, birefringent Maltese cross under polarised light, alternating crystalline (clusters of amylopectin double helices) and amorphous (branch points) growth rings, ~9 nm repeat.
- **Physico-chemical properties (3 marks):** Amylose → film-forming, retrogradation, complexation with lipids/iodine. Amylopectin → viscosity, water-holding, freeze-thaw stability. Waxy starches (high amylopectin) → stable pastes. High-amylose starches → resistant starch, biodegradable films.

**(b) [7 marks]**
Given: $w_{\text{AP}} = 0.98$, $Đ = 1.6$, $\bar{DP}_n = 1.2 \times 10^5$ (read as number-average per problem statement; for $Đ$ calculation we need both $n$ and $w$ averages). Reinterpret: take $\bar{DP}_n = 1.2 \times 10^5 / 1.6 = 7.5 \times 10^4$, $\bar{DP}_w = 1.2 \times 10^5$.

**Step 1 — Number-average molecular weight:**

$$
\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_n = 162.14 \times 7.5 \times 10^4 = 1.216 \times 10^7 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight-average molecular weight:**

$$
\bar{M}_w = M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_w = 162.14 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 = 1.946 \times 10^7 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Crystallinity (Segal method):**

$$
\chi_c = \frac{4800}{4800 + 2200} \times 100\% = \frac{4800}{7000} \times 100\% = 68.57\%
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of amylopectin in 1 kg starch:**

$$
m_{\text{AP}} = 0.98 \times 1000 = 980 \text{ g}
$$

*(Award full 7 marks for stepwise calculation, units, and final answers.)*

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice Alternative**

> **Q: (a)** Discuss the hierarchical structure of cellulose from the level of a single anhydroglucose unit to a macroscopic cotton fibre. Explain how hydrogen bonding contributes to its mechanical strength and high crystallinity.  **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** A cellulose sample of mass 0.250 g is completely hydrolysed to glucose. If the number-average degree of polymerisation is 5000, calculate (i) the number of cellulose chains, (ii) the moles of water consumed, and (iii) the theoretical mass of glucose produced.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
- **Hierarchical structure (4 marks):** Glucose → cellobiose (true repeat, 180° rotated) → elementary fibril (~36 chains) → microfibril (10–30 nm) → macrofibril → fibre.
- **Hydrogen bonding (2 marks):** Three –OH groups per AGU (C2, C3, C6). Intra-chain O3–H···O5' and O2–H···O6' bonds lock chain conformation; inter-chain O6–H···O3 bonds hold parallel sheets. Total H-bond density ~ 1 per AGU per interface → high cohesive energy density.
- **Mechanical + crystalline consequence (1 mark):** Yields tensile strength of ~ 0.5–1.0 GPa, Young's modulus ~ 100–130 GPa (Cellulose I), and crystallinity 40–90 %; cellulose Iβ is the thermodynamically stable allomorph in higher plants.

**(b) [7 marks]**
Given: $m_s = 0.250$ g, $\bar{DP}_n = 5000$, $M_0 = 162.14$ g/mol, $M_{\text{glucose}} = 180.16$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Number of chains:**

$$
N = \frac{m_s}{M_0 \cdot \bar{DP}_n} = \frac{0.250}{162.14 \times 5000}
$$

$$
N = \frac{0.250}{8.107 \times 10^5} = 3.083 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol of chains}
$$

Multiplying by Avogadro's number:

$$
N_{\text{chains}} = 3.083 \times 10^{-7} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.857 \times 10^{17} \text{ chains}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of water consumed:** Each chain of DP 5000 requires (5000 − 1) water molecules.

$$
n_{H_2O} = 3.083 \times 10^{-7} \times (5000 - 1) = 1.541 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of glucose produced:** Each chain releases 5000 glucose molecules.

$$
n_{\text{glucose}} = 3.083 \times 10^{-7} \times 5000 = 1.541 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{glucose}} = 1.541 \times 10^{-3} \times 180.16 = 0.2777 \text{ g}
$$

*(Note the 11.1 % mass gain due to water uptake — a classic KTU examiner's check.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $M_0$ values:** Students often write 180 g/mol (glucose MW) as the repeat unit. **Always use 162.14 g/mol (AGU)** for the anhydrous polymer backbone; 180.16 g/mol is the hydrolysed monomer mass.
> 2. **Wrong linkage stereochemistry:** Marking α-1,4 for cellulose (or β-1,4 for starch) is the single most common error — costs the full linkage sub-question (2 marks).
> 3. **Mixing up $Đ$ definitions:** Some students define $Đ = \bar{M}_n / \bar{M}_w$, the inverse of the correct definition. KTU strictly uses $Đ = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n \geq 1$.
> 4. **Forgetting the water stoichiometry:** In hydrolysis, $(n-1)$ water molecules are added per chain, not $n$.
> 5. **Not stating the repeat unit for cellulose:** Examiners specifically check if you write "**cellobiose**" as the true repeat of cellulose — missing this costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerics:** Always write g/mol, mol, and °C; KTU deducts ½ mark for missing units.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Starch = α-D-glucose; Cellulose = β-D-glucose. **One stereochemical change → completely different polymer behaviour.**
- ✅ Starch has two components: **amylose (linear, helical, ~25 %)** and **amylopectin (branched, ~75 %)**.
- ✅ Starch linkages: **α-(1→4)** (backbone) + **α-(1→6)** (branches in amylopectin).
- ✅ Cellulose linkage: **β-(1→4)** only; repeat unit is **cellobiose (M₀ = 342.28 g/mol)**, but for DP calculations use AGU = 162.14 g/mol.
- ✅ **Iodine test:** amylose = blue, amylopectin = red-violet, cellulose = negative. Mechanism = polyiodide helix inclusion complex.
- ✅ **Crystallinity order:** Cellulose I (cotton, ~85 %) > Amylopectin granules (B-type, 20–45 %) > Amylose (mostly amorphous after gelatinisation).
- ✅ **DP values to memorise:** Amylose 300–3000; Amylopectin up to 2 × 10⁵; Cellulose 1000–15000 (cotton ≈ 10000).
- ✅ **Hydrolysis equation:** $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + (n-1)H_2O \rightarrow n \, C_6H_{12}O_6$; mass gain = 11.1 % of original.
- ✅ **Key equations:** $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \bar{DP}_n$, $\bar{M}_w = M_0 \bar{DP}_w$, $Đ = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$, Segal CrI = $(I_{200} - I_{am})/I_{200} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Solubility:** Starch granules swell in hot water (gelatinisation, 60–80 °C); cellulose is insoluble in water and most organic solvents but soluble in Schweizer's reagent, ionic liquids and NMMO (lyocell process).
- ✅ **Digestibility:** Humans have α-amylase → digest starch; **no cellulase** → cellulose is dietary fibre.
- ✅ **Industrial importance:** Starch → packaging, pharma, ethanol. Cellulose → paper, regenerated fibres, CNC, hydrogels, dialysis membranes, tissue scaffolds.
- ✅ **Crystalline allomorphs of cellulose:** Cellulose I (native, parallel chains) and Cellulose II (regenerated, anti-parallel, more thermodynamically stable) — frequently asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Modifications of Cellulose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Modifications of Cellulose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cellulose** is a linear, semi-crystalline homopolysaccharide composed of β-(1→4)-linked D-glucopyranose units (anhydroglucose units, AGU), forming the most abundant natural biopolymer on Earth. The chemical formula of the repeating unit is $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$, where $n$ denotes the **degree of polymerization (DP)**, typically ranging from **300 to 15,000** depending on the source and isolation method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Chemical modification of cellulose refers to the controlled chemical transformation of the hydroxyl (–OH) groups present at the C2, C3, and C6 positions of the anhydroglucose unit to alter the polymer's solubility, processability, reactivity, hydrophobicity, and biological performance, while retaining its biodegradable backbone.*

Each anhydroglucose unit carries **three hydroxyl groups** (one primary –CH₂OH at C6 and two secondary –OH at C2 and C3), which act as the active sites for almost all chemical modifications. The β-(1→4) glycosidic linkage forces the polymer into an extended linear conformation stabilized by extensive **intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding**, producing the characteristic crystalline microfibril architecture.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The LEGO Brick Wall with Three Studs"**

Imagine cellulose as a long wall made of identical LEGO bricks (the glucose units), glued tightly together by hydrogen bonds (the "mortar"). Each brick has **three studs (the –OH groups)** — one big central stud and two smaller ones. Plain cellulose is hard, stiff, and doesn't dissolve in regular solvents because the studs are all "locked in handshake" with neighboring bricks.

**Chemical modification = changing those studs**:
- Replace them with "acetate caps" (esterification) → makes the wall water-resistant and soluble in acetone.
- Replace them with "carboxylate groups" (carboxymethylation) → makes the wall water-soluble and gel-forming.
- Add "vinyl polymer chains" dangling from the studs (grafting) → makes the wall smart and stimuli-responsive.

By choosing which studs to modify and how, we can tune cellulose from a *stiff, insoluble plant fiber* into *hydrogels, films, drug carriers, and surgical meshes*.

## 1.3 The "Big Picture" — Why Modify Cellulose?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Native cellulose, despite its abundance and biocompatibility, suffers from three engineering drawbacks that limit direct biomedical use:
> 1. **Poor solubility** in water and most common organic solvents.
> 2. **High crystallinity** that prevents melt processing.
> 3. **Lack of functional handles** for drug conjugation, crosslinking, or smart responsive behavior.

Chemical modification solves all three by transforming the surface chemistry without destroying the biodegradable polysaccharide backbone.

## 1.4 The Three Reactive Hydroxyl Sites

| Position | Type | Reactivity | Steric Accessibility |
|----------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **C2–OH** | Secondary | Moderate | Medium |
| **C3–OH** | Secondary | Lowest (intramolecular H-bond) | Lowest |
| **C6–OH** | Primary | **Highest** | **Highest** |

The **C6 primary hydroxyl** is preferentially modified in most kinetically controlled reactions due to its minimal steric hindrance, although regioselective chemistry at C2 and C3 is also well established.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Cellulose Modifications

Chemical modifications of cellulose are organized into **four major families** based on the chemistry of the transforming agent:

### A. Esterification
Replacement of the H of –OH with an acyl group to form **–O–CO–R**.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Organic esters**: Cellulose acetate (CA), Cellulose acetate phthalate (CAP), Cellulose acetate butyrate (CAB)
- **Inorganic esters**: Cellulose nitrate (CN), Cellulose sulfate, Cellulose phosphate

**Reaction (Acetylation example):**

$$
\text{Cell–OH} + (CH_3CO)_2O \xrightarrow{\,H_2SO_4\,} \text{Cell–O–COCH}_3 + CH_3COOH
$$

### B. Etherification
Replacement of the H of –OH with an alkyl/alkyl-substituted group to form **–O–R**.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)**: –O–CH₂COOH
- **Hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC)**: –O–CH₂CH₂OH
- **Methyl cellulose (MC)**: –O–CH₃
- **Ethyl cellulose (EC)**: –O–CH₂CH₃
- **Benzyl cellulose**

**Reaction (Williamson ether synthesis, CMC example):**

$$
\text{Cell–OH} + ClCH_2COONa \xrightarrow{\,NaOH\,} \text{Cell–O–CH}_2COONa + HCl
$$

### C. Oxidation
Selective conversion of –OH groups (especially C2–C3 vicinal diols) to **carbonyls or carboxyls**.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Periodate oxidation** → dialdehyde cellulose (DAC) by cleaving the C2–C3 bond.
- **TEMPO-mediated oxidation** → C6-carboxylated cellulose (TOCNC) using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical.

**Reaction (Periodate):**

$$
\text{Cell–(C2OH)(C3OH)} + NaIO_4 \longrightarrow \text{Cell–(CHO)(CHO)}
$$

### D. Graft Copolymerization
"Covalent tethering" of synthetic polymer side-chains onto the cellulose backbone via free-radical, ATRP, RAFT, or ROP mechanisms.

**Initiator systems commonly used:** Ceric ammonium nitrate (CAN), Fenton's reagent, potassium persulfate (KPS), γ-radiation.

$$
\text{Cell–OH} \xrightarrow{\,Ce^{4+}\,} \text{Cell–O}^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\,M\,} \text{Cell–O–(M)}_n
$$

## 2.2 Degree of Substitution (DS) — The Central Quantitative Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Substitution (DS)** is the *average number of substituted hydroxyl groups per anhydroglucose unit*. Because each AGU has 3 hydroxyls, **DS ranges from 0 (unmodified) to 3 (fully substituted).**

**The Fundamental DS Formula:**

$$
DS = \frac{M_{AGU} \times \%\,\text{substituent}}{M_{substituent} \times 100 - M_{removed-H} \times \%\,\text{substituent}}
$$

Where:
- $M_{AGU}$ = Molar mass of anhydroglucose unit = **162 g/mol**
- $M_{substituent}$ = Molar mass of the grafted group
- $M_{removed-H}$ = Mass of H removed per substitution (1 g/mol for simple OH replacement)

**Special case for CMC (carboxymethyl cellulose):**

$$
DS = \frac{162 \times \%\,\text{NaCH}_2COO}}{80 \times 100 - 80 \times \%\,\text{NaCH}_2COO}}
$$

Where **80** is the molar mass of the sodium carboxymethyl group (–CH₂COONa).

## 2.3 Molar Substitution (MS) — For Ether Substituents

For hydroxyalkyl ethers (e.g., HEC, HPC), the substituent itself carries –OH that can react further, creating **oligomeric side chains**. In such cases, MS ≥ DS.

$$
MS = DS + \text{(average additional ethylene oxide units)}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Repeating unit mass | $M_{AGU} = 162$ g/mol | Anhydroglucose unit |
| 2 | Degree of Substitution | $DS = \dfrac{162 \times \%\,S}{M_S \times 100 - M_{H,removed} \times \%\,S}$ | $0 \le DS \le 3$ |
| 3 | DS for CMC | $DS = \dfrac{162 \times \%\,S}{80 \times 100 - 80 \times \%\,S}$ | $S$ = –CH₂COONa |
| 4 | Molar Substitution | $MS = DS + n_{EO}$ | $n_{EO}$ = avg EO units |
| 5 | Acetyl content | $\%\text{Acetyl} = \dfrac{43 \times DS}{162 + 42 \times DS} \times 100$ | CA ester |
| 6 | Crystallinity index (XRD) | $CrI = \dfrac{I_{200} - I_{am}}{I_{200}} \times 100$ | Segal method |
| 7 | Periodate consumption | $n_{IO_4^-}/\text{AGU}$ | Max = 1 mol/AGU |
| 8 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (CMC pKa ≈ 4.0) | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | pH-responsiveness |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Biomedical Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU biomedical engineering students:**
> - **Cellulose Acetate (CA, DS 2.0–2.5)** → hemodialysis membranes, drug coatings, absorbable sutures.
> - **CMC (DS 0.6–0.95)** → hydrogels, wound dressings, viscosity enhancer, superdisintegrant in tablets.
> - **Dialdehyde Cellulose (DAC)** → crosslinker, Schiff-base bioadhesive, enzyme immobilization matrix.
> - **HEC / MC** → thermoresponsive gels (LCST behavior in MC around **40–55 °C**).
> - **Cellulose Sulfate** → antiviral (HIV microbicides), anticoagulant (heparin mimic).
> - **Grafted Cellulose-g-PNIPAM** → thermoswitchable drug release platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating DS of Cellulose Triacetate from % Acetyl

**Problem:** A sample of cellulose acetate is found by saponification to contain **44.8% acetyl groups** by mass. Calculate the **degree of substitution (DS)**.

**Given:**
- % Acetyl = 44.8
- Molar mass of acetyl group (–COCH₃) = 43 g/mol
- Molar mass of anhydroglucose unit (AGU) = 162 g/mol
- Mass of H removed per substitution = 1 g/mol

### Step 1 — Write the General DS Equation

$$
DS = \frac{M_{AGU} \times \%\,S}{M_{S} \times 100 - M_{H,removed} \times \%\,S}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$
DS = \frac{162 \times 44.8}{43 \times 100 - 1 \times 44.8}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerator Evaluation

$$
162 \times 44.8 = 7257.6
$$

### Step 4 — Denominator Evaluation

$$
43 \times 100 = 4300
$$

$$
4300 - 44.8 = 4255.2
$$

### Step 5 — Final Division

$$
DS = \frac{7257.6}{4255.2} = 1.706
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

$$
DS \approx 1.71 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Cellulose Acetate (CA), commercial grade ("secondary acetate")}
$$

If DS were **3.0** → full **cellulose triacetate** (CTA, used in photographic film and high-strength fibers).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DS of Carboxymethyl Cellulose (CMC) from Back-Titration

**Problem:** 0.500 g of purified CMC is dissolved, acidified, washed, dried, and weighed as the free acid form. The recovered mass of free-acid cellulose derivative is **0.587 g**. Calculate DS.

**Given:** Sample mass = 0.500 g; Recovered acid form = 0.587 g; M(CH₂COONa) = 80 g/mol; M(CH₂COOH) = 59 g/mol.

### Step 1 — Mass of Sodium Carboxymethyl Substituent in the Original Salt

Let $m$ = mass of NaCMC recovered, $m_{salt}$ = mass of original CMC sample. The CMC sodium salt has the substituent mass per AGU of $80 \times DS$. For the free acid, this becomes $59 \times DS$.

The recovered mass ratio gives:

$$
\frac{m_{acid}}{m_{salt}} = \frac{M_{AGU} + 59 \times DS}{M_{AGU} + 80 \times DS}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numbers

$$
\frac{0.587}{0.500} = \frac{162 + 59 \times DS}{162 + 80 \times DS}
$$

$$
1.174 = \frac{162 + 59 \times DS}{162 + 80 \times DS}
$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Multiply

$$
1.174 \times (162 + 80 \times DS) = 162 + 59 \times DS
$$

$$
190.19 + 93.92 \times DS = 162 + 59 \times DS
$$

### Step 4 — Collect DS Terms

$$
93.92 \times DS - 59 \times DS = 162 - 190.19
$$

$$
34.92 \times DS = -28.19
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A negative DS indicates an *inconsistency in the experimental data*. The mass of the acid form should be **less than** the mass of the sodium salt form (since 59 < 80). Recheck: if recovered mass is **0.456 g** instead of 0.587 g, proceed below.

### Step 4 (Corrected) — Re-derivation

$$
\frac{0.456}{0.500} = \frac{162 + 59 \times DS}{162 + 80 \times DS}
$$

$$
0.912 = \frac{162 + 59 \times DS}{162 + 80 \times DS}
$$

$$
0.912 \times (162 + 80 \times DS) = 162 + 59 \times DS
$$

$$
147.74 + 72.96 \times DS = 162 + 59 \times DS
$$

$$
72.96 \times DS - 59 \times DS = 162 - 147.74
$$

$$
13.96 \times DS = 14.26
$$

$$
DS = \frac{14.26}{13.96} \approx 1.02
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

$$
DS \approx 1.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{CMC with ~one carboxymethyl group per AGU (high-DS CMC)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Modification Reagent vs. Product

| Modification | Reagent | Catalyst / Conditions | Product | Typical DS / MS | Key Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acetylation** | Acetic anhydride | H₂SO₄ / pyridine, 50–80 °C | Cellulose Acetate (CA) | DS 0.5–3.0 | Hemodialysis membrane, drug coating |
| **Nitration** | HNO₃ / H₂SO₄ | Mixed acid, 0–30 °C | Cellulose Nitrate (CN) | DS 1.5–2.9 | Wound dressing (Collodion) |
| **Carboxymethylation** | Monochloroacetic acid | NaOH (slurry), 30–60 °C | CMC | DS 0.4–1.5 | Hydrogel, superdisintegrant |
| **Hydroxyethylation** | Ethylene oxide | NaOH, 50 °C | HEC | MS 1.5–3.0 | Thickener, ophthalmic lubricant |
| **Methylation** | CH₃Cl / DMS | NaOH / DMSO | MC | DS 1.0–2.0 | Thermoresponsive gel |
| **Periodate Oxidation** | NaIO₄ | Aqueous, dark, 25 °C | Dialdehyde Cellulose (DAC) | DHO 0.1–1.0 | Crosslinker, bioadhesive |
| **TEMPO Oxidation** | NaClO / NaBr / TEMPO | pH 10, 25 °C | C6-Carboxyl Cellulose (TOCN) | COOH 0.2–1.0 mmol/g | Nanofibrils, hydrogel scaffold |
| **Sulfation** | ClSO₃H / SO₃·pyridine | DMF / pyridine, 0–60 °C | Cellulose Sulfate | DS 0.2–2.5 | Anticoagulant, antiviral |
| **Grafting (PNIPAM)** | NIPAM monomer | CAN initiator, 60 °C | Cellulose-g-PNIPAM | Graft% 20–150 | Thermosensitive drug release |
| **Silylation** | Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS) | Pyridine / DMF | Trimethylsilyl Cellulose | DS 0.5–3.0 | Hydrophobic coatings, chromatography |

---

## 3.4 Mechanism Walk-Through: Acetylation of Cellulose

The most industrially important modification — **heterogeneous acetylation with acetic anhydride catalyzed by H₂SO₄**.

**Step 1 — Activation of anhydride:**

$$
(CH_3CO)_2O + H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow CH_3CO^+ + CH_3COO^- + HSO_4^-
$$

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic attack by cellulose C6–OH:**

$$
\text{Cell–CH}_2OH + CH_3CO^+ \longrightarrow \text{Cell–CH}_2O^+(H)COCH_3
$$

**Step 3 — Proton transfer, release of acetic acid:**

$$
\text{Cell–CH}_2O^+(H)COCH_3 \longrightarrow \text{Cell–CH}_2OCOCH_3 + H^+
$$

**Net stoichiometric equation:**

$$
\text{Cell–OH} + (CH_3CO)_2O \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O–COCH}_3 + CH_3COOH
$$

Each substitution releases one **molecule of acetic acid** as by-product. The **DS is controlled** by:
- Reaction time
- Temperature
- Anhydride-to-AGU ratio
- Catalyst loading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** H₂SO₄ is the catalyst because it protonates the anhydride to generate the highly electrophilic acylium ion. Some industrial processes use **HClO₄ or p-toluenesulfonic acid** as alternatives.

---

## 3.5 Mechanism Walk-Through: Periodate Oxidation (C2–C3 Cleavage)

Sodium metaperiodate (NaIO₄) selectively cleaves the **vicinal diol** (C2–C3) of the AGU, generating **two aldehyde groups** per oxidized unit.

**Step 1 — Cyclic periodate ester formation:**

$$
\text{Cell–CHOH–CHOH–} + IO_4^- \longrightarrow \text{Cell–CH–O}\cdots\text{O–CH–} + IO_3^-
$$

**Step 2 — Malaprade-type C–C bond cleavage:**

$$
\text{Cell–CH–O}\cdots\text{O–CH–} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–CHO} + \text{–CHO–Cell (or terminal)}
$$

**Stoichiometry:** **1 mol NaIO₄ consumed per mol AGU** at full oxidation.

**Degree of oxidation** is reported as:
- DHO (dialdehyde content, % of oxidized units), or
- Moles of periodate consumed per mole of AGU.

> [!NOTE]
> DAC is the foundation for **Schiff-base crosslinked hydrogels** used in pH-sensitive drug delivery — aldehydes react with amine-bearing drugs/polymers (e.g., chitosan, gelatin) to form **–CH=N–** imine bonds, which are stable in circulation but hydrolyze in the acidic endosomal pH (≈ 5.0).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Cellulose Repeating Unit with Three Reactive Hydroxyls

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Anhydroglucose Unit AGU] --> B[C1 Beta Linkage]
    A --> C[C2 Secondary OH]
    A --> D[C3 Secondary OH]
    A --> E[C6 Primary OH]
    
    C --> F[Reactivity: Moderate]
    D --> G[Reactivity: Lowest]
    E --> H[Reactivity: Highest]
    
    E --> I[Preferred Site for Acetylation]
    E --> J[Preferred Site for Carboxymethylation]
    E --> K[Preferred Site for Silylation]
    
    style A fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — DS-Property Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    DS0[DS equals 0 Native Cellulose] --> R1[Insoluble in Water]
    DS0 --> R2[Insoluble in Most Organic Solvents]
    DS0 --> R3[Highest Crystallinity]
    
    DS1[DS 0.5 to 1.5 Partially Substituted] --> S1[Water Swellable]
    DS1 --> S2[Soluble in Dilute Alkali]
    DS1 --> S3[Film Forming Property]
    
    DS2[DS 1.5 to 2.5 Highly Substituted] --> T1[Soluble in Acetone DMF DMSO]
    DS2 --> T2[Thermoplastic]
    DS2 --> T3[Hydrophobic]
    
    DS3[DS equals 3 Fully Substituted Triacetate] --> U1[Insoluble in Acetone]
    DS3 --> U2[Soluble Only in Chloroform Dichloromethane]
    DS3 --> U3[Maximum Hydrophobicity]
    
    style DS0 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#212121
    style DS1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style DS2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style DS3 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define the term "Degree of Substitution (DS)" in the context of cellulose chemistry. Why is it a critical parameter in choosing a cellulose derivative for biomedical applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Degree of Substitution is the average number of hydroxyl groups (out of the three available per anhydroglucose unit, AGU) that have been chemically substituted. Mathematically, $0 \le DS \le 3$.
- **Significance (2 marks):** DS directly governs solubility, swellability, hydrophobicity, and biological fate. For example, **CMC with DS ≈ 0.7** is the standard pharmaceutical superdisintegrant, while **CA with DS ≈ 2.5** is the FDA-approved hemodialysis membrane material. In drug delivery, DS controls the rate of drug release, biodegradation, and polymer–cell interactions. Hence, biomedical product specification always requires tight DS control (typically ±0.1).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Degree of Substitution (DS) and Molar Substitution (MS). When is the MS concept necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **DS (1 mark):** Defined per AGU; only valid when each substituent is *monofunctional* (i.e., cannot itself react further). Maximum value = 3.
- **MS (1 mark):** Average number of *moles of monomeric unit of the substituent* attached per AGU. **MS can exceed 3** because the substituent (e.g., –CH₂CH₂OH in HEC) carries a hydroxyl that can undergo further reaction with more ethylene oxide, forming oligomeric pendant chains.
- **When necessary (1 mark):** MS is required for **hydroxyalkyl ethers** such as HEC, HPC, and HP-β-CD where the substituent itself has reactive –OH groups. For simple esters (CA, CN) and simple ethers (MC, EC), DS = MS.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme — Question Paper Pattern**
> *(Each question carries 14 marks, sub-parts are 7 + 7)*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat schematic, describe the **acetylation of cellulose** using acetic anhydride. Discuss the effect of **degree of substitution (DS)** on the solubility and applications of cellulose acetate.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Reaction scheme (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Cell–OH} + (CH_3CO)_2O \xrightarrow{H_2SO_4} \text{Cell–O–COCH}_3 + CH_3COOH
$$

Industrial process: Cellulose is pre-treated with **glacial acetic acid** to activate, then reacted with excess **acetic anhydride** in the presence of **H₂SO₄ catalyst** at 50–80 °C. Triacetate (DS = 3) is first formed; controlled hydrolysis with water then "knocks back" the DS to 2.0–2.5 for the commercial secondary acetate.

**(ii) Effect of DS on solubility (3 marks):**

| DS Range | Common Name | Solubility |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0–0.5 | – | Insoluble in common solvents |
| 0.5–1.5 | Low DS CA | Water-swellable, alkali-soluble |
| 1.5–2.5 | Secondary Acetate (CA) | Soluble in **acetone, DMF, DMSO** |
| 2.8–3.0 | Primary Acetate / Triacetate (CTA) | Soluble only in **chloroform, dichloromethane** |

**(iii) Applications (2 marks):**
- **CA (DS ≈ 2.5):** Hemodialysis membranes, reverse osmosis membranes, biodegradable films for transdermal drug patches, cigarette filters, spectacle frames.
- **CTA (DS ≈ 3.0):** Photographic film base, high-strength fibers, electrically insulating papers.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **periodate oxidation of cellulose**. How is the resulting **dialdehyde cellulose (DAC)** used as a crosslinker in biomedical hydrogels?

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Reaction (2 marks):**
Sodium metaperiodate (NaIO₄) cleaves the C2–C3 vicinal diol of the AGU to give two aldehydes per oxidized unit. Maximum theoretical consumption = 1 mol NaIO₄ per mol AGU.

$$
\text{Cell–CHOH–CHOH–} + NaIO_4 \longrightarrow \text{Cell–CHO + OHC–} + NaIO_3 + H_2O
$$

The reaction is carried out in the **dark** (periodate is light-sensitive) at **25 °C** in aqueous suspension.

**(ii) Control of oxidation (2 marks):**
- Moles of NaIO₄ per mole AGU (typically 0.1–1.0)
- Reaction time (1–48 h)
- Temperature
- pH (best at pH 3–5)

The product is reported as **DHO (dialdehyde content, %)** determined by oxime titration or by measuring periodate consumption spectrophotometrically at **222 nm**.

**(iii) Use in biomedical hydrogels (3 marks):**
The aldehyde groups of DAC form **Schiff base (imine) bonds** with primary amines of biopolymers (chitosan, gelatin, BSA) and amine-bearing drugs (e.g., doxorubicin, peptides):

$$
\text{DAC–CHO} + H_2N\text{–R} \longrightarrow \text{DAC–CH=N–R} + H_2O
$$

These **dynamic covalent bonds** are:
- **Stable at physiological pH 7.4** (sustained release).
- **Cleaved in acidic environments** (tumor pH ≈ 6.5, endosomal pH ≈ 5.0) → triggered drug release.
- **Self-healing** in some formulations.

Applications: **injectable in-situ forming hydrogels** for post-surgical tumor therapy, cartilage regeneration scaffolds, bioadhesive wound dressings, and enzyme (e.g., trypsin, lipase) immobilization supports.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]:** Describe the **carboxymethylation of cellulose** to prepare CMC. Discuss how DS affects its viscosity behavior and pharmaceutical applications.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Reaction (2 marks):**
Two-step slurry process using monochloroacetic acid (MCA) and NaOH:

**Step 1 — Alkali activation:**
$$
\text{Cell–OH} + NaOH \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O}^-Na^+ + H_2O
$$

**Step 2 — Williamson etherification:**
$$
\text{Cell–O}^-Na^+ + ClCH_2COONa \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O–CH}_2COONa + NaCl
$$

**(ii) Process parameters (2 marks):**
- NaOH/cellulose ratio: 1.5–3.0
- MCA/cellulose ratio: 0.4–1.5 (controls DS)
- Solvent: water/ethanol or water/isopropanol slurry
- Temperature: 30–60 °C; Time: 1–6 h

The sodium salt is purified by washing with **70 % ethanol**, neutralizing with **acetic acid**, then drying.

**(iii) DS–Viscosity–Application relationship (3 marks):**

| DS | Viscosity Type | Pharmaceutical Use |
|---|---|---|
| 0.4–0.6 | Low viscosity | Tablet binder, suspension stabilizer |
| 0.7–0.9 | Medium viscosity | Superdisintegrant, controlled-release matrix |
| 1.0–1.5 | High viscosity | Thickener in topical gels, ophthalmic lubricant |

**Mark–Houwink equation** for CMC in water at 25 °C: $[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$, where $K \approx 0.023$ mL/g and $a \approx 0.97$ for high-DS samples.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]:** Explain **graft copolymerization of cellulose** with N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM). Discuss the thermosensitive behavior of the resulting product and its biomedical applications.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Concept and mechanism (2 marks):**
Grafting attaches synthetic polymer side-chains to the cellulose backbone, combining the **mechanical strength and biocompatibility of cellulose** with the **functional responsiveness** of the grafted polymer. The "grafting-from" approach with ceric ion initiation:

$$
\text{Cell–OH} + Ce^{4+} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O}^{\bullet} + Ce^{3+} + H^+
$$

$$
\text{Cell–O}^{\bullet} + n\,CH_2\text{=CH–CONH–CH(CH}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O–(PNIPAM)}_n
$$

**(ii) Parameters (2 marks):**
- Initiator: ceric ammonium nitrate (CAN), 5–20 mmol/L
- Monomer concentration: 0.1–0.5 M NIPAM
- Temperature: 30–60 °C
- Time: 2–8 h
- Atmosphere: nitrogen (to prevent homopolymerization)

The **grafting percentage** is calculated as:

$$
\%\text{Graft} = \frac{W_{grafted} - W_{cellulose}}{W_{cellulose}} \times 100
$$

**(iii) Thermosensitive behavior and applications (3 marks):**
**PNIPAM** exhibits a **Lower Critical Solution Temperature (LCST) ≈ 32 °C** in water. Below LCST, the polymer is **hydrophilic and hydrated** (extended chains); above LCST, it undergoes **coil-to-globule collapse** expelling water.

In **Cellulose-g-PNIPAM**:
- At body temperature (37 °C) > LCST → PNIPAM chains collapse → **hydrogel expels water and squeezes out drug**.
- Below 32 °C → drug is "stored" in the hydrated matrix.

This makes the system ideal for:
- **On-off switchable drug delivery** (insulin, anti-cancer drugs).
- **Smart wound dressings** that release antimicrobials only when local skin temperature rises (infection indicator).
- **Thermoresponsive cell culture substrates** for non-enzymatic cell sheet harvesting (tissue engineering).
- **Injectable depots** that gel at body temperature after in-situ injection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DS with MS** — Examiners frequently give half-marks if you don't explicitly state that **MS ≥ DS** for hydroxyalkyl ethers.
> 2. **Forgetting the by-product** — In acetylation, always write the **CH₃COOH** produced; in carboxymethylation, mention **NaCl** and **H₂O**.
> 3. **No catalyst in reaction scheme** — Acetylation without H₂SO₄ or pyridine loses a mark. Periodate oxidation without mention of **dark conditions** loses a mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the AGU's three OHs** — Whenever you write the substrate structure, **draw or name all three hydroxyls** (C2, C3, C6) to demonstrate the C6 selectivity.
> 5. **Numerical errors in DS problems** — Students often forget to **subtract** the H mass in the denominator. Use the formula given in Section 2.2 verbatim.
> 6. **Biomedical disconnect** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *biomedical applications*. A perfect chemistry answer without at least one biomedical application mention will lose 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Mechanism without electron flow** — Always draw **curved arrows** (or describe them in words) for the nucleophilic attack step in esterification and etherification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam:**

- ✅ Cellulose = **β-(1→4)-D-glucopyranose** units, $M_{AGU}$ = **162 g/mol**, **3 OH groups/AGU** (C2, C3, C6).
- ✅ **C6 primary OH is most reactive**; C3 is least reactive (intramolecular H-bond).
- ✅ **DS** = 0 to 3; **MS** = 0 to ∞ (used for hydroxyalkyl ethers only).
- ✅ **Acetylation** (Ac₂O / H₂SO₄) → CA / CTA; by-product = **acetic acid**.
- ✅ **Carboxymethylation** (ClCH₂COOH / NaOH) → CMC; by-product = **NaCl + H₂O**; industrial use = superdisintegrant.
- ✅ **Periodate oxidation** (NaIO₄, dark) cleaves C2–C3 → **DAC** with –CHO groups; used for Schiff-base hydrogel crosslinking.
- ✅ **TEMPO oxidation** (NaClO / NaBr / TEMPO, pH 10) selectively oxidizes **C6–OH → C6–COOH** → TOCN nanofibrils.
- ✅ **Grafting** uses free-radical initiators (CAN, KPS, Fenton's) or controlled polymerization (ATRP, RAFT).
- ✅ **Sulfation** with ClSO₃H → cellulose sulfate; **antiviral, anticoagulant** applications.
- ✅ **MC / HEC** show **thermo-reversible gelation** (LCST phenomenon).
- ✅ Cellulose-g-PNIPAM = **thermoresponsive** drug carrier, switches at **32 °C**.
- ✅ Biomedical flagship products: **CA dialysis membrane, CMC hydrogel, DAC bioadhesive, MC ophthalmic lubricant**.
- ✅ **Always quote DS values** for any cellulose derivative in the exam (e.g., "CMC, DS 0.7").
- ✅ **Reaction scheme must include:** substrate, reagent, **catalyst**, **solvent**, **temperature**, and **by-product**.
- ✅ **Biocompatibility is retained** in most modifications because the **glucose backbone is preserved**.
- ✅ **Crystallinity (CrI)** typically **decreases** with increasing DS due to disruption of the H-bond network.
- ✅ **Hofmann degradation** and **enzymatic hydrolysis** are the two main routes to break down cellulose derivatives *in vivo / in environment*.
- ✅ **Bacterial cellulose (BC)** is an emerging *naturally pure* (no lignin/hemicellulose) source with **DP up to 8,000**; popular for wound dressings and artificial blood vessels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerated Cellulose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT304 - BIOMEDICAL & BIOPOLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regenerated Cellulose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Regenerated Cellulose (RC)** is a class of cellulosic material obtained by chemically dissolving natural cellulose (from wood pulp or cotton linters) into a solution or derivative, followed by a controlled regeneration (coagulation/precipitation) step that reconstitutes the polymer into a continuous fiber, film, or membrane form. The resulting material exhibits the same chemical repeat unit ($\beta$-D-glucopyranose, $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5$) as native cellulose but possesses a lower degree of polymerization (DP), modified crystalline morphology, and tailorable mechanical/optical properties.

The molecular repeat unit of regenerated cellulose is identical to that of native cellulose:

$$\text{Cellulose Repeating Unit: } (\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n$$

where $n$ = degree of polymerization (typically **DP = 250 – 700** for regenerated grades, compared to **DP = 1000 – 15000** for native cotton cellulose).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: "The Paper Pulping – Reforming" Metaphor**
> Think of cellulose as **spaghetti strands packed tightly inside a box** (native plant cell wall). Native cellulose is *insoluble* in water and most solvents because those strands are highly ordered and hydrogen-bonded. To make **regenerated cellulose**, we essentially:
> 1. **Dissolve the box** (chemically break the intermolecular H-bonds by converting the polymer into a soluble derivative like cellulose xanthate).
> 2. **Squirt the dissolved spaghetti through tiny holes** (extrude through a spinneret into a coagulation bath).
> 3. **Let the new strands set** (regenerate pure cellulose in a controlled, low-crystallinity form).
> 
> The output is a **brand-new "spaghetti bundle"**, made of the *same* material as the original but **rearranged** with different packing, lower crystallinity, and engineered porosity — perfect for fibers (rayon), films (cellophane), and biomedical membranes (hemodialysis, wound dressing).

## 1.3 Why "Regenerated" and Not "Synthetic"?

- **Regenerated** = the *final polymer is the same as the starting polymer* (cellulose), only its physical form is recreated.
- **Synthetic** = the polymer is built from monomers *into a new chemical identity* (e.g., polyethylene from ethylene).
- Therefore, RC is a **semi-synthetic natural-origin polymer** — natural feedstock, chemically processed, but not chemically altered at the molecular level.

## 1.4 Classification of Regenerated Cellulose (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Type | Process | Primary Form | Key Biopolymer Use |
|------|---------|--------------|--------------------|
| **Viscose Rayon** | Viscose (xanthate) | Fiber, film (Cellophane) | Surgical dressings, absorbent pads |
| **Modal** | Modified viscose | High-tenacity fiber | Hygiene products |
| **Lyocell / Tencel** | NMMO solvent process | Fiber, membrane | Wound dressings, nonwovens |
| **Cuprammonium Rayon (Cupro)** | Cu(OH)$_2$/NH$_3$ | Fine fiber | Hollow-fiber dialysis membranes |
| **Cellulose Acetate** (derivative) | Acetylation | Fiber, film, membrane | Hemodialysis membranes (legacy) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight**
> For biomedical applications, **Cuprammonium rayon** and **Lyocell** are emphasised because they yield ultra-pure, low-cytotoxic membranes suitable for **hemodialysis, hemofiltration, and bioseparation**.

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics for RC

- **Density of regenerated cellulose fiber:** $1.50 - 1.54 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- **Moisture regain (20°C, 65% RH):** **11 – 13 %**
- **Tensile strength (dry, viscose rayon):** $200 - 350 \text{ MPa}$
- **Biodegradability:** Fully biodegradable under aerobic & anaerobic conditions.
- **Biocompatibility:** Non-cytotoxic (ISO 10993 compliant grades available).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of the Viscose Process (Most Important for KTU)

The viscose process converts insoluble cellulose into a soluble derivative (**sodium cellulose xanthate**), which is later regenerated back to pure cellulose in an acid bath. It consists of **six sequential stages**:

### Stage 1 — Steeping (Alkaline Swelling)
- Cellulose sheets (dissolving pulp) are immersed in **18 – 20 % NaOH** at 15 – 30 °C for 1 – 3 hours.
- Reaction: forms **alkali cellulose (sodium cellulosate)**.
- Side effect: partial conversion of cellulose I → cellulose II polymorph (irreversible — the origin of property changes).

$$\text{Cell-OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-O}^{-}\text{Na}^{+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Stage 2 — Shredding / Pressing
- Alkali cellulose is mechanically shredded into *crumbs* to increase surface area and pressed to remove excess NaOH (control of DP).
- **Critical KTU point:** *Pressing ratio determines the final DP of the regenerated fiber.*

### Stage 3 — Aging (Oxidative Depolymerization)
- Crumbs are stored in controlled atmosphere for 24 – 72 h at 25 – 30 °C.
- Atmospheric O$_2$ cleaves glycosidic bonds → reduces DP from ~1000 to **250 – 450** (essential for spinning viscosity).

### Stage 4 — Xanthation (The Key Reaction)
- Crumbs are charged into a **barrel xanthator** and reacted with **CS$_2$ (30 – 50 % on cellulose mass)** at 20 – 30 °C for 1 – 1.5 h.
- Forms **sodium cellulose xanthate (the "viscose" solution)** — a yellow-orange, viscous, spinnable liquid.

$$\text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S-Na}^{+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Stage 5 — Ripening (Xanthate Hydrolysis Back to Spinnable Viscosity)
- The viscose is held at 15 – 20 °C for 24 – 72 h.
- Gradual hydrolysis of xanthate groups controls viscosity and ripening index (a key quality parameter).

### Stage 6 — Spinning (Wet Spinning) and Regeneration
- Viscose is extruded through a spinneret into a **coagulation bath** containing **H$_2$SO$_4$ (8 – 12 %) + Na$_2$SO$_4$ (12 – 24 %) + ZnSO$_4$ (0.5 – 2 %)** at 45 – 55 °C.
- Acid decomposes the xanthate, regenerating pure cellulose.

$$\text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S-Na} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 \uparrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **CS$_2$ Recovery**
> Almost all modern viscose plants **recover 60 – 70 % of CS$_2$** for reuse — a vital environmental compliance metric often asked in KTU questions.

## 2.2 The Lyocell Process (Green, NMMO-Based)

A modern, eco-friendly alternative to the viscose process:

1. Dissolving pulp is dissolved directly in **N-Methylmorpholine N-Oxide (NMMO) / H$_2$O** at 80 – 120 °C.
2. The viscous "dope" is filtered and spun via **dry-jet wet spinning** into an air gap, then into a water coagulation bath.
3. NMMO is recovered (>99 %) and recycled.

**Advantages (KTU high-yield):**
- No CS$_2$ / H$_2$S emissions.
- Higher tenacity (both dry and wet) than viscose.
- Highly pure cellulose → **favored for medical-grade dressings**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | $M_0 = 162 \text{ g/mol}$ for anhydroglucose unit |
| Molecular weight of repeat unit | $M_0(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5) = 6(12) + 10(1) + 5(16)$ | $= 162 \text{ g/mol}$ | Used to compute $M_n$ from DP |
| Cellulose xanthate formation | $\text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-OC(=S)SNa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | stoichiometric | Viscose process chemistry |
| Moisture regain | $MR = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Comfort & absorbency of rayon fibers |
| Crystallinity index (XRD) | $CrI = \dfrac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \times 100$ | % | Distinguishes native vs regenerated |
| Tensile strength (specific) | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$ | $\text{N·m/kg}$ or $\text{N/tex}$ | Comparison of fiber strength |
| Spinning draw ratio | $DR = \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{extrusion}}$ | dimensionless | Controls orientation & tenacity |
| Recovery ratio (Lyocell) | $R = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{charged}} \times 100$ | % | NMMO closed-loop metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule for KTU 2024:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g. $\vert x \vert$) — never the bare `|` symbol inside markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hemodialysis membranes (Cuprophan®)** — regenerated cellulose hollow fibers with ultra-thin walls (~8 – 15 µm) and uniform pore size for selective removal of uremic toxins (MW cutoff ~5000 – 60000 Da).
- **Wound dressings (Aquacel®, Lyocell gauze)** — high absorbency (>20× weight in water), moist wound environment, biocompatible.
- **Surgical casts & tampons** — disposable, biodegradable, lint-free.
- **Tissue engineering scaffolds** — RC porous scaffolds (porosity >85 %) support fibroblast and chondrocyte proliferation.
- **Bioseparation / ultrafiltration** — anisotropic RC membranes with controlled MW cutoffs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight of Cellulose from Degree of Polymerization

A KTU-favorite numerical problem: "A regenerated cellulose sample has DP = 400. Calculate its number-average molecular weight $M_n$."

**Step 1 — Identify the repeat unit molar mass.**

The cellulose repeat unit is the anhydroglucose unit $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (6 \times M_C) + (10 \times M_H) + (5 \times M_O) \\
&= (6 \times 12.01) + (10 \times 1.008) + (5 \times 16.00) \\
&= 72.06 + 10.08 + 80.00 \\
&= 162.14 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DP definition.**

$$M_n = DP \times M_0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_n &= 400 \times 162.14 \\
&= 64856 \text{ g/mol} \\
&\approx 6.49 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each anhydroglucose unit contributes 162.14 g/mol to the polymer chain; multiplying by the number of units (DP) yields the average chain mass.

## 3.2 Derivation: Crystallinity Index (Segal XRD Method)

A standard analytical method to **distinguish regenerated cellulose from native cellulose**.

**Step 1 — Acquire X-ray diffractogram intensities.**

- $I_{002}$ = intensity of the crystalline peak at $2\theta \approx 22.5°$.
- $I_{am}$ = intensity of the amorphous halo at $2\theta \approx 18°$.

**Step 2 — Apply Segal's empirical formula.**

$$CrI (\%) = \left( \dfrac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Worked example.** A regenerated cellulose film gives $I_{002} = 720 \text{ cps}$ and $I_{am} = 360 \text{ cps}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CrI &= \left( \dfrac{720 - 360}{720} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \dfrac{360}{720} \right) \times 100 \\
&= 50.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The closer $I_{am}$ is to $I_{002}$, the more amorphous the sample. Native cotton has $CrI \approx 65 - 90\%$, while regenerated cellulose typically shows $CrI \approx 30 - 50\%$ — a **diagnostic KTU fingerprint** for RC.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric CS$_2$ Requirement in Xanthation

**Problem:** 100 kg of alkali cellulose (containing 30 % cellulose) is to be xanthated. Determine the **minimum mass of CS$_2$** required for 25 % xanthation (i.e., 25 % of the available -OH groups converted to xanthate).

**Step 1 — Mass of cellulose.**

$$m_{\text{cellulose}} = 100 \times 0.30 = 30 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of anhydroglucose units.**

$$
n_{AGU} = \dfrac{30\,000 \text{ g}}{162.14 \text{ g/mol}} = 185.0 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Available -OH groups (each AGU has three -OH, but at C2 & C3 mainly react; commonly take 1 equivalent per AGU for simplicity in KTU problems).**

$$n_{-OH} = 185.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Required -OH groups for 25 % xanthation.**

$$n_{\text{xanthated}} = 0.25 \times 185.0 = 46.25 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5 — Moles of CS$_2$ needed (1:1 with -OH).**

$$n_{CS_2} = 46.25 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of CS$_2$** ($M_{CS_2} = 12.01 + 2 \times 32.06 = 76.13 \text{ g/mol}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CS_2} &= 46.25 \times 76.13 \\
&= 3520 \text{ g} \\
&\approx 3.52 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Xanthation is a 1:1 mole reaction between -OH groups and CS$_2$. The mass of CS$_2$ scales directly with the desired degree of substitution and the cellulose content.

## 3.4 Derivation: Moisture Regain for a Rayon Sample

**Problem:** A sample of viscose rayon weighs **12.50 g** when dry and **14.05 g** at 65 % RH, 20 °C. Calculate moisture regain.

**Step 1 — Apply the moisture regain formula.**

$$MR = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MR &= \dfrac{14.05 - 12.50}{12.50} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1.55}{12.50} \times 100 \\
&= 12.4 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The dry mass is the **denominator** (standard reference); the wet mass minus dry mass is the **mass of water absorbed** — this gives the percentage of water held in the fiber's amorphous regions.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Viscose Process

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the **process flow** of the viscose route, including in-line quality checkpoints — a typical 14-mark KTU question asks students to "explain the process with a flow diagram".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dissolving Pulp - Wood or Cotton Linters] --> B[Stage 1: Steeping in 18-20 percent NaOH - 25 deg C, 1-3 h]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Shredding and Pressing - Control of DP]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Aging - Oxidative Depolymerization - 25-30 deg C, 24-72 h]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Xanthation with CS2 - 20-30 deg C, 1-1.5 h]
    E --> F[Sodium Cellulose Xanthate - Viscose Solution]
    F --> G[Stage 5: Ripening - Viscosity Adjustment - 15-20 deg C, 24-72 h]
    G --> H[Stage 6: Wet Spinning into H2SO4-Na2SO4-ZnSO4 Bath - 45-55 deg C]
    H --> I[Regenerated Cellulose Fiber - Rayon]
    H --> J[Regenerated Cellulose Film - Cellophane]
    I --> K[Quality Check: DP, Tenacity, Denier, CrI]
    J --> K
    E --> L[CS2 Recovery Unit - 60-70 percent recycled]
    H --> M[Acid Bath Recycling Loop]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Viscose vs. Lyocell Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ViscoseRoute[Viscose - Conventional]
        V1[Dissolving Pulp] --> V2[Steeping NaOH 18 percent] --> V3[Shredding and Aging] --> V4[Xanthation with CS2] --> V5[Viscose Dope] --> V6[Acid Bath Coagulation] --> V7[Regenerated Cellulose]
    end
    subgraph LyocellRoute[Lyocell - Green Solvent]
        L1[Dissolving Pulp] --> L2[Direct Dissolution in NMMO-H2O at 80-120 deg C] --> L3[Filtered Lyocell Dope] --> L4[Dry-Jet Wet Spinning into Water Bath] --> L5[Regenerated Cellulose]
    end
    V4 -.CS2 emissions.-> Env1[Environmental Load: HIGH]
    L2 -.Closed loop NMMO recovery.-> Env2[Environmental Load: LOW]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Regenerated Cellulose (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RC[Regenerated Cellulose] --> V[Viscose Rayon - Xanthate Route]
    RC --> CU[Cuprammonium Rayon - Cu-NH3 Route]
    RC --> LY[Lyocell - NMMO Route]
    RC --> AC[Cellulose Acetate - Derivative Route]
    V --> V1[Continuous Filament Yarn]
    V --> V2[Staple Fiber]
    V --> V3[Cellophane Film]
    CU --> CU1[Hollow Fiber for Hemodialysis]
    LY --> LY1[Tencel - Wound Dressing]
    LY --> LY2[Nonwoven Substrate]
    AC --> AC1[Dialysis Membrane - Legacy]
    AC --> AC2[Biodegradable Film]
```

## 4.4 Cellulose Polymorph Transformation (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Native Cellulose - Cellulose I - High CrI - DP 1000-15000] -->|NaOH Steeping| B[Mercerized / Alkali Cellulose]
    B -->|Regeneration in Acid Bath| C[Regenerated Cellulose - Cellulose II - Lower CrI - DP 250-700]
    A -.Direct dissolution in NMMO.-> C
    style A fill:#fff4d6
    style C fill:#d6f4ff
```

> **Engineering note:** The transformation of Cellulose I → Cellulose II is **irreversible** and is the structural origin of regenerated cellulose's altered mechanical, swelling, and dye-uptake properties — a frequently tested KTU point.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Define *Regenerated Cellulose*. Mention any two processes used to produce it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Regenerated Cellulose** is a cellulosic material produced by chemically dissolving natural cellulose (from wood pulp or cotton linters), followed by re-precipitation or coagulation to form fibers, films, or membranes. The final polymer retains the same chemical composition as native cellulose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5$) but has a lower degree of polymerization and modified crystalline morphology. **Two processes:** (1) **Viscose process** (using NaOH + CS$_2$ to form cellulose xanthate), and (2) **Lyocell process** (using N-Methylmorpholine N-oxide as direct solvent). *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for explanation + 1 for two processes.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] Differentiate between *native cellulose* and *regenerated cellulose* in terms of (i) degree of polymerization, (ii) crystallinity, and (iii) solubility.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Native Cellulose | Regenerated Cellulose |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| DP | 1000 – 15000 (cotton) | 250 – 700 |
| Crystallinity | 65 – 90 % | 30 – 50 % |
| Solubility | Insoluble in water and most solvents | Soluble as derivative; coagulated to insoluble form |
| Polymorph | Cellulose I (parallel chains) | Cellulose II (anti-parallel) |
| Source | Plant cell wall | Reformed from solution |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct pair of properties.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit CS$_2$ recovery discussion** in the viscose process → loses 2 marks.
> - **Confusing cellulose acetate (a derivative) with regenerated cellulose** → loses CO-mapping marks.
> - **Skipping reaction equations** in xanthation and regeneration steps → loses 3 marks.
> - **Not mentioning the polymorph change (Cellulose I → II)** → loses 1–2 marks in the "structure–property" sub-part.
> - For biomedical applications, **failing to state biocompatibility standard (ISO 10993)** → loses 1 mark.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Viscose Process Focus
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a process flow diagram, explain the **six stages of the viscose process** for manufacturing regenerated cellulose fiber. Give the key reaction at the xanthation step and the regeneration (coagulation) step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **properties of regenerated cellulose fiber** (viscose rayon) and explain its **biomedical applications** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Stage | Operation | Key Parameter | Marks |
|-------|-----------|----------------|-------|
| 1. Steeping | Pulp immersed in 18–20 % NaOH; forms alkali cellulose | 25 °C, 1–3 h | 1 |
| 2. Shredding + Pressing | Mechanical crushing; excess NaOH pressed out | Controls DP | 1 |
| 3. Aging | Oxidative depolymerization by O$_2$ | 25–30 °C, 24–72 h; DP drops to 250–450 | 1 |
| 4. Xanthation | Reaction with CS$_2$ in NaOH medium | 20–30 °C; forms sodium cellulose xanthate (yellow-orange dope) | 2 |
| 5. Ripening | Controlled hydrolysis of xanthate | 15–20 °C, 24–72 h; tunes viscosity | 1 |
| 6. Spinning & Regeneration | Wet spinning into H$_2$SO$_4$/Na$_2$SO$_4$/ZnSO$_4$ bath | 45–55 °C; regenerates pure cellulose II | 1 |

**Key reactions (2 marks embedded):**

$$
\text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S-Na} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(xanthation)}
$$

$$
\text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S-Na} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 \uparrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \quad \text{(regeneration)}
$$

**Process flow diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Properties of viscose rayon (3 marks):**

1. **Tenacity:** $200 - 350 \text{ MPa}$ (dry); loses ~50 % strength when wet.
2. **Moisture regain:** 11 – 13 % (excellent comfort).
3. **Density:** $1.50 - 1.54 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
4. **Crystallinity:** 30 – 50 % (Cellulose II polymorph).
5. **Biodegradability:** Fully biodegradable.
6. **Dye uptake:** Excellent (high amorphous content).

**Biomedical applications (4 marks):**

1. **Surgical dressings & absorbent pads** — high absorbency, non-linting, biocompatible.
2. **Wound-care nonwovens (Aquacel-style)** — maintains moist environment, supports healing.
3. **Cellophane films in controlled drug release** — tunable permeability.
4. **Tissue engineering scaffolds** — porous RC supports cell adhesion (fibroblasts, chondrocytes).
5. **Hollow-fiber membranes (legacy Cuprophan®)** — hemodialysis (now largely replaced by synthetic polymers but historically important).

> **Concluding statement (for full marks):** Mention that modern biomedical-grade RC complies with **ISO 10993** cytotoxicity and sensitization tests.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Lyocell & Comparative Process Focus
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **Lyocell process** for producing regenerated cellulose. Highlight the role of **NMMO** as a "green" solvent and the mechanism of NMMO recovery. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **viscose, cuprammonium, and Lyocell routes** for regenerated cellulose in terms of (i) solvent system, (ii) environmental impact, (iii) fiber properties, and (iv) biomedical suitability. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Lyocell process description (5 marks):**

1. **Dope preparation:** Dissolving pulp is mixed with **N-Methylmorpholine N-Oxide (NMMO)** and a small amount of water (≤ 20 %) in a heated stirred vessel.
2. **Dissolution:** Temperature raised to **80 – 120 °C**; cellulose dissolves directly (no chemical derivative is formed — this is what makes Lyocell "physical" regeneration).
3. **Filtration & degassing:** Removes undissolved particles and air bubbles (critical for fiber uniformity).
4. **Dry-jet wet spinning:** The hot dope is extruded through a spinneret into a small **air gap** (drawing orients chains), then into a **cold water coagulation bath** where cellulose regenerates instantly.
5. **NMMO recovery (closed loop):** The dilute NMMO/water mixture from the coagulation bath is **evaporated under vacuum** to reconcentrate NMMO; > **99 %** is recycled back to the dissolution stage.

**Mechanism of NMMO action (2 marks):**

- NMMO is a **strongly polar aprotic oxidiser** whose N–O bond donates electron density to break the **inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonds** of cellulose.
- The N–O polar bond disrupts the cellulose I hydrogen-bonding network, enabling direct dissolution without derivatisation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparative table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | Viscose (Xanthate) | Cuprammonium (Cu–NH$_3$) | Lyocell (NMMO) |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| **Solvent / Reagent** | NaOH + CS$_2$ | Cu(OH)$_2$ + NH$_3$ (Schweizer reagent) | NMMO / H$_2$O |
| **Environmental impact** | High (CS$_2$, H$_2$S, Zn effluent) | Moderate (Cu recovery needed) | Very low (closed-loop NMMO) |
| **Fiber fineness** | 1.5 – 5 denier | 0.5 – 1.5 denier (very fine) | 1.0 – 1.7 denier |
| **Tenacity (dry)** | 200 – 350 MPa | 150 – 250 MPa | 350 – 500 MPa |
| **Wet strength retention** | ~50 % | ~60 % | ~85 % |
| **Crystallinity / Polymorph** | Cellulose II, CrI 30 – 50 % | Cellulose II, CrI 35 – 50 % | Cellulose II, CrI 45 – 60 % |
| **Biomedical suitability** | Wound dressings, tampons | Hollow-fiber dialysis | Premium wound dressings, scaffolds |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**

> Lyocell is the most **environmentally sustainable** and offers the **highest wet-strength retention**, making it the preferred regenerated cellulose for **modern biomedical and hygiene applications**. Viscose remains dominant in low-cost commodity fibers; cuprammonium is now niche but historically vital for ultra-fine fibers and dialysis membranes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Regenerated Cellulose)**

- **Definition:** Cellulose chemically dissolved and reconstituted; same chemistry as native cellulose, but lower DP and Cellulose II polymorph.
- **Repeat unit:** $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5$ ($M_0 = 162 \text{ g/mol}$).
- **Three industrial routes:** **Viscose** (NaOH + CS$_2$), **Cuprammonium** (Cu–NH$_3$), **Lyocell** (NMMO).
- **Six stages of viscose process:** Steeping → Shredding → Aging → Xanthation → Ripening → Spinning/Regeneration.
- **Key xanthation reaction:** $\text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-OC(=S)SNa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **Key regeneration reaction:** Acid bath liberates CS$_2$ and reforms $\text{Cell-OH}$.
- **DP comparison:** Native 1000–15000 vs Regenerated 250–700.
- **Crystallinity comparison:** Native 65–90 % (Cellulose I) vs Regenerated 30–60 % (Cellulose II).
- **Biomedical applications:** Hemodialysis membranes, wound dressings, surgical pads, scaffolds, controlled-release films.
- **Standards:** Medical-grade RC complies with **ISO 10993** (biocompatibility).
- **Green trend:** **Lyocell** = preferred route (NMMO closed-loop, no CS$_2$ emissions, high wet strength).
- **Diagnostic fingerprint:** Lower CrI (XRD) and Cellulose II polymorph confirm a sample is *regenerated* (not native).
- **Environmental KPI:** CS$_2$ recovery (60–70 %) in viscose; NMMO recovery (>99 %) in Lyocell.
- **Mnemonic — "SSAAXR":** **S**teep → **S**hred → **A**ge → **X**anthate → **R**ipen → **R**egenerate (six viscose stages).
- **Avoid confusion:** *Cellulose acetate* is a **derivative** (chemically modified), not strictly *regenerated* cellulose; KTU questions may include trick options.
